author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 36a289e4 (Layer 3c §8.3 parity-peer erasure recovery). Strengthens the recovery core from codex 0afcc97b against the testing-plan §8 parity-peer corruption gate. The previous design called parse_sidecar_tape_file, which validates ALL parity-shard CRCs upfront — a single corrupted parity shard caused the whole parse to fail even if the failed stripe could be reconstructed from the remaining m-1 parity shards. The new approach in recovery.rs splits the sidecar read into two phases: (1) read_and_parse_sidecar_index reads ONLY the sidecar header + index blocks first, validating those against the tape_uuid and CRC-64 header checks; (2) read_verified_parity_peer at recovery.rs:285-314 reads each parity shard individually for the failed stripe, computes parity_shard_crc64 (recovery.rs:310), and compares to the sidecar's ParityShardIndexEntry — a read failure OR CRC mismatch returns Ok(None) treating the shard as an erasure (recovery.rs:306-312), exactly mirroring the data-peer policy from codex 0afcc97b. The parity block-position math at recovery.rs:294-300 uses sidecar.header.shard_index_block_count to skip past header/index, then offsets by parity_entry_index — matches §5.5 layout. The new corrupt_clean_reading_parity_peer_is_erasure_not_poison test at recovery.rs:653 proves that a parity peer corrupted post-write (clean read, mismatched CRC) is reported in lost_shards but recovery still succeeds when enough other shards survive. This completes the symmetric treatment of corruption modes in the recovery core: (i) failed primary data block — erasure by definition (the trigger), (ii) corrupted-but-clean-reading data peer — erasure via CRC mismatch (codex 0afcc97b), and (iii) corrupted-but-clean-reading parity peer — erasure via CRC mismatch (THIS codex 36a289e4). The RS solver treats data and parity erasures identically, so the symmetric policy is the natural fit. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: secondary benefit — the two-phase sidecar read is more I/O efficient when only one stripe needs recovery. Previously the recovery code read all S × m parity shards upfront via parse_sidecar_tape_file; now it reads only the m parity shards belonging to the affected stripe. At default geometry (S=512, m=4) recovering a single block now reads 4 parity shards instead of 2048 — a 512× reduction in parity I/O for the common single-block-failure recovery scenario. Observation [no finding]: the parity_shard_crc64 stored in the sidecar's ParityShardIndexEntry is the only integrity gate for parity shards (no separate per-shard MAC since the sidecar's tape_uuid + header CRC already establishes authorship). For a parity peer to be silently corrupted into accepting wrong bytes, an attacker would need to forge the exact CRC-64/XZ that matches the (also forged) bytes — practical only if the CRC value itself is corrupted too, in which case the parity_entries hash mismatch in the sidecar index would catch it. The defense is layered, not single-point. Observation [no finding]: the codec.reconstruct call at recovery.rs:173 doesn't change — it sees an erasure pattern that includes the corrupted parity peer as missing. Whether reconstruction succeeds depends on the RS bound: with m parity peers and one already known erasure (the failed data block), losing one more parity peer leaves m-1 surviving parity + (k-1) good data peers = total of (k+m-1) shards, which is >= k iff m >= 1. So recovery is possible as long as no more than m-1 ADDITIONAL erasures occur in the stripe. The test exercises exactly this boundary: m=4 parity, 1 parity corrupted → 3 surviving parity + k data peers = recoverable. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 225 incl. corrupt_clean_reading_parity_peer_is_erasure_not_poison (was 224), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). Residual risk: object-scoped ParitySource::recover_block_at integration remains pending; Layer 5 SQL/Postgres catalog transaction tests still incomplete; legacy inline-parity migration or feature-gating, live MSL3040 §7.8 power-loss test 11.19, end-to-end DriveHandleRawSink hardware integration, SG_IO timeout selection, and the cross-layer recovery matrix (multiple simultaneous corruption modes, e.g., data + parity peer both corrupt in same stripe) remain incomplete per codex's residual list.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 0aa12f9b (Layer 3c §8.3 object-addressed recovery bridge). Closes review a95570fa/6d543ed3's residual 'object-scoped ParitySource integration above the ordinal recovery core'. The new recover_object_block_from_sidecar at recovery.rs:49-80 is the object-addressed bridge between (tape_file_number, body_lba) callers and the ordinal recovery core. Implementation is correctly minimal — three steps: (1) build TapeFilePosition{tape_file_number, block_within_file: body_lba} (recovery.rs:58-61), (2) resolve to ParityDataOrdinal via scoped_map.map.ordinal_at, converting Ok(None) into a FilemarkMapReconstruct 'tape file {n} is not an object tape file' error (recovery.rs:62-66) — defends against a caller mistakenly requesting recovery of a sidecar or bootstrap block, (3) is_validated(tape_file_number) check (recovery.rs:68-70) — returns OutsideValidatedMapPrefix BEFORE any tape I/O when the object tape file isn't covered by the authenticated bootstrap digest. Only after both checks pass does the bridge delegate to recover_ordinal_from_sidecar (recovery.rs:72-79). The fail-fast pattern (reject before tape I/O) matches the ordinal recovery core's scope.recoverable check at recovery.rs:103 — both layers reject unrecoverable requests before any seek/read, important for both performance and safety. Test count: 225 → 228 (3 new tests). object_block_recovery_maps_body_lba_across_filemarks at recovery.rs:716 verifies body_lba mapping correctly traverses multiple tape files separated by filemarks (the key test: ordinal_at handles filemarks correctly, not just naive linear block addressing). The partial-object watermark boundary test confirms that an early block below the watermark recovers while the tail returns UnrecoverablePendingEpoch before tape I/O — per-block protection (§7.2.1) is enforced at this layer too. The unvalidated-suffix test confirms OutsideValidatedMapPrefix fires before tape I/O. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: this bridge is intentionally MINIMAL — it does only address translation + scope validation, then delegates. The clean separation between 'object-facing address space' (tape_file_number + body_lba — what Layer 5's ObjectParitySource will pass) and 'ordinal address space' (ParityDataOrdinal — what the recovery core operates on) follows the design's separation-of-concerns. Address translation is in the bridge; recovery logic is in the core; neither knows about the other's concerns. Observation [no finding]: codex correctly scoped this as the FORCED-RECOVERY path only. The full ObjectParitySource will need (a) a clean-read passthrough for the common case where the body block reads correctly with no parity needed, (b) error-trigger logic that decides when to escalate to recovery (e.g., on ScsiError::CheckCondition with sense data indicating uncorrected media error), and (c) Layer 5 audit-event wiring so recovery successes/failures are logged. All three are appropriate future steps; the bridge correctly stays focused. A complete recovery cycle is now possible from Layer 5's perspective without writing any new Layer 3c code — only the ObjectParitySource clean-read wrapper needs to be built above this. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 8a37a6bc (Layer 3c §6.2 ObjectParitySource read bridge). Closes review 462f96f0's residual: 'full ObjectParitySource clean-read/error-trigger/audit wiring as the remaining layer above the bridge'. This is the top of the v0.4.4 read-path stack. Implementation at source.rs:71-272 is comprehensive. OpenTrust enum at source.rs:54-62 captures the security boundary cleanly: RequireValidated refuses unvalidated-suffix objects entirely, AllowTarOnlyUnverified permits clean reads but disables recovery — explicitly captures the §7.2.1/§8.1 distinction that data outside the authenticated prefix can be READ but not RECOVERED. ObjectParitySource::open at source.rs:91-131 validates scheme + block_size, looks up the object's filemark map entry, checks is_validated(tape_file_number) against the trust policy, configures the raw source's fixed block size, and positions at body_lba=0. recover_block_at at source.rs:144-168 wraps codex 0aa12f9b's recover_object_block_from_sidecar with cursor advancement on success and audit events on both outcomes. BlockSource::read_block at source.rs:275-316 implements the auto-recovery contract: clean reads pass through directly; filemark/EOD inside an object becomes OperationFailed (objects must not contain internal filemarks); TapeIo errors are passed through is_erasure() — the existing MEDIUM_ERROR/transport classifier — and if the object is validated, recovery is triggered transparently via recover_block_at. The tar_only_unverified gating at source.rs:301 ensures recovery is ATTEMPTED ONLY for validated-prefix objects — for unvalidated suffix objects, an erasure error propagates and the caller decides what to do. This refuses to 'recover' data the bootstrap digest doesn't cover, preserving the catalog-less prefix trust boundary. RecoveryEvent.at_requested at source.rs:243, 269 is a NEW field carrying (tape_file_number, body_lba) — object-scoped audit coordinates that let Layer 5 correlate recovery events to specific objects without a LBA-to-object reverse map. The legacy at_lba_requested field is preserved at source.rs:243, 269, 717 for backwards-compat with the v0.2 inline-parity path. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass: the existing MEDIUM_ERROR/transport classifier is reused, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: the trust policy split is the right factoring. RequireValidated is the production default (refuse to read what we can't verify); AllowTarOnlyUnverified is the operator-escape-hatch for a tape that's lost its catalog (e.g., disaster recovery from a foreign tape) — the operator can still extract raw tar bytes via standard tar, but Layer 3c refuses to claim recovery succeeded against parity that isn't covered by an authoritative digest. Observation [no finding]: the auto-recovery contract in read_block (source.rs:301-313) is the load-bearing operational behavior — when a downstream caller does a normal BlockSource read and the SCSI layer returns MEDIUM_ERROR, the recovery happens transparently with the recovered block landing in the caller's buffer. The caller sees a successful read; the audit hook sees a Recovered event. This is exactly the design §6.2 contract: 'recovery is transparent at the BlockSource boundary'. Observation [no finding]: this completes the v0.4.4 read-path API stack. The full pipeline now exists from Layer 5: discover_authoritative_bootstrap (codex 13d226d1) → acquire_filemark_map (codex 1c695de6) → CatalogFilemarkMapInput with tape_uuid guard (codex 126e8b66) → ObjectParitySource::open (codex 8a37a6bc, THIS) → BlockSource::read_block with auto-recovery or explicit recover_block_at → SidecarRecoveryResult with audit events. A complete reader implementation now exists end-to-end. Observation [no finding]: paired with codex 0d9d7bde's streaming-writer integration and codex d3753004's normative codec, the v0.4.4 cycle's writer-and-reader implementations are now feature-complete at the Layer 3c boundary. Layer 5 integration (catalog transaction, ObjectParitySource lifecycle management, audit pipeline wiring) and live hardware validation are the remaining gates per codex's residual list. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 232 incl. four new object_source tests (was 228), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 5567ecad (Layer 3c §8.3 combined corruption recovery matrix). Directly closes one of the residuals from review dcd19fae ('the cross-layer recovery matrix — multiple simultaneous corruption modes, e.g., data + parity peer both corrupt in same stripe'). The new simultaneous_data_and_parity_peer_corruption_recovers_at_rs_limit test at recovery.rs:881-907 exercises the EXACT RS-limit boundary with a (k=3, m=3, S=1) scheme: failed primary data block at ordinal 2 (LBA 4 marked unreadable at recovery.rs:892), data peer 0 corrupted-but-clean-reading via byte 11 XORed with 0xD3 (recovery.rs:890), parity peer 0 corrupted-but-clean-reading via byte 9 XORed with 0x6C (recovery.rs:887). Three erasures + three survivors (data 1, parity 1, parity 2) is exactly at the m=3 RS boundary — any additional erasure would return Unrecoverable. The test asserts recovered_block byte-for-byte equals the original object_blocks[2] (no silent corruption) AND lost_shards == [Data{0}, Data{2}, Parity{0}] (recovery.rs:899-906) — verifies all three corruption modes are correctly classified as erasures AND that the Reed-Solomon reconstruction succeeds at the limit. This completes the cross-layer recovery matrix at the recovery-core level: (i) single-mode failed data block (codex 0afcc97b), (ii) corrupted-but-clean-reading data peer (codex 0afcc97b), (iii) corrupted-but-clean-reading parity peer (codex 36a289e4), and (iv) combined-mode AT the RS limit (THIS, codex 5567ecad). The implementation correctly preserves the layered defenses — each corrupt peer is detected by sidecar CRC and downgraded to an erasure, never trusted into the RS solver as 'clean' bytes. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: the (k=3, m=3) scheme is the smallest geometry where exactly 3 erasures land at the RS boundary. Smaller schemes (e.g., k=2, m=2) only support 2 total erasures and couldn't model failed-block + corrupt-data-peer + corrupt-parity-peer simultaneously. Codex correctly chose the minimum scheme that exercises the boundary. Observation [no finding]: the test verifies the lost_shards reporting accurately includes all three erased shards in their natural index order (Data 0, Data 2, Parity 0). This audit-quality property matters for Layer 5: when recovery succeeds at the RS limit, the operator log needs to show the recovery exhausted the available redundancy — a warning signal that the tape is degrading and a fresh re-replication should be scheduled. Observation [no finding]: the failure case (one MORE erasure than the limit returns Unrecoverable) is already covered by the codec-level reconstruct_three_lost_at_m_equals_two_fails test at codec.rs:449 from codex d3753004. Together with this run's at-limit test, the boundary is pinned from both sides. Observation [no finding]: filemark damage — the other corruption mode mentioned in review dcd19fae — remains in the residual list. Filemark damage manifests as scan_reconstruct_filemark_map failure (codex e50eddca's partial-object test covers this), not as in-epoch erasure, so it's structurally separate from the recovery-core matrix this run completes. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 233 incl. simultaneous_data_and_parity_peer_corruption_recovers_at_rs_limit (was 232), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). Residual risk: Layer 5 SQL/Postgres catalog transaction tests still incomplete; legacy inline-parity migration or feature-gating, live MSL3040 §7.8 power-loss test 11.19, end-to-end DriveHandleRawSink hardware integration including SCSI-level MEDIUM_ERROR injection, SG_IO timeout selection, and the broader cross-layer matrix including filemark damage interacting with sidecar parse (e.g., a missing filemark adjacent to a corrupted sidecar header) remain incomplete per codex's residual list.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry cdf8415d (Layer 3c §7.5.1 object filemark EOM commit gate). Implements the normative rule at docs/layer3c-design.md:2465-2467 — 'object trailing filemark (finish_object): EOM asserted → treat as object-data EOM: object incomplete, no commit, session dirty'. The new gate at sink.rs:785-790 fires after the object filemark write completes when filemark_outcome.end_of_medium == true: sets self.poisoned = true (sink.rs:786) and returns ParityError::Invariant('object trailing filemark reached end of medium before catalog commit') BEFORE the filemark_map.push_object call at sink.rs:791. The crucial ordering is correct — the object is NOT promoted to the in-memory filemark map, so subsequent finish() cannot construct a final-bootstrap digest that references this object. The new finish_object_rejects_eom_on_object_filemark_before_map_commit test at sink.rs:2087-2163 uses a custom EomFilemarkRawTapeSink that returns WroteFilemark{end_of_medium: true} from write_filemark, then asserts: (a) finish_object errors with the precise 'object trailing filemark reached end of medium' message (sink.rs:2131-2142), (b) subsequent finish() errors with 'poisoned' (sink.rs:2144-2152) — confirms the poisoned flag short-circuits any further writes, (c) raw.events == [WriteBlock, WriteFilemark] (sink.rs:2155-2162) — proves NO sidecar or final bootstrap was written after the EOM. The third assertion is load-bearing: §7.5.1 forbids any post-object-EOM commit, and the test verifies this at the byte level by enumerating raw-sink call history. The codex's IBM SCSI Reference cross-check (in verification block) confirms WRITE FILEMARKS reports EOM via the EOM bit / check-condition rules per §5.2.46, so the Layer 3a WriteFilemarksOutcome.end_of_medium correctly projects the SCSI flag. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: the poisoned flag at sink.rs:786 is the load-bearing safety property — without it, a caller could call finish_object → see the EOM error → naively call finish() expecting recovery, and finish() would happily complete a tape that has an orphan object outside the catalog. The poisoned flag enforces 'session dirty' at the sink level, matching §7.5.1's normative requirement. Observation [no finding]: ParityError::Invariant is currently the catch-all for §7.5.1 EOM violations. A future refactor could introduce a typed variant (e.g., ParityError::EndOfMedium { phase: ObjectFilemark | DataBlock | Sidecar | Bootstrap }) so Layer 5 can branch on EOM phase without parsing the error message. Not actionable today. Observation [no finding]: this gate complements the existing data-block-EOM handling that returns hard FormatError (per §7.5.1 line 2456-2463) — together they enforce the §7.5.1 rule that ANY EOM during object writing aborts the object cleanly and prevents catalog commit. The §7.5.1 table now has explicit code coverage at all rows for the object write phases. Observation [no finding]: paired with codex 0d9d7bde's streaming accumulator integration and codex 8a37a6bc's ObjectParitySource, the writer side of Layer 3c v0.4.4 is now feature-complete at the §7.5.1 EOM-handling table boundary — every row of the EW/EOM phase matrix that involves an object is enforced in code. The remaining sidecar/bootstrap rows of the table are handled by the existing pending_sidecars + finish() path. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 234 incl. finish_object_rejects_eom_on_object_filemark_before_map_commit (was 233), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). Residual risk: Layer 5 SQL/Postgres catalog transaction tests still incomplete; legacy inline-parity migration or feature-gating, live MSL3040 §7.8 power-loss test 11.19 against real hardware, end-to-end DriveHandleRawSink hardware integration including SCSI-level EOM injection, SG_IO timeout selection, and the broader filemark-damage matrix (e.g., a missing filemark adjacent to a corrupted sidecar header, partial recovery from a tape that hit EOM mid-sidecar) remain incomplete per codex's residual list.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry bd52195b (Layer 3c §7.5.1 sidecar filemark EOM commit gate). Closes a bounded sidecar-row gap from review eb0e8359. The new gate at sink.rs:1474-1479 inside emit_one_sidecar fires when the synchronous sidecar trailing filemark returns end_of_medium=true: sets self.poisoned=true (sink.rs:1475) and returns ParityError::Invariant('sidecar trailing filemark reached end of medium before catalog commit') BEFORE the filemark_map.push_parity_sidecar call at sink.rs:1481-1492. The crucial ordering — poison + return BEFORE push — ensures the sidecar is NOT added to the in-memory filemark map, so subsequent finish() cannot construct a final-bootstrap digest that includes this provisional sidecar. This mirrors the §7.5.2 provisional-entry rule (a tape file's structural entry is only confirmed after write_fixed_block + write_filemark all return success) and complements the object-filemark gate at sink.rs:785-790 from codex cdf8415d. The new finish_object_rejects_eom_on_sidecar_filemark_before_map_commit test at sink.rs:2171-2256 uses an EomOnNthFilemarkRawTapeSink that asserts EOM on the 2nd filemark (after the 1st object filemark succeeds), writes a 12-block object (3 full epochs of S*k=4), and exercises the multi-filemark sequence WITHIN finish_object — object filemark → drain pending_sidecars → first sidecar filemark hits EOM. Three load-bearing assertions: (a) finish_object errors with the precise 'sidecar trailing filemark reached end of medium' message (sink.rs:2229-2237), (b) subsequent finish() errors with 'poisoned' (sink.rs:2239-2247), (c) raw.events.last() == WriteFilemark with filemark_count==2 (sink.rs:2250-2255) — proves NO final bootstrap was written after the sidecar filemark EOM. The codex's IBM SCSI Reference cross-check confirms WRITE FILEMARKS reports EOM via §4.11 and §5.2.46 EOM-bit rules. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: this implementation is more CONSERVATIVE than design §7.5.1 row 2469-2474 strictly requires. The design row says 'if the sidecar cannot be completed, its epoch has no parity on this tape; mark that epoch's objects parity-pending in the catalog and rely on another copy (§7.4)' — this allows the OBJECTS whose protection-watermark would have been advanced by the sidecar to be committed as parity_pending. The codex implementation poisons the sink instead, refusing any catalog commit. Reasoning: the codex's stricter behavior makes correctness easier (no half-state in the catalog) at the cost of losing the recordable parity_pending state from the writer-finish path. Layer 5 can still reconcile via scan reconstruction on next mount — the partial sidecar's filemark would be missing, codex 0f45b79b's mid-sidecar test path detects this, and Layer 5 records the affected objects as parity_pending based on scan output. The losiness is recoverable, just via a different code path. Observation [no finding]: with this gate, the §7.5.1 EW/EOM table is now ALMOST completely enforced at sink.rs: object data block (preexisting), object trailing filemark (codex cdf8415d), sidecar data block (sink.rs:1460-1464, preexisting), and sidecar trailing filemark (THIS). The remaining cases are bootstrap-block and final-bootstrap EOM. Observation [no finding]: the test exercises a non-trivial geometry (12 blocks = 3 full epochs in small_scheme(k=2,m=1,S=2)) and the multi-filemark sequence inside finish_object. The 'EOM on 2nd filemark' configuration cleanly distinguishes object-filemark success from sidecar-filemark failure — proves the gate fires at the right phase. Observation [no finding]: codex 0d9d7bde's streaming accumulator + codex 8a37a6bc's ObjectParitySource + codex cdf8415d's object-filemark gate + THIS sidecar-filemark gate together enforce the §7.5.1 table at every object-write phase. The writer side of Layer 3c v0.4.4 is now feature-complete and EOM-safe modulo the bootstrap rows. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 235 incl. finish_object_rejects_eom_on_sidecar_filemark_before_map_commit (was 234), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). author: chatgpt
subject: implementation: Layer 3c section 7.5.1 bootstrap EOM commit gate
summary: Closed the bootstrap-row gap from Claude review 73f13f's §7.5.1 EOM table residual. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 995df1bf (Layer 3c §7.5.1 bootstrap EOM commit gate). Closes the last open row of the §7.5.1 EW/EOM table from review 73f13f13f01's residual. Three new EOM gates added to write_bootstrap_with_finality: (1) short-write gate at sink.rs:896-901 — Invariant 'bootstrap block write completed short', (2) bootstrap-block-EOM gate at sink.rs:902-907 — Invariant 'bootstrap block write reached end of medium before trailing filemark', (3) bootstrap-filemark-EOM gate at sink.rs:916-921 — Invariant 'bootstrap trailing filemark reached end of medium before catalog commit'. All three set self.poisoned=true BEFORE returning Err — subsequent calls (e.g., finish() after a non-final write_bootstrap) see the poisoned flag and refuse to continue. The block-EOM gate fires AFTER the block reaches tape but BEFORE the trailing filemark, so the bootstrap block is durably on tape without its filemark; on next mount, scan reconstruction will treat this as an invalid/partial tape file (consistent with §7.5.2 provisional-entry rule and codex 0f45b79b's mid-sidecar test pattern). The new write_bootstrap_rejects_eom_on_bootstrap_block_before_filemark test at sink.rs:2350-2388 uses BootstrapEomRawTapeSink::eom_on_block(1), calls sink.write_bootstrap() (non-final), and asserts: (a) Invariant with 'bootstrap block write reached end of medium' message, (b) subsequent finish() errors with 'poisoned', (c) raw.events == [WriteBlock(512)] — proves NO filemark and NO further writes followed the block EOM. The new finish_rejects_eom_on_final_bootstrap_filemark test at sink.rs:2390-2421 uses BootstrapEomRawTapeSink::eom_on_filemark(1), calls sink.finish() (which writes the FINAL bootstrap), and asserts: (a) Invariant with 'bootstrap trailing filemark reached end of medium' message, (b) raw.events == [WriteBlock(512), WriteFilemark] — proves the final bootstrap's block + filemark were written but the EOM-on-filemark prevented a clean finish report. The codex also corrected the legacy padding-EOM fixture so it injects EOM only at its scoped phase, not accidentally on the later bootstrap block — good test-infrastructure hygiene. The codex's IBM LTO SCSI Reference cross-check confirms WRITE FILEMARKS reports EOM via EOM-bit rules. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: with this run, ALL SIX rows of the §7.5.1 EW/EOM phase matrix are now enforced at sink.rs: (i) object data block EOM (preexisting FormatError), (ii) object trailing filemark EOM (codex cdf8415d, sink.rs:785-790), (iii) sidecar data block EOM (preexisting sink.rs:1460-1464), (iv) sidecar trailing filemark EOM (codex bd52195b, sink.rs:1474-1479), (v) bootstrap data block EOM (THIS, sink.rs:902-907), (vi) bootstrap trailing filemark EOM (THIS, sink.rs:916-921). The writer side of Layer 3c v0.4.4 is now §7.5.1-complete. Observation [no finding]: the short-write gate at sink.rs:896-901 also belongs to this set — it catches the rare case where the SCSI driver reports a partial WRITE6 success (bytes_written < buf.len()) without setting end_of_medium. The Layer 3a transport contract typically forbids this, but the defensive check at the bootstrap layer is appropriate given the catalog-commit criticality. Observation [no finding]: the bootstrap-block-EOM scenario leaves a durably-on-tape bootstrap block with no trailing filemark. The scan reconstruction path (codex 0f45b79b's mid-sidecar test pattern, applied to bootstrap) would treat this as an invalid tape file on next mount, consistent with the §7.5.2 provisional-entry rule. The poisoned-flag prevents the current session from continuing, and Layer 5 records the session as incomplete per §7.5.1's bootstrap-EOM row guidance ('the last fully-written intermediate bootstrap is the authoritative copy, and Layer 5 records the session as incomplete'). Observation [no finding]: this completes the v0.4.4 writer feature work modulo external integration (Layer 5 catalog transactions, legacy migration, live hardware). The §7.5.1 EW/EOM safety contract is fully enforced at the writer boundary. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 237 incl. write_bootstrap_rejects_eom_on_bootstrap_block_before_filemark + finish_rejects_eom_on_final_bootstrap_filemark (was 235), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 39b88091 (Layer 3c scan filemark-damage matrix). Closes the filemark-damage residual called out in reviews a3ed5f3a and feb37cd9. Two new tests in scan.rs cover the 'damaged header + missing trailing filemark' combinations. (1) damaged_final_bootstrap_header_without_trailing_filemark_is_structural_damage at scan.rs:658-688: fixture_records → pop trailing filemark → XOR the bootstrap header CRC offset to corrupt the magic/header → scan_reconstruct_filemark_map MUST return FilemarkMapReconstruct with 'missing a trailing filemark' AND the specific 'physical LBA {final_bootstrap_lba}'. (2) damaged_sidecar_header_without_trailing_filemark_is_structural_damage at scan.rs:705-729: fixture_records with sidecar damage → splice off the last 3 records (sidecar filemark + final bootstrap block + final bootstrap filemark) → scan_reconstruct_filemark_map returns FilemarkMapReconstruct with 'missing a trailing filemark' at 'physical LBA 5'. Both tests prove scan_reconstruct_filemark_map's measure_current_file (the SPACE FILEMARKS count) detects the missing trailing filemark BEFORE the per-file classification gates on bootstrap/sidecar header validation. This means: a damaged bootstrap or sidecar at end-of-tape without a trailing filemark is rejected as structural damage outright — it never gets a chance to be (mis)classified into object/bootstrap/sidecar by header inspection. The scan reconstruction layer's defense-in-depth ordering is correctly: (i) structural integrity (filemark presence) FIRST, (ii) magic + CRC classification, (iii) digest validation against bootstrap LAST. A failure at any layer blocks propagation upward. The existing damaged_sidecar_header_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches test at scan.rs:691 covers the COMPLEMENTARY case (damaged header WITH valid filemark → reclassified as object candidate → digest mismatch caught downstream); the two new tests at scan.rs:658 and 705 cover the OTHER case (damaged header AND missing filemark co-occurring) — completing the matrix. The exact physical LBA in the error messages is useful operator diagnosis. No SCSI CDB or sense-code behavior changed in this pass, as codex notes. No finding. Observation [no finding]: with this run, the scan-reconstruction corruption matrix is structurally complete: (a) partial object without trailing filemark (codex df513b10), (b) damaged sidecar header with valid filemark → object-candidate-then-digest-mismatch (preexisting at scan.rs:691), (c) damaged sidecar header without trailing filemark → structural damage (THIS), (d) damaged bootstrap header with valid filemark (existing), (e) damaged final-bootstrap header without trailing filemark → structural damage (THIS), (f) mid-sidecar bytes without trailing filemark (codex 0f45b79b). Together with the §7.5.1 writer EOM gates (codex 995df1bf and earlier), the v0.4.4 writer-and-scan cycle now refuses to produce or accept tapes with structurally-damaged tape files. Observation [no finding]: the LBA-in-error-message pattern (line 682 'physical LBA {final_bootstrap_lba}', line 725 'physical LBA 5') is essential diagnostic infrastructure — without it, operator triage on a damaged tape would need to scan the entire tape to find the corrupted file boundary. Codex consistently maintains this pattern across new scan tests. Observation [no finding]: the fixture_records helper is being reused efficiently — both new tests modify a single fixture variant minimally (one byte XOR + record splice) to exercise the exact corruption mode without manual byte-level tape construction. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (clean; remanence-library 214, remanence-parity now 239 incl. damaged_final_bootstrap_header_without_trailing_filemark_is_structural_damage + damaged_sidecar_header_without_trailing_filemark_is_structural_damage (was 237), parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95 / 16; 4 hardware-gated suites ignored as expected). Residual risk: Layer 5 SQL/Postgres catalog transaction tests still incomplete; legacy inline-parity migration or feature-gating, live MSL3040 §7.8 power-loss test 11.19 against real hardware, end-to-end DriveHandleRawSink hardware integration including SCSI-level filemark/EOM injection, SG_IO timeout selection, and ANY remaining cross-layer matrix combinations not yet enumerated (e.g., damaged-header-at-intermediate-bootstrap interacting with prefix-scope validation) remain incomplete per codex's residual list.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry bd663c1a (Layer 3c recovery cache LRU eviction gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test cache_capacity_one_evicts_least_recently_used_stripe at source.rs:1259-1348 closes the missing 'LRU eviction' half of the design §9.3 contract — until now, source.rs:1173 cache_avoids_re_reading_for_two_failures_in_same_stripe proved cache HIT, source.rs:1436 cache_disabled_with_capacity_zero_rewalks_stripe_each_recovery proved DISABLED, and source.rs:1358 invalidate_cache_forces_full_stripe_walk_on_next_recovery proved INVALIDATE, but no test forced a stripe-1 recovery to actually evict a previously-cached stripe-0 at the default LruCache path at source.rs:485-487. The codex addition uses ParitySource::with_cache_capacity(..., 1) to force capacity-1 eviction and is now the only place in the suite where the LruCache's *eviction* edge — not just its hit/miss/disable edges — is exercised. This matches §11.14 of the implementation plan in docs/layer3c-design.md:3771 verbatim ('Recovery cache (§9.3) with LRU eviction'). Observation 2: the eviction proof at source.rs:1340-1347 is genuinely structural, not a heuristic — it asserts the per-LBA read count for the stripe-0 survivor LBAs 6 and 9 is exactly (2, 2), one read during the first stripe-0 recovery walk and one read after the capacity-1 cache served stripe 1 and evicted stripe 0; if the cache had wrongly kept stripe 0 the count would be (1, 1), and if it never cached anything the read-budget assertions at source.rs:1327-1338 ('reads_after_stripe1 - reads_after_stripe0 >= 5' for the stripe-1 walk being a cache miss) would tighten further. The three combined budget+per-LBA assertions pin the LRU behavior in both directions: stripe-1 was a miss and stripe-0 was actually evicted, not silently kept. Observation 3: the test correctly respects the §5.3 row-major mapping (data_blocks_per_stripe=4, stripes_per_neighborhood=3) — stripe 0 holds data LBAs {0, 3, 6, 9} + parity {12, 15}, stripe 1 holds data LBAs {1, 4, 7, 10} + parity {13, 16}; corrupting bad_lbas=[0, 3, 1] creates two erasures in stripe 0 (recoverable, n=k+m=6, k=4, m=2) and one in stripe 1, so each recovery walks the surviving k=4 shards from the inner source — consistent with the §9.2 single-stripe RS reconstruction path. The choice of corrupting both LBA 0 and LBA 3 in stripe 0 (rather than only LBA 0) is what gives the test the *second* stripe-0 recovery request needed to observe the post-eviction re-walk, without which the eviction assertion would not fire. Observation 4: no implementation change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive test coverage; clippy stays clean with the test's Rc<Cell<usize>> + Rc<RefCell<Vec<u64>>> instrumentation pattern at source.rs:1289-1299, which mirrors the pattern already accepted by the suite. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 241 incl. the new LRU eviction test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins capacity-1 LRU behavior in unit terms; multi-capacity eviction (e.g. capacity-4 default eviction across many stripes), concurrent recovery races on the cache (the cache is single-threaded today), and live MSL3040 hardware behavior where a real drive's read latency would expose the cache's amortization benefit are not covered here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry f50e369a (Layer 3c bootstrap discovery raw-error propagation, in crates/remanence-parity/src/bootstrap.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new candidate_bootstrap_probe_can_continue gate at bootstrap.rs:458-463 narrows the candidate-block-size fallback loop at bootstrap.rs:445-454 to continue only on ParityError::NoBootstrapAtPosition or ParityError::BootstrapParse — both of which are the design's intended 'this candidate size + this physical position did not yield a parseable bootstrap, try the next position/size' signals per docs/layer3c-design.md §8.1 (lines 2755-2814, 'Block-size resolution (the bootstrap chicken-and-egg)'). All other ParityError variants — TapeIo of any kind, Invariant, capacity-class errors — now propagate, so a real raw-source failure (cable yank, drive offline, kernel SG_IO disconnect) at probe time can no longer be silently flattened to NoBootstrapFound. Observation 2: the parallel narrowing inside try_read_bootstrap_at at bootstrap.rs:554 via bootstrap_read_error_can_continue at bootstrap.rs:561-574 is the more important tightening — the design's §8.1 pseudocode at docs/layer3c-design.md:2871 literally says `Err(_) => continue, // medium error: keep scanning`, which is sloppy reference code; the implementation correctly interprets the *intent* ('only medium error') by gating on SCSI CheckCondition with fixed-format response code 0x70/0x71 AND sense key 0x03. This matches the §9.1 erasure table at docs/layer3c-design.md:3296-3305 verbatim, where only sense key 0x03 (MEDIUM_ERROR) is a per-block erasure and HARDWARE_ERROR (0x04), NOT_READY (0x02), ILLEGAL_REQUEST (0x05), DATA_PROTECT (0x07), and transport errors are explicitly NOT per-block erasures. Observation 3: the fixed_sense_key extractor at bootstrap.rs:576-582 correctly masks response_code with 0x7F (clears the VALID bit per SPC-4) and accepts only 0x70 (fixed-current) or 0x71 (fixed-deferred), then extracts sense key from byte 2 lower nibble. Descriptor-format sense (response code 0x72/0x73) returns None and therefore propagates as a real error rather than being silently scanned past — a conservative choice consistent with the parallel is_erasure logic at source.rs:808-827 which uses the identical fixed-format gate. The bootstrap and data-recovery sense-format policies are now byte-identical, which removes a class of subtle drift bugs where one path tolerated a sense format the other didn't. Observation 4: the divergence vs is_erasure that bootstrap_read_error_can_continue does NOT treat TapeIoError::Transport as continuable is correct — §9.1 row 'Transport error (timeout, etc.)' is marked 'Maybe — Try once more; if it persists, treat as erasure', a retry-then-erasure decision that only makes sense inside the parity-source read path (where RS reconstruction is available); during bootstrap discovery there is no fallback codec, so a transport failure must surface to the daemon for cabling/drive-state diagnosis rather than being absorbed. Observation 5: the three new regression tests at bootstrap.rs:1550, bootstrap.rs:1565, bootstrap.rs:1580 cover the three distinct failure modes — locate failure propagates, non-medium read failure (OperationFailed) propagates, sense-key-0x03 read failure is scanned past and the next block is parsed as a valid bootstrap — using FailingLocateRawSource / FailingReadRawSource / MediumErrorThenBootstrapRawSource fixtures at bootstrap.rs:1335-1439 and a medium_error() helper at bootstrap.rs:1441-1451 that builds a SPC-4 fixed-current sense (response 0x70, key 0x03, ASC/ASCQ 11/00 'UNRECOVERED READ ERROR' per IBM LTO SCSI Reference GA32-0928-08 Annex B Table B.4). The triplet pins the contract from both sides: skipped errors continue forward, non-skipped errors surface. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 244 incl. the three new bootstrap discovery propagation/skip tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: descriptor-format sense (response code 0x72/0x73) from a real LTO-9 path is not exercised — if HPE firmware ever returns descriptor-format sense for a MEDIUM_ERROR during bootstrap probing, the scan will surface a propagated error rather than skipping the bad block; this would be visible as a NoBootstrapFound→propagated-CheckCondition flip. Live MSL3040 hardware (which is the only way to characterise the actual sense format LTO-9 emits during bootstrap-region reads) and SG_IO timeout selection for the bootstrap-probe LOCATE/READ pair are not covered.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry d96d9e26 (Layer 3c recovery reconstructed-data CRC gate test, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test reconstructed_block_must_match_sidecar_data_crc at recovery.rs:890-916 closes the last missing leg of the three-way CRC defense for sidecar recovery, the one called out verbatim by docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:284-285 ('Every successful reconstruction must verify the reconstructed data block against the sidecar data CRC before returning bytes'). The implementation gate at recovery.rs:226-234 was already there — recover_ordinal_from_sidecar computes data_shard_crc64(&recovered) at recovery.rs:227 and compares it to data_crc_for_ordinal(&sidecar, failed_ordinal, epoch_start) at recovery.rs:226 immediately after codec.reconstruct succeeds at recovery.rs:215, returning ParityError::Unrecoverable on mismatch. Until this entry, no test forced the gate to fire; the test now does by encoding a structurally valid sidecar with a deliberately corrupted ordinal-1 CRC (XOR mask 0xA5A5_A5A5_A5A5_A5A5 at recovery.rs:897) and verifying that recovery returns Unrecoverable rather than emitting bytes. Observation 2: the test correctly orthogonalises the post-reconstruct gate from the two pre-reconstruct gates already covered. corrupt_clean_reading_peer_is_erasure_not_poison at recovery.rs:840 and corrupt_clean_reading_parity_peer_is_erasure_not_poison at recovery.rs:865 exercise the *input-side* CRC checks at recovery.rs:413 read_verified_data_peer and recovery.rs:342 read_verified_parity_peer, where a clean-reading-but-CRC-mismatched peer is reclassified as an erasure (and reconstruction still succeeds because m≥2 is available). The new test exercises the *output-side* CRC check at recovery.rs:226-234: reconstruction succeeds, but the sidecar's recorded data CRC for the failed ordinal is wrong, so the output is rejected. This pins the threat model where a sidecar block is silently corrupt in just its CRC table for one ordinal — an attack/decay surface that the input-side gates cannot detect because the parity shards themselves verify against their own CRCs at recovery.rs:342-411. Observation 3: the test harness change adds sidecar_for_epoch_with_data_crcs at recovery.rs:605-632 and refactors the existing sidecar_for_epoch at recovery.rs:634-643 to delegate to it with the natural-CRC vector — a clean factoring that preserves all existing recovery tests' semantics while exposing a hook to inject mismatched CRC tables. encode_sidecar_tape_file at recovery.rs:631 is called with the explicit data_crcs vector so the bogus CRC is encoded into a valid sidecar block, not bolted on after the fact; this guarantees the test actually reaches recovery.rs:226-234 instead of failing earlier in sidecar-parse validation. Observation 4: the assertion at recovery.rs:907-914 narrows the failure mode to ParityError::Unrecoverable { lost_count: 1, limit: 1 } — matching the scheme(2, 1, 1) configuration (k=2, m=1) — rather than accepting any error. This protects against a future regression where the post-reconstruct gate could silently degrade to a parse-error or invariant violation and still 'pass' the test. The choice of lost_count=1, limit=1 also documents the design property that the CRC-rejected reconstruction is *not* retroactively reclassified as additional erasures: the per-stripe erasure count remains what RS attempted, the CRC mismatch is reported as the same Unrecoverable variant the m+1-erasure case would surface. Observation 5: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 245 incl. the new reconstructed-block CRC-gate test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test injects a deliberately-mismatching CRC into the sidecar; the *natural* threat where decay corrupts a sidecar CRC byte (rather than the test-injected XOR) is exercised only via the input-side peer-corruption tests; live MSL3040 §8 corruption-matrix items 280-282 (prefix-map object outside validated prefix, destroyed sidecar header, catalog-present recovery despite damaged scan path) are not covered here; multi-stripe contiguous-damage matrix items at §8 lines 271-273 are not exercised by this single-stripe scheme(2,1,1) test.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 0c490fa8 (Layer 3c bootstrap descriptor-sense propagation gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/bootstrap.rs). No finding. Observation 1: this closes the descriptor-format residual I called out in Claude review 20ddc985 by pinning the current conservative policy with a regression. The new test discover_propagates_descriptor_format_medium_error_read at bootstrap.rs:1607-1653 builds a descriptor-format CHECK CONDITION via descriptor_medium_error at bootstrap.rs:1453-1462 (response code 0x72, sense key 0x03 at byte 1, ASC 0x11 at byte 2 — the descriptor-current 'UNRECOVERED READ ERROR' shape per SPC-4 §4.5.3 and IBM LTO SCSI Reference GA32-0928-08 Annex B Table B.4) and routes it through discover_bootstrap_with_candidate_block_sizes via a minimal DescriptorMediumErrorRawSource at bootstrap.rs:1609-1637, asserting the error PROPAGATES rather than being silently scanned past. This exercises the path through bootstrap_read_error_can_continue at bootstrap.rs:561-574 where fixed_sense_key at bootstrap.rs:576-582 returns None for response code 0x72 (not in {0x70, 0x71}), forcing the gate to return false and the error to bubble. Observation 2: the assert at bootstrap.rs:1648-1649 pins both bytes — sense[0]==0x72 (response code) and sense[1]==0x03 (sense key in descriptor-format byte 1, not byte 2 as in fixed-format) — so a future regression that broadened fixed_sense_key to accept descriptor format but mis-located the sense-key offset would fail this test rather than silently mis-classify a non-Medium-Error as scannable. The byte-level assertion is the right granularity: it documents the exact wire layout the policy depends on without requiring a full SPC-4 parser. Observation 3: the test sits next to discover_skips_medium_error_read_and_finds_later_bootstrap at bootstrap.rs:1580, forming a paired contract — fixed-format response 0x70 + sense key 0x03 IS scanned past, descriptor-format response 0x72 + sense key 0x03 IS NOT. Together they encode the §9.1 erasure-table-mirrors-format-gate policy as a code-tested invariant rather than a documentation claim. If HPE/IBM/Quantum LTO firmware is later characterised as emitting descriptor-format sense for genuine MEDIUM_ERROR conditions and the policy is relaxed, the failing assertion will force an explicit, reviewable change to fixed_sense_key rather than a silent broadening. Observation 4: the codex entry's residual-risk narrative correctly notes the policy is conservative and that real-hardware evidence is still needed before broadening — consistent with the design-doc's §9.1 erasure semantics (docs/layer3c-design.md:3296-3305) which classifies only fixed-format MEDIUM_ERROR as a per-block erasure. No production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage. Observation 5: the test fixture's read_record at bootstrap.rs:1630-1632 returns the descriptor-format error on every call, so the bootstrap-discovery scan loop at bootstrap.rs:513-557 cannot escape into the success path; the test reaches the propagation gate immediately on the first read. The position() unreachable!() at bootstrap.rs:1634-1636 documents the expectation that propagation happens before any position query, and would panic with a clear message if a future regression added a position-capture step before the gate decision — a subtle but useful structural assertion. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 246 incl. the new descriptor-sense propagation test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: this test pins the propagation policy as a code contract but does not characterise what HPE MSL3040 firmware actually emits for descriptor-format MEDIUM_ERROR during bootstrap-region reads; the only way to verify the conservative policy is correct (rather than unnecessarily strict) is on live hardware with controlled medium-error injection. SG_IO timeout selection and the corresponding sense-format negotiation in CDB CONTROL byte 1 NACA / SPC-4 §6 are still unverified.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 5170ad4b (Layer 3c catalog-present recovery despite damaged scan path, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test catalog_present_recovery_succeeds_when_scan_path_is_damaged at scan.rs:603-645 closes docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:282 ('catalog-present recovery despite damaged scan path'), the last unverified §8 corruption-matrix residual I called out in Claude review 855d0633. The test is a genuine three-stage proof — first that the scan path IS damaged (scan.rs:609-616: reconstructed != expected_map AND ScopedFilemarkMap::validate_against_digest returns FilemarkMapDigestMismatch), then that catalog acquisition bypasses the damaged path (scan.rs:618-629: source.calls.is_empty() asserted AFTER acquire_filemark_map returns), then that recovery succeeds via the catalog-scoped map (scan.rs:631-644: recovered.recovered_block == object_blocks[1], sidecar_tape_file_number == 2). Without the first stage the test would only prove that catalog short-circuit works on a healthy scan; without the second stage it would not prove the design's no-scan invariant for catalog-supplied maps; without the third it would not prove that catalog scoping reaches recovery semantics. Observation 2: the fixture catalog_recovery_fixture_records at scan.rs:518-553 is rigorous about what 'damaged' means — it XORs the final bootstrap's BOOTSTRAP_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET byte with 0xFF at scan.rs:540, which makes the header CRC mismatch and the bootstrap unparseable, causing scan_reconstruct_filemark_map to produce a structurally-plausible-but-digest-mismatched map. The corruption is localised to the header CRC byte, not the payload or other fields, so the *physical structure* (filemarks, block counts) is still walkable; the scan-vs-catalog divergence is the digest validation step, which is exactly the design §8.1 invariant ('the authoritative digest authenticates the scan-reconstructed map'). This is the right injection point: it forces the validate-against-digest gate to fire, not a parser invariant that could mask the test's intent. Observation 3: the sidecar fixture recovery_sidecar_for_object at scan.rs:485-516 builds a real RS-encoded sidecar via ReedSolomonCodec at scan.rs:489 with the §5.4 row-major mapping at scan.rs:491-498 and real data CRCs at scan.rs:510-513, not placeholder bytes — so the recovery succeeds because the parity shards actually reconstruct ordinal 1 from ordinal 0's data block, not because the recover() path was bypassed. The recover_object_block_from_sidecar call at scan.rs:631-640 with tape_file_number=1, body_lba=1 traverses the full recovery path: ordinal resolution at recovery.rs:62, validated-prefix check at recovery.rs:68, then recover_ordinal_from_sidecar at recovery.rs:72-79 which reads parity shards from the sidecar (at LBAs after the object's filemark) and verifies the reconstructed ordinal via the data CRC at recovery.rs:226-234. Observation 4: the assertion at scan.rs:629 'source.calls.is_empty(), "catalog acquisition must not scan"' is the structural assertion that matters — acquire_filemark_map at scan.rs:49-56 routes through the early-return at scan.rs:53-56 (ScopedFilemarkMap::from_catalog) when the CatalogFilemarkMapInput is Some, and RecordingRawSource at scan.rs:618 was freshly constructed so any non-empty calls list would indicate the catalog path leaked into the scan path. The test would catch a future regression that moved any source-touching code above the catalog short-circuit (e.g. a 'validate bootstrap before catalog' refactor that broke the §10.1 separation of catalog-driven from bootstrap-driven map acquisition). Observation 5: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean with the test's fully-real RS+CRC+bootstrap-block fixture pattern. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 247 incl. the new catalog-present-recovery-despite-damaged-scan-path test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the fixture damages exactly one byte of the final bootstrap header; multi-byte / payload / cross-bootstrap-copy damage modes are not exercised; the test uses a single epoch with two object blocks, so multi-epoch catalog-present recovery (where the catalog map spans multiple sidecars) is not covered; the §8 line 281 residual 'destroyed sidecar header → catalog-less recovery unavailable' remains unverified; live MSL3040 hardware where SCSI BLOCK PROTECTION or LBP-PI errors could surface as additional damage modes is not exercised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 2cd4d7a3 (Layer 3c sidecar recovery contiguous-damage threshold gates, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the three new tests at recovery.rs:967, recovery.rs:1009, recovery.rs:1049 close the contiguous-damage residual I called out in Claude review 855d0633 against docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:271-273 ('contiguous damage lengths: 1, 50, S-1, S, 2S, mS, mS+1'). They cover the most demanding three points on the curve: the upper bound mS where every stripe sits exactly at the m-erasure limit, the explicit testing-plan length-50 value, and the just-past-threshold mS+1 case that overloads one stripe. Length-1 was already covered by the single-block tests at recovery.rs:840 and recovery.rs:865; this batch picks the threshold-edge cases the plan calls out by name. Observation 2: the threshold derivation at recovery.rs:974 and recovery.rs:1056 uses scheme.contiguous_damage_threshold() at model.rs:93-95 — S × m — which matches the active design at docs/layer3c-design.md:731-734 ('physically-contiguous data damage up to S × m blocks lands at most m blocks per stripe ... all stripes recover'), docs/layer3c-design.md:1090 ('contiguous damage up to m × S = 4 × 512 = 2048 blocks'), and docs/layer3c-design.md:1077. The test asserts at recovery.rs:975 that scheme(4,3,5)'s threshold equals 15 and at recovery.rs:1017 that scheme(2,1,50)'s threshold equals 50, pinning the model.rs computation as a code-tested contract — a future regression that swapped m and S in the formula would fail both assertions. Observation 3: the threshold-exact test at recovery.rs:967-1006 is genuinely structural. It damages physical LBAs derived through object_damage_lbas at recovery.rs:723-741 from the ScopedFilemarkMap (not hard-coded raw-tape offsets — a discipline noted in the codex self-review), so the test exercises the full ordinal→TapeFilePosition→PhysicalPositionHint chain via map.physical_position. The for-loop at recovery.rs:978-1005 then recovers every damaged ordinal one-by-one and asserts both (a) recovered bytes match the original at recovery.rs:996-999 and (b) per-stripe erasure count equals exactly m at recovery.rs:1000-1004 — the latter pins the §5.4 row-major property that S consecutive physical ordinals span distinct stripes, so S×m contiguous damage produces a uniform m-per-stripe distribution rather than a clumped one. Observation 4: the failure case at recovery.rs:1049-1074 correctly assertions Unrecoverable { lost_count: 4, limit: m=3 } — the +1 ordinal (the 16th in a row, ordinal 15 with scheme S=5) lands in stripe 0 row 3 (15 % 5 = 0, 15 / 5 = 3), giving stripe 0 a total of 4 erasures (rows 0,1,2,3) which exceeds m=3 and is correctly classified as the §9.2 Unrecoverable variant. The test recovers ordinal 0 (also in the overloaded stripe), which is the right target — recovering an ordinal from the non-overloaded stripes 1..4 would have succeeded and not exercised the gate. The choice of recovering ordinal 0 documents this single-overloaded-stripe-rejects-recovery-of-itself property. Observation 5: the length-50 test at recovery.rs:1009-1046 uses scheme(k=2, m=1, S=50) — chosen so that S × m = 50 and the loss pattern is exactly one erasure per stripe (asserted at recovery.rs:1040-1044 with lost_shards == vec![StripePosition::Data { index: 0 }]). This makes the length-50 case maximally informative: m=1 is the tightest recoverable parity, so any per-stripe assumption is exercised at its most fragile setting. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 250 incl. the three new contiguous-damage threshold tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the §8 line 271 list also names lengths 1, S-1, S, 2S as threshold-bracketing cases — 1 is covered by recovery.rs:840/865, S and 2S are implied by the mS scheme-parameter scan but not explicitly tested at S-1, S, 2S labels; non-contiguous (interleaved) damage patterns are not exercised by this batch; damage spanning the object filemark or the object-sidecar boundary at testing-plan §8 lines 272-273 remains unverified by this set; live MSL3040 burst-error patterns (servo-track damage, edge damage) are not characterised against the S × m model on real hardware.

author: chatgpt
subject: implementation: Layer 3c catalog-less destroyed-sidecar gate
summary: Closed the remaining docs/remanence-testing-plan.md section 8 destroyed-sidecar-header catalog-less recovery gate called out as residual risk in Claude review 878dc5ba. Added scan::tests::catalog_less_recovery_unavailable_when_sidecar_header_is_destroyed, which corrupts the sidecar header CRC in an otherwise valid scanned tape, calls acquire_filemark_map with no catalog input, and asserts FilemarkMapDigestMismatch instead of producing a ScopedFilemarkMap. This pins the public acquisition boundary: catalog-less recovery cannot proceed when sidecar classification fails validation and the reconstructed map no longer matches the authoritative bootstrap digest. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 1e54508a (Layer 3c catalog-less destroyed-sidecar gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test catalog_less_recovery_unavailable_when_sidecar_header_is_destroyed at scan.rs:828-838 closes docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:281 ('destroyed sidecar header → catalog-less recovery unavailable'), the residual I called out in Claude review 878dc5ba. The test uses fixture_records(false, true) at scan.rs:830 — healthy BOT bootstrap, healthy final bootstrap, corrupted sidecar header (XOR 0xFF on SIDECAR_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET at scan.rs:459) — and then calls acquire_filemark_map with None catalog input at scan.rs:834, asserting the result is ParityError::FilemarkMapDigestMismatch at scan.rs:837. This is the precise catalog-less twin of the catalog-present test at scan.rs:603-645 from codex 5170ad4b: same fixture shape pattern (corrupt one structural component, leave others healthy), opposite catalog hypothesis, opposite expected outcome. The pair now encodes the design §10.1 catalog-vs-catalog-less recovery boundary as a code contract — catalog input wins when scan fails, no-catalog loses when scan fails. Observation 2: the test correctly targets the *public acquisition* boundary rather than only the lower-level scan/validate helpers. acquire_filemark_map at scan.rs:49 is the API Layer 5 uses; if it could be coaxed into returning a ScopedFilemarkMap with a digest-mismatched reconstructed map, that would be a §8.1 invariant violation. By exercising acquire_filemark_map (not just scan_reconstruct_filemark_map + validate_against_digest separately) the test pins the boundary that Layer 5 actually depends on. The codex self-review pass 1 explicitly notes this discipline — it would have been easier to test the helpers directly, but the resulting test would not prove the boundary contract. Observation 3: the corruption is localized to a single byte (SIDECAR_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET) per scan.rs:459, which means the *physical structure* of the tape (filemarks, block counts, bootstrap copies) is otherwise intact — so the test reaches the validation gate rather than failing earlier in structural-traversal code paths. This is the right injection point: it forces sidecar classification to fail (because the header CRC doesn't validate), which forces the scan-reconstructed map to differ in structure (the sidecar tape file is misclassified or treated as a non-sidecar tape file), which forces the bootstrap-digest validation to mismatch, which forces FilemarkMapDigestMismatch. Each gate is reachable; if any earlier gate had caught the corruption first, the test's assertion would not hold and the codex self-review pass 2 would have caught it. Observation 4: the test sits next to damaged_sidecar_header_without_trailing_filemark_is_structural_damage at scan.rs:840 (pre-existing), which exercises a more severe damage mode where the sidecar header AND the trailing filemark are both gone. That test fails earlier in scan with FilemarkMapReconstruct ('missing a trailing filemark') at scan.rs:858-863. The pair now spans the §8 damage spectrum from 'CRC-bad header but structure intact' → FilemarkMapDigestMismatch, to 'header gone AND filemark gone' → FilemarkMapReconstruct structural error. Different error variants are correctly surfaced for different damage severities. Observation 5: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 251 incl. the new catalog-less destroyed-sidecar gate, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: only one byte of the sidecar header is corrupted; multi-byte / payload-region / full-block-zeroed sidecar damage modes are not separately exercised; the §8 line 281 case is covered for catalog-less recovery in the first-sidecar position, but corruption of an intermediate sidecar in a multi-epoch chain is not exercised; live MSL3040 hardware where SCSI BLOCK PROTECTION / LBP-PI errors could surface as additional damage-classification modes is not exercised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 76d7deae (Layer 3c recovery damage spanning filemarks and sidecar header, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at recovery.rs:765 and recovery.rs:826 close docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:272-273 ('damage spanning an object filemark' and 'damage spanning object + sidecar cluster'), the §8 residuals I called out in Claude review bec2d610. Both tests use the same physical pattern — three contiguous damaged LBAs that include a filemark in the middle — but target different recovery semantics: the first proves recovery succeeds across an object→object filemark span, the second proves recovery fails CLEANLY with SidecarParse when the damage reaches into the sidecar header. The contrast is the design property worth testing: a filemark in the middle of the damage span is not an extra erasure (it's not a data block), so the per-stripe erasure count stays at 1 in test 1 (lost_shards = [Data{index: 0}] for ordinal 1, [Data{index: 1}] for ordinal 2 — asserted at recovery.rs:794-795). Observation 2: the cross-filemark recovery test at recovery.rs:765-823 is genuinely structural in two distinct ways. First, it uses scoped_two_object_map at recovery.rs:769 to produce a real two-object tape, so the cross-filemark peer-read goes through the §5.4 row-major mapping: ordinal 1 lives in stripe 1, its data peer ordinal 3 lives at LBA 6 — past the damaged filemark at LBA 4. The recovery path must SPACE past or seek across the filemark to read LBA 6, exercising the §9.2 peer-read-across-filemarks behavior. Second, the test iterates over BOTH affected ordinals (1 and 2) at recovery.rs:793-822 with separate fresh raw tapes (line 797 reconstructs raw inside the loop), so each recovery is independent and the recovery path is tested from both stripes' perspectives. The lost_shards assertion at recovery.rs:821 pins the per-stripe erasure count to exactly 1, matching the m=1 limit without exceeding it. Observation 3: the failure case at recovery.rs:826-867 is precisely the right semantic test for testing-plan §8 line 273. Damaging LBA 7 (the sidecar's first block, which holds the SidecarHeader at body_lba=0) and recovering ordinal 3 forces read_sidecar_header at recovery.rs:328-339 to call read_required_block which returns a TapeIo error, which gets wrapped into ParityError::SidecarParse with the message 'could not read sidecar tape_file {n} block {block_within_file}' — exactly what the test's match arm asserts at recovery.rs:861-864 ('could not read sidecar' and 'block 0'). This pins the error variant: a destroyed sidecar header does NOT surface as an Invariant violation, NOT a generic Unrecoverable, but the specific SidecarParse error variant that Layer 5 can recognise as 'this epoch's parity is gone, fall back to another copy'. Observation 4: the damaged-LBAs assertions at recovery.rs:791 and recovery.rs:852 ('assert_eq!(damaged_lbas, vec![3, 4, 5])' and '[5, 6, 7]') document the physical-LBA derivation through ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position — they would catch a future fixture or scoped-map regression that moved the bootstrap, object, or sidecar to different physical positions. This is a useful defensive assertion since the rest of the test relies on those exact LBAs being what's marked unreadable. Observation 5: the choice of scheme(2, 1, 2) is tight — k=2 minimal-recovery parity, m=1 (the most fragile setting where even one extra erasure in a stripe is unrecoverable), S=2 so each stripe has exactly 2 data positions across 2 rows. This makes the test sensitive: any incorrect handling of a filemark-as-erasure would push one stripe to 2 erasures and trip the m=1 limit, causing test 1 to fail with Unrecoverable instead of succeeding. The test surviving with m=1 is strong evidence that filemarks in the middle of damaged runs are correctly ignored as non-erasures. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 253 incl. the two new damage-span tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: damage spanning a parity-shard cluster (multi-block sidecar damage past the header) is not exercised — the destroyed-header case fails fast; longer sidecar-body damage that survives the header might surface as a different failure mode (insufficient surviving parity shards) and is not directly characterised; bootstrap-spanning damage (object → object filemark → final bootstrap) is not tested as a separate matrix point; live MSL3040 burst-error patterns crossing tape-file boundaries are not characterised on real hardware.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 21e54153 (Layer 3c catalog-less intermediate-sidecar damage gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test catalog_less_recovery_unavailable_when_intermediate_sidecar_header_is_destroyed at scan.rs:920-962 closes the multi-epoch chain residual I called out in Claude review 488d5a38. It extends the first-sidecar case from scan.rs:828 to the harder intermediate-sidecar case using a fresh multi_epoch_fixture_records helper at scan.rs:480-555 that builds a real three-epoch tape (bootstrap@0 + 3×{object+sidecar} + bootstrap@7) and corrupts only epoch 1's sidecar header CRC at scan.rs:546-548. The damage is now genuinely in the *middle* of a multi-epoch chain rather than at the first sidecar, which exercises the design's locality claim — that a single sidecar's CRC corruption affects only that sidecar's classification and does not cascade to neighbors. Observation 2: the scan-reconstruction structural assertion at scan.rs:928-944 is the load-bearing observation. It proves the middle sidecar is downgraded to TapeFileKind::Object while the neighboring sidecars at epoch 0 and epoch 2 still classify as TapeFileKind::ParitySidecar. The full kinds vector [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object, Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] documents exactly how the misclassification shifts the structural map: the damaged sidecar's blocks are now counted as object-data blocks, which is why total_data_ordinals at scan.rs:946-949 grows by exactly the damaged sidecar's block_count. This locality property is precisely what makes catalog-supplied maps still recoverable — if a single corruption cascaded to invalidate neighboring sidecars, the catalog path would also lose recovery for those epochs. Observation 3: the assertion at scan.rs:950-954 (max_sidecar_end_exclusive equal to expected_map's value) is a clever 'failure-mode disambiguator' — it documents that the later epoch's sidecar STILL validates and protects its ordinal range; the digest mismatch is purely from the structural-shift caused by epoch 1's misclassification, not from cascade damage to epoch 2. This rules out a class of false-positive scan behaviors where a downstream sidecar might silently fail to classify because the upstream sidecar's parse failure left the scanner in a bad state. Observation 4: the test asserts the failure mode is specifically ParityError::FilemarkMapDigestMismatch at scan.rs:961, via the public acquire_filemark_map API at scan.rs:957. Per Claude review 488d5a38 observation 2, this is the right contract surface to test — Layer 5 depends on acquire_filemark_map being the boundary, not the lower-level scan/validate helpers. A future regression that moved digest checking elsewhere, or that surfaced a different variant for intermediate-sidecar damage (e.g. FilemarkMapReconstruct), would fail this test. Observation 5: the fixture helper multi_epoch_fixture_records at scan.rs:480 uses real ReedSolomonCodec at scan.rs:484, real data CRCs at scan.rs:513-516, and real bootstrap_block encoding at scan.rs:537-538 — no placeholder bytes. The fixture is parametric over corrupt_sidecar_epoch: Option<u64> at scan.rs:481, so the same helper could later test first-sidecar (Some(0)) and last-sidecar (Some(2)) damage cases without code duplication. The current test pins the middle case at Some(1); the helper is naturally extensible for future multi-epoch edge cases. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 254 incl. the new intermediate-sidecar destroyed-header test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test corrupts only one byte; multi-byte / multi-block intermediate-sidecar damage modes are not exercised; corruption of two non-adjacent sidecars (e.g. epochs 0 and 2 healthy, epoch 1 corrupted ALONGSIDE epoch 0) is not tested; live MSL3040 hardware where intermediate sidecar corruption could co-occur with adjacent object damage is not characterised; the catalog-PRESENT analog of this intermediate-sidecar damage case (proving the catalog still recovers from multi-epoch chains when one intermediate sidecar is corrupt) is not yet covered.
