author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 7f20adb8 (Layer 3c sidecar body damage recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_body_damage_past_header_is_counted_as_missing_parity at recovery.rs:870-925 closes the sidecar-body-past-header residual I called out in Claude review df8b0883. It is the natural sibling of recovery.rs:826 damage_spanning_object_into_sidecar_header_makes_epoch_unrecoverable from codex 76d7deae: that test damages the sidecar header and surfaces ParityError::SidecarParse; this test damages the sidecar parity-shard body while leaving the header AND index readable, and surfaces ParityError::Unrecoverable. The pair now spans the full sidecar-damage matrix: header damaged → SidecarParse (epoch is opaque), header intact + parity body damaged → Unrecoverable (epoch parses but parity is gone). Observation 2: the damage-region computation at recovery.rs:889-901 is the load-bearing structural choice. It uses sidecar.header.shard_index_block_count at recovery.rs:875 (decoded from the parsed header) to derive first_parity_block, then maps each parity-shard offset through ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position to get the physical LBA. This means damaged_lbas covers EXACTLY the parity-shard region — not the header (block 0) and not the index (blocks 1..shard_index_block_count). The assertion at recovery.rs:902-905 ('damaged_lbas[0] as u64 > header_lba') explicitly documents that the header is not damaged, which is the property that distinguishes this from the header-damage case. Observation 3: the contiguity assertion at recovery.rs:906-909 ('damaged_lbas.windows(2).all(|pair| pair[1] == pair[0] + 1)') pins the test as a *contiguous* damage scenario — physically adjacent parity blocks all damaged at once, which is the realistic scenario for tape-edge damage hitting the post-index region of a sidecar tape file. A future regression that interleaved parity-shard storage with other blocks (e.g. an embedded recovery hint between parity shards) would fail this assertion and force an explicit policy decision. Observation 4: the failure-variant assertion at recovery.rs:918-924 is precise: ParityError::Unrecoverable { lost_count: m + 1, limit: m }. The +1 accounts for the failed data shard at ordinal 2 itself; the m accounts for the m=3 missing parity shards. With scheme(3, 3, 1) the survivors are exactly 2 valid data peers (ordinals 0 and 1) — below k=3 needed for reconstruction. The choice of scheme(3, 3, 1) is sharp: only one stripe per neighborhood (S=1), so all m=3 parity shards live in this single stripe and damaging them all overloads it cleanly without ambiguity about which stripe is affected. Observation 5: the test correctly verifies the read path's structural separation of header/index vs body. recover_ordinal_from_sidecar first calls read_sidecar_header at recovery.rs:328-339 (block 0), then reads index blocks via the loop at recovery.rs:308 (blocks 1..shard_index_block_count), then attempts to read parity shards via read_verified_parity_peer at recovery.rs:342+. The header+index succeed (intact LBAs), the parity reads fail (damaged LBAs) and are correctly counted as erasures rather than as a parse-failure cascade. This is what makes the Unrecoverable variant correct: the sidecar parsed, it's just that every parity shard is missing. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 255 incl. the new sidecar body damage test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: this test damages ALL parity shards at once; a partial parity-shard damage scenario (e.g. only m of n parity shards damaged when m < n) is not separately exercised, though it is covered implicitly by the existing recover_ordinal_at_rs_limit tests with parity corruption; the sidecar-index region (blocks 1..shard_index_block_count) is not specifically targeted for damage — that would test a different failure mode (index parse failure rather than parity-shard absence); live MSL3040 hardware where SCSI tape-edge damage patterns might hit the sidecar's body region but not its header is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 163bb172 (Layer 3c §9 huge-object spool reserve gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/capacity.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test huge_object_sidecar_cluster_spool_reserve_scales_without_overflow at capacity.rs:333-399 closes a docs/remanence-testing-plan.md §9 'Capacity, EOM, and spool gates' coverage point that the existing small-scale tests at capacity.rs:260 (tape_shortfall) and capacity.rs:292 (spool_shortfall) couldn't reach: arithmetic correctness at sidecar-cluster scale of 1,000,000 epochs. The input parameters at capacity.rs:334-343 are chosen so that projected_object_blocks (12M) divides exactly by data_shards_per_epoch (12) into 1M epochs, sidecar_tape_file_blocks = 2 (index) + 6 (parity) + 1 (filemark) = 9, and expected_spool_bytes = 1M × 9 × 524288 ≈ 4.7e12 bytes — well within u64 range but large enough that any checked_mul/checked_add missed in the capacity arithmetic at capacity.rs:139-175 would surface as a different error mode than the asserted ParitySpoolCapacity shortfall. Observation 2: the test pins three orthogonal properties in one positive case at capacity.rs:363-372. (a) epochs_completed_by_object == 1_000_000 verifies the §7.5 epoch-counting math at sidecar-cluster scale; (b) !final_partial_sidecar_needed verifies the §7.5.2 exact-epoch-boundary case (no partial sidecar reserved when the object lands on an epoch boundary) — a critical edge case where an off-by-one in the modulo computation would reserve an unnecessary 9-block partial sidecar; (c) reserve_after_object_blocks == 1 + 9_000_000 + 2 + 32 verifies the §7.5 reserve formula composition (object filemark + sidecar cluster + bootstrap tape file + safety margin). The arithmetic equation at capacity.rs:371 is explicit, so a future regression that shifted the formula composition would fail with a clear arithmetic discrepancy. Observation 3: the negative case at capacity.rs:374-398 is the load-bearing cause-discrimination assertion for the v0.4.4 CapacityReserveCause split. By setting remaining_tape_blocks = u64::MAX at capacity.rs:358 in the base input, the test makes it structurally impossible for a TapeCapacity cause to fire — only ParitySpoolCapacity can fail because the spool is short by exactly one byte. The assertion at capacity.rs:391-395 then pins the failure shape exactly: cause == ParitySpoolCapacity, remaining_blocks == None and reserve_blocks == None (those are TapeCapacity-only fields), remaining_spool_bytes == Some(expected - 1), required_spool_bytes == Some(expected). This proves the codex 89050c1d v0.4.4 CapacityReserveCause/Remedy split correctly omits the tape-capacity fields when the cause is spool — a critical contract for Layer 5's operator-facing diagnostics. Observation 4: the test exercises the checked_mul / checked_add / checked_add3 helpers at capacity.rs:139-175 at the 1M-scale boundary. The expected_spool_bytes computation 1,000,000 × 9 × 524,288 = 4,718,592,000,000 is ~4.7 TB — at this scale, any intermediate u32 cast or unchecked multiplication would either overflow (surfacing as Invariant) or produce a wrong number (surfacing as a different ParitySpoolCapacity shortfall amount). The test asserts the EXACT expected number at capacity.rs:368 and capacity.rs:394-395, so wrong arithmetic at any intermediate step would fail the test with a clear discrepancy rather than silently masking the bug. Observation 5: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. The test sits naturally beside exact_epoch_boundary_does_not_reserve_final_partial_sidecar at capacity.rs:401-415 (pre-existing), which covers the same epoch-boundary property at small scale. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 9863e09d (Layer 3c sidecar index damage recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_index_damage_after_header_is_sidecar_parse_failure at recovery.rs:930-984 closes the sidecar-index residual I called out in Claude review 20f9e32c. The three sidecar-damage tests now form a complete tri-band matrix across the §5.6 sidecar layout (header, index, parity body): recovery.rs:826 damage_spanning_object_into_sidecar_header → ParityError::SidecarParse with 'block 0', recovery.rs:930 (new) sidecar index damage → ParityError::SidecarParse with 'block 1', recovery.rs:870 sidecar_body_damage_past_header → ParityError::Unrecoverable with lost_count m+1. The asymmetry is the right design choice: damage to header OR index makes the sidecar opaque (we can't even decode which parity shards exist), but damage to the parity body lets the sidecar parse and surfaces as countable missing parity erasures. Observation 2: the test correctly positions the damage between header_lba+1 and first_parity_lba via the two assertions at recovery.rs:964-968 — index_lba == header_lba + 1 (immediately after header) AND index_lba < first_parity_lba (before parity body). These two assertions document the structural invariant the §5.6 sidecar layout depends on (header block 0, index blocks 1..shard_index_block_count, parity blocks shard_index_block_count..n) — a future regression that reordered the sidecar's internal layout would fail these assertions cleanly. Observation 3: the precondition assertion at recovery.rs:935-938 'shard_index_block_count > 1' is the right defensive gate. If a future scheme parameter change reduced the sidecar to a single header+index block (with index packed into the header), the test would refuse to run rather than silently passing a degenerate case that doesn't actually exercise the index-read path. The test then targets block_within_file=1 specifically — the first POST-HEADER block — at recovery.rs:951-955, so the index-read loop at recovery.rs:308 ('for block_within_file in 1..u64::from(header.shard_index_block_count)') is genuinely entered. Observation 4: the error-variant assertion at recovery.rs:977-983 pins the failure to ParityError::SidecarParse with two substring checks: 'could not read sidecar' (from read_sidecar_header's wrapper at recovery.rs:328-339, applied via read_required_block to subsequent blocks too) and 'block 1' (from the block_within_file parameter). The 'block 1' message is the load-bearing assertion — it distinguishes index damage from header damage (which would surface 'block 0') AND from parity-body damage (which would surface Unrecoverable, not SidecarParse). Three discrete error paths, three discrete message signatures, each documented by an explicit assertion. Observation 5: scheme(3, 3, 1) at recovery.rs:931 is chosen so that the sidecar's parity_block_count = 3 and shard_index_block_count = 2 (header block 0 + index block 1), with parity shards at blocks 2..5. The damaged LBA index_lba is at body_lba=1 which falls into the index region; first_parity_lba is at body_lba=2 which begins the parity body. The geometric assertion at recovery.rs:965-968 (index_lba < first_parity_lba) confirms the test reaches the index-read failure mode without accidentally landing in the parity body. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. The three sibling tests now collectively prove the sidecar's §5.6 layout is structurally walked left-to-right (header → index → parity) and that each region's read failure surfaces a specific, identifiable error variant suitable for Layer 5 fallback decisions. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 257 incl. the new sidecar index damage test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test damages only the first index block (body_lba=1); damage of a non-first index block (body_lba in 2..shard_index_block_count) is not separately exercised — though the same code path is hit, the resulting 'block N' message would carry a different N; multi-block index damage spanning the entire index region is not tested as a distinct case; live MSL3040 hardware where index-region damage might co-occur with adjacent parity-body damage is not characterised against the SidecarParse-vs-Unrecoverable boundary on real hardware.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 12041b36 (Layer 3c catalog-present intermediate sidecar recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test catalog_present_recovery_succeeds_past_corrupt_intermediate_sidecar_header at scan.rs:965-1034 closes the catalog-present analog of my 1d4c726c residual. It pairs with scan.rs:920 catalog_less_recovery_unavailable_when_intermediate_sidecar_header_is_destroyed from codex 21e54153 to encode the full design §10.1 catalog-vs-catalog-less recovery boundary at multi-epoch chains: catalog-less + intermediate damage → unavailable, catalog-present + intermediate damage → recovery succeeds for non-dependent epochs. Both tests use the same multi_epoch_fixture_records(Some(1)) helper at scan.rs:480 — leveraging the parametric fixture I called out as naturally extensible in Claude review 1d4c726c observation 5. Observation 2: the test's four-stage structure at scan.rs:965-1034 is methodical. Stage 1 (scan.rs:968-981) computes the physical LBAs of BOTH the corrupt middle sidecar (tape_file 4) AND the healthy later sidecar (tape_file 6) via ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position, so they can be used as locate-call probe points later. Stage 2 (scan.rs:983-993) proves the scan path is damaged with the now-standard FilemarkMapDigestMismatch assertion. Stage 3 (scan.rs:995-1006) proves catalog acquisition bypasses scan via source.calls.is_empty(). Stage 4 (scan.rs:1008-1033) actually recovers ordinal 5 via the healthy later sidecar AND verifies the corrupted middle sidecar is never touched. Observation 3: the recovery target choice — ordinal 5, tape_file 5 body_lba 1 — is sharp. In the multi_epoch_fixture_records layout (bootstrap@0, object@1, sidecar@2, object@3, sidecar@4-CORRUPT, object@5, sidecar@6, bootstrap@7), ordinal 5 lives in epoch 2's object at tape_file 5. Its epoch sidecar is at tape_file 6, NOT tape_file 4. So recovering ordinal 5 should reach the healthy tape_file 6's sidecar exclusively. If the recovery path incorrectly tried sidecars in tape-file order (e.g. epoch 0 → epoch 1 → epoch 2), it would touch tape_file 4 and fail or produce wrong data. The test pins that recovery uses *the right sidecar for the failed ordinal*, not a sweep. Observation 4: the load-bearing assertions are scan.rs:1022-1033 — the positive 'source.calls.contains(&ScanCall::Locate(recovery_sidecar_header_lba))' AND the negative '!source.calls.contains(&ScanCall::Locate(corrupt_sidecar_header_lba))'. Together they document the isolation property: epoch 2's recovery cannot inadvertently propagate the epoch 1 corruption. This is exactly the design property that makes catalog-present recovery robust on multi-epoch tapes — the catalog map's per-epoch sidecar pointers are authoritative and the recovery path follows them surgically rather than scanning. Observation 5: recovered.sidecar_tape_file_number == 6 at scan.rs:1021 documents the SidecarRecoveryResult's tape-file-number field carries the correct healthy sidecar identifier, not the corrupted middle one. recovered.recovered_block == block(0x31) at scan.rs:1020 matches the fixture's epoch_blocks[2][1] at scan.rs:495 — proving end-to-end correctness of the ordinal→stripe→sidecar→reconstructed-bytes pipeline through the catalog-supplied map. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. The pair of intermediate-sidecar tests (catalog-less at scan.rs:920 + catalog-present at scan.rs:965) now mirror the first-sidecar pair (scan.rs:828 catalog-less + scan.rs:603 catalog-present from codex 5170ad4b), giving full first/intermediate × catalog-less/catalog-present coverage of the damaged-sidecar matrix. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 258 incl. the new catalog-present intermediate-sidecar recovery test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: last-sidecar damage in a multi-epoch chain (corrupt_sidecar_epoch = Some(N-1)) is the third corner of the catalog-present/catalog-less × first/intermediate/last matrix and is not yet covered; cross-epoch recovery where the FAILED ordinal IS in the corrupted-sidecar's epoch (not just a non-dependent epoch) under catalog presence — should surface SidecarParse via the catalog map's pointer to the corrupted sidecar — is not separately tested; live MSL3040 hardware where intermediate sidecar damage could co-occur with adjacent object damage at SCSI level is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 7c42f778 (Layer 3c later sidecar index spill recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_later_index_spill_damage_reports_the_damaged_block at recovery.rs:986-1038 closes the specific residual I called out in Claude review d32b0741 observation 6 — 'damage of a non-first index block (body_lba in 2..shard_index_block_count) is not separately exercised — though the same code path is hit, the resulting block N message would carry a different N'. The new test damages block N = shard_index_block_count - 1 at recovery.rs:999-1007 and asserts the message contains exactly `block {final_index_block}` at recovery.rs:1031-1034 via the format!() string interpolation. This pins the block_within_file parameter's propagation through read_required_block → SidecarParse format string at recovery.rs:334-337 as carrying the exact damaged block number, not a hardcoded '1' or '0'. Observation 2: the scheme(5, 2, 2) parameter choice at recovery.rs:988 is precisely engineered to force index spill. With k=5, m=2, S=2 the index payload is 10 (data shards) × 8-byte CRCs + 4 (parity shards) × 16-byte index entries = 144 bytes plus the header — which exceeds the 128-byte BLOCK_SIZE used by the recovery test fixtures, so shard_index_block_count must be > 2. The precondition assertion at recovery.rs:994-997 ('shard_index_block_count > 2') is the right defensive gate: if a future scheme/block-size choice collapsed the index to ≤ 2 blocks, the test would refuse to run rather than silently degrading to the same case the prior block-1 test already covers. Observation 3: the geometric assertion at recovery.rs:1016-1019 ('final_index_lba < first_parity_lba') documents the §5.6 sidecar layout's structural property — index blocks (1..shard_index_block_count) precede parity blocks (shard_index_block_count..n) in physical LBA order. This is the right gate to distinguish 'I damaged a spill index block' from 'I damaged the first parity block', which would surface as Unrecoverable (per recovery.rs:870) rather than SidecarParse. Observation 4: the recovery target ordinal 9 at recovery.rs:1025 is the highest ordinal in the epoch (data ordinals 0..9 with epoch_data_shards = k×S = 5×2 = 10). Recovering the last ordinal forces the recovery path to walk the entire index to find ordinal 9's stripe membership and peer pointers, which guarantees the spill index block is read. If the test recovered ordinal 0, it might only need the FIRST index block's bytes and could shortcut past the spill block. The choice of the last ordinal is sharp. Observation 5: this completes the index-damage matrix per region: recovery.rs:930 sidecar_index_damage_after_header (first index block, block 1, codex 9863e09d) + recovery.rs:987 (last index spill block, block N-1, this entry). With recovery.rs:826 header damage (block 0) + recovery.rs:870 parity-body damage, the four sidecar-region damage cases now span the full §5.6 layout: block 0 → SidecarParse 'block 0', block 1 → SidecarParse 'block 1', block N-1 → SidecarParse 'block N-1', blocks N..end → Unrecoverable. Each case carries a distinct, externally-observable error signature. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 259 incl. the new later-index spill damage test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test damages exactly one index spill block (block N-1); multi-block contiguous index damage (e.g. damaging blocks 1..N-1 simultaneously) is not separately tested — it would surface SidecarParse on the FIRST attempted block read, masking the propagation property the new test pins; non-contiguous interleaved index damage is similarly unverified; live MSL3040 hardware where multi-block index damage might co-occur with adjacent object damage is not characterised; index payload sizes for production scheme parameters (e.g. k=4, m=2, S=512 at default geometry) are not specifically pinned beyond the test's scheme(5,2,2) configuration.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry e043a9d0 (Layer 3c last-sidecar scan recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at scan.rs:1037 (catalog-less) and scan.rs:1083 (catalog-present) close BOTH residuals I called out in Claude review a33e1d15 — 'last-sidecar damage in a multi-epoch chain' AND 'cross-epoch recovery where the FAILED ordinal IS in the corrupted-sidecar's epoch under catalog presence'. With these, the damaged-sidecar matrix is now fully covered along both axes: first × catalog-less (scan.rs:828) + first × catalog-present (scan.rs:603) + intermediate × catalog-less (scan.rs:920) + intermediate × catalog-present (scan.rs:965) + last × catalog-less (scan.rs:1037, this entry) + last × catalog-present (scan.rs:1083, this entry) = full 3 × 2 matrix. The parametric multi_epoch_fixture_records(Some(N)) helper from codex 21e54153 has now been used at all three sidecar positions, validating my Claude review 1d4c726c observation 5's prediction that the fixture would extend naturally. Observation 2: the catalog-less last-sidecar test at scan.rs:1037-1080 carries a sharper structural assertion than its first/intermediate-sidecar siblings. Beyond the kinds-vector downgrade check at scan.rs:1045-1062 and the total_data_ordinals shift at scan.rs:1063-1066, it asserts at scan.rs:1067-1073 that 'max_sidecar_end_exclusive equals the PRIOR sidecar's protected_ordinal_end_exclusive' — proving the catalog-less protection watermark stops at the last healthy sidecar (epoch 1) rather than extending into the corrupted final epoch's range. This is the right §7.2.1 invariant to test at the last-sidecar position: the watermark must not optimistically advance past a sidecar that failed validation, otherwise Layer 5 would think recovery is available for an ordinal range that has no functional parity. Observation 3: the catalog-present test at scan.rs:1083-1190 is the most comprehensive of the entire scan damage matrix because it carries TWO recovery sub-cases, not one. The earlier-epoch recovery at scan.rs:1129-1154 (recovers ordinal 1 via tape_file 2's healthy sidecar, contains Locate(healthy_sidecar_header_lba), does NOT contain Locate(corrupt_sidecar_header_lba)) proves isolation. The corrupted-epoch recovery at scan.rs:1156-1189 (recovers ordinal 5 via tape_file 6's corrupted sidecar, expects SidecarParse, DOES contain Locate(corrupt_sidecar_header_lba)) proves the catalog correctly identified the right sidecar AND that the read failure surfaces cleanly. Together they document the design property: the catalog's per-epoch sidecar pointers are authoritative, recovery follows them surgically, and a corrupted sidecar fails cleanly per-epoch rather than poisoning recovery for unrelated epochs. Observation 4: the locate-call assertion at scan.rs:1183-1188 'corrupt_epoch_source.calls.contains(&ScanCall::Locate(corrupt_sidecar_header_lba))' is the load-bearing positive assertion for the corrupted-epoch sub-case. Without it, the test would only prove that 'something failed' for ordinal 5 — possibly because the catalog map was wrong, possibly because recovery short-circuited before touching the sidecar. The positive assertion proves the recovery path correctly LOCATED to the catalog-identified sidecar (so the catalog map's pointer is correct) and then the read at that LBA failed. This separates 'catalog map bug' from 'sidecar genuinely corrupt' in any future debug. Observation 5: the symmetric assertion pattern between the two sub-cases — both at scan.rs:1124-1127 (catalog acquisition makes no scan calls) and at scan.rs:1167-1170 (same property for the corrupt-epoch source) — documents that the catalog short-circuit at scan.rs:53-56 fires consistently regardless of whether the catalog points to a healthy or corrupted sidecar. This is the §10.1 invariant that catalog acquisition is purely structural metadata, independent of any sidecar's content. Observation 6: no production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. The complete 3 × 2 matrix now spans the design's catalog-vs-catalog-less recovery semantics for all corrupt-sidecar positions across multi-epoch chains. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 261 incl. the two new last-sidecar tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: multi-sidecar damage (e.g. epochs 0 AND 2 BOTH corrupt) is not exercised — would require fixture extension to accept a Vec<u64> of corrupt epoch indices; the catalog-present test's two sub-cases use fresh RecordingRawSource instances (scan.rs:1113 and scan.rs:1156) rather than sequential calls on a single source, so a regression that caused state leakage between consecutive recovery attempts on a real Layer 5 source would not be detected here; live MSL3040 hardware where last-sidecar damage might co-occur with adjacent final-bootstrap or filemark damage is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 7a4201b1 (Layer 3c default sidecar index sizing gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sidecar.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test default_geometry_index_layout_matches_design_sizing at sidecar.rs:1294-1374 closes the production-geometry residual I called out in Claude review 55d6f675 observation 6 — 'index payload sizes for production scheme parameters (e.g. k=4, m=2, S=512 at default geometry) are not specifically pinned beyond the test's scheme(5,2,2) configuration'. The test invokes the actual production helper crate::default_scheme_for_block_size(256 * 1024) at sidecar.rs:1297, so any future change to the default scheme parameter selection logic (lib.rs:179) would surface here as a test failure with clean arithmetic discrepancies, not just a vague 'something looks different'. Observation 2: the geometry constants pinned at sidecar.rs:1328-1338 match docs/layer3c-design.md §5.5 exactly — k=128, m=4, S=512 (so n=k+m=132 per stripe), logical_shard_count = 65,536 = k×S, parity_block_count = 2,048 = m×S, data_crc_count = 65,536 (one CRC per data shard), shard_index_block_count = 3 (the index spills across THREE blocks at 256 KiB). The assertion at sidecar.rs:1335-1338 with the explicit message 'default 256 KiB geometry should spill the ~544 KiB index into H=3 blocks' is documentation-quality: future readers see at a glance that the index size derives from the parity-index (2,048 × 16 = 32 KiB) + data-CRC (65,536 × 8 = 512 KiB) = ~544 KiB total, hence H=3 at block_size=256 KiB. Observation 3: the inline-split alignment assertion at sidecar.rs:1340-1348 documents the §5.6 invariant that block 0's inline_index_entry_bytes is a multiple of DATA_CRC_ENTRY_LEN (8). Without this property the split could fall inside a CRC entry, requiring partial-entry reassembly across blocks — the assertion proves the encoder rounds inline_index_entry_bytes down to the largest data-CRC-aligned value that fits in block 0 after subtracting the header and trailing CRC. A future regression that produced a non-aligned split would fail this assertion. Observation 4: the spill arithmetic at sidecar.rs:1351-1360 documents the exact distribution: inline block contains 28,658 data CRCs + 2,048 parity index entries, first spill block contains 32,767 data CRCs (filling capacity minus the trailing 8-byte CRC), final spill block contains 4,111 data CRCs (the remainder). The numbers sum: 28,658 + 32,767 + 4,111 = 65,536 = total data CRCs, matching data_crc_count. Three explicit integer assertions at sidecar.rs:1358-1360 mean a future regression that changed the inline split or spill packing would surface as ONE-of-three specific discrepancies pointing directly at which block's allocation drifted. Observation 5: the zero-fill assertion at sidecar.rs:1362-1368 documents the §7.7 deterministic-blocks invariant — unused trailing bytes in the final spill block (after 4,111 × 8 = 32,888 bytes of payload, before the 8-byte trailing block CRC) are zero-filled. This is the right §7.7 property to verify: a non-zero-filled tail would change the block CRC unpredictably across writes (poisoning bit-exact reproducibility) AND would leave a subtle channel for stale-buffer leakage. The all-zero assertion catches both classes of bug. Observation 6: the test avoids the memory blowup of constructing a full 256 KiB-block sidecar by calling encode_sidecar_index_blocks at sidecar.rs:1327 — which only encodes the index region, not the 2,048 × 256 KiB ≈ 512 MiB of parity body data. This is the right engineering choice; the alternative (calling encode_sidecar_tape_file) would have made the test slow AND would have required allocating ~512 MiB of parity shard placeholders. The codex self-review pass 1 explicitly noted this property. Observation 7: the round-trip via parse_sidecar_index_blocks at sidecar.rs:1370-1373 proves the encoded blocks deserialize back to byte-identical header + index — catching any future encode/decode asymmetry at production geometry. No production code change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no public API change — purely additive coverage; clippy stays clean. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 262 incl. the new default-geometry index sizing test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins ONE production block_size (256 KiB); the conservative_scheme_for_block_size variant at lib.rs:192 (different default for non-ideal block sizes) is not separately exercised at this geometry-pinning depth; alternative block sizes (e.g. 1 MiB or 64 KiB) are not pinned, so a future regression that changed how default_scheme_for_block_size scales with block_size would surface only at 256 KiB; live MSL3040 hardware where the actual 256 KiB block-write performance interacts with the 3-block sidecar index spill is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry b3eb4236 (Layer 3c catalog-present sequential recovery gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: this refactor closes the state-leakage residual I called out in Claude review 8aae1461 observation 6 — 'the catalog-present test's two sub-cases use fresh RecordingRawSource instances (scan.rs:1113 and scan.rs:1156) rather than sequential calls on a single source, so a regression that caused state leakage between consecutive recovery attempts on a real Layer 5 source would not be detected here.' The refactored test now uses a SINGLE `source` at scan.rs:1113 for BOTH recoveries — the healthy earlier-epoch recovery at scan.rs:1126-1135 AND the corrupted-last-epoch failure at scan.rs:1154-1163. If recovery left dangling internal state between calls (locate cursors, buffer state, cached headers), the second recovery's behavior would diverge from the fresh-source case and the test would surface a discrepancy. Observation 2: the call-count boundary at scan.rs:1152 ('let calls_after_healthy_recovery = source.calls.len();') is the load-bearing structural device. It snapshots the source's recorded call list at the moment the first recovery completed, so the subsequent assertion at scan.rs:1166 ('source.calls[calls_after_healthy_recovery..].contains(...)') can verify the second recovery's locate calls in isolation — without false-positives from the first recovery's calls polluting the assertion. This is the right surgical pattern: share the source to detect leakage, slice the call log to keep assertions scoped. Observation 3: the assertion ordering is correct. The healthy-recovery assertions at scan.rs:1140-1151 still check that source.calls (the full list, accumulated up to that point) contains the healthy sidecar locate AND does NOT contain the corrupt sidecar locate. Crucially this MUST be evaluated BEFORE the second recovery, since after the second recovery runs source.calls WOULD contain the corrupt sidecar locate. The test orders these correctly — assert healthy recovery's calls (scan.rs:1140-1151), snapshot boundary (scan.rs:1152), run corrupted recovery (scan.rs:1154-1163), assert ONLY post-boundary calls (scan.rs:1165-1169). A future regression that reordered or merged the assertions would surface as a false-positive 'corrupt sidecar locate was in the healthy recovery's call list', which the existing scan.rs:1146-1151 negative assertion would catch. Observation 4: the changes are purely additive in correctness terms — the test count remains at 262 (no new tests added, this is a refactor of the existing test from a33e1d15). All other previously-passing properties remain pinned: catalog acquisition makes no scan calls (scan.rs:1124), the failed_ordinal/recovered_block/sidecar_tape_file_number for ordinal 1 (scan.rs:1137-1139), the catalog map's authority for both healthy and corrupted epochs, the SidecarParse error variant for the corrupted-epoch case. Observation 5: this is the right minimal change to address the residual — codex did NOT expand the test's scope, add new fixtures, or refactor unrelated tests. The residual was specifically about state leakage between consecutive recovery attempts; the fix is specifically to share the source between attempts. Restraint matters in test refactors: a broader rewrite would have made the diff harder to review and risked introducing new bugs. No production code, no public API, no SCSI CDB, no sense-code, no tape-command behavior changed. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 262 unchanged from the prior count — this is a refactor of an existing test, not an addition, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the shared-source test still uses an in-memory RecordingRawSource; a real Layer 5 raw source backed by a SCSI drive could have stateful interactions (PERSISTENT RESERVE, mode page state, drive-side buffers) that this in-memory test cannot simulate; live MSL3040 hardware where consecutive recovery attempts on a real drive could expose drive-state leakage between SCSI command sequences is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry d400ac79 (Layer 3c scaled sidecar index sizing gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sidecar.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test scaled_scheme_index_layouts_match_design_sizing at sidecar.rs:1391-1519 closes the alternate-block-size and conservative-scheme residuals I called out in Claude review 4bcdebda — 'alternative block sizes (e.g. 1 MiB or 64 KiB) are not pinned' and 'the conservative_scheme_for_block_size variant at lib.rs:192 (different default for non-ideal block sizes) is not separately exercised'. The table-driven test covers three production scheme configurations beyond the default 256 KiB: default 512 KiB (k=128, m=4, S=256), default 1 MiB (k=128, m=4, S=128), and conservative 256 KiB (k=64, m=6, S=256). All four production profiles (the default 256 KiB at sidecar.rs:1334 plus the three new cases here) are now code-tested. Observation 2: the geometry-derivation arithmetic at sidecar.rs:1484-1491 is the load-bearing structural assertion. For each case, it computes the expected inline_index_entry_bytes from the scheme parameters via 'expected_parity_entries × PARITY_INDEX_ENTRY_LEN (16) + expected_data_crcs × DATA_CRC_ENTRY_LEN (8)' and asserts this equals BOTH the encoded header's inline_index_entry_bytes AND the case's expected_inline_index_bytes. Sample verification for default 512 KiB: 1,024 × 16 + 32,768 × 8 = 16,384 + 262,144 = 278,528 ✓; for default 1 MiB: 512 × 16 + 16,384 × 8 = 8,192 + 131,072 = 139,264 ✓; for conservative 256 KiB: 1,536 × 16 + 16,384 × 8 = 24,576 + 131,072 = 155,648 ✓. Three doubly-verified arithmetic equations across the three cases. Observation 3: all three new cases land at H=1 (shard_index_block_count = 1, the index fits in block 0 inline), so they exercise the inline-only encoding path that the default 256 KiB H=3 spill case did NOT exercise. This is the right diversification: the existing test pins H=3 spill, the new test pins H=1 inline; future regressions in either path surface separately. The codex's choice of three production-realistic block sizes (rather than synthetic edge cases) means a future scheme tuning change would surface here as a real-world impact assertion. Observation 4: the conservative_scheme_for_block_size variant at lib.rs:192 is now structurally pinned via the third case at sidecar.rs:1431-1442. k=64, m=6 (vs default's k=128, m=4) documents the conservative scheme's parity tradeoff — half the data density but 50% more parity (m/n = 6/70 ≈ 8.6% conservative vs 4/132 ≈ 3.0% default). The test demonstrates conservative still fits H=1 at 256 KiB blocks, so the conservative scheme does not pay an extra block-write cost at this block size — a useful operational property documented in code. Observation 5: the zero-fill assertion at sidecar.rs:1504-1512 is the H=1 analog of the H=3 final-spill zero-fill from the default 256 KiB test. It verifies unused bytes between 'SIDECAR_HEADER_LEN + expected_inline_index_bytes' and 'block_size - 8' (the trailing block CRC) are zero-filled. Same §7.7 deterministic-blocks invariant, applied at the inline-only configuration. Observation 6: the refactor of the existing default_geometry_index_layout_matches_design_sizing test to use descriptor_for_scheme + metadata_only_index_for helpers (visible at sidecar.rs:1338-1339) is a clean factoring — it makes the new scaled test ergonomic without duplicating descriptor/index construction code. The helpers construct only metadata/index entries, not parity shard bodies, so even the largest configuration (default 512 KiB: 1,024 × 512 KiB ≈ 512 MiB of parity that we DON'T allocate) runs in negligible memory. Observation 7: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage plus a clean helper refactor. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 263 incl. the new scaled-scheme index sizing test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: this test does not include a separate H>1 spill case for an alternate block size — e.g. default_scheme_for_block_size(128 KiB) might spill, but is not exercised; non-multiple-of-block-size data shard counts (where the inline split would fall mid-CRC) are still only implicitly covered via the alignment assertion; the conservative_scheme variant is only tested at 256 KiB and could land at a different H if applied to a smaller block size; live MSL3040 hardware where actual block-size negotiation interacts with the scheme selection (especially the inverse relationship between block_size and S) is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry d8fe0487 (Layer 3c CRC-64/XZ pure-codec gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sidecar.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test crc64_xz_testing_plan_vectors_and_little_endian_packing_are_stable at sidecar.rs:1127-1152 directly satisfies docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:50-60 §2.1 'CRC-64/XZ' requirements line-by-line. Empty buffer at sidecar.rs:1129 (CRC = 0x0000_0000_0000_0000), one-byte buffer at sidecar.rs:1130-1131 (0x00 → 0x1FAD_A173_6467_3F59, 0xFF → 0xFF00_0000_0000_0000), all-zero 256 KiB at sidecar.rs:1133+1139, all-0xFF 256 KiB at sidecar.rs:1134+1140, deterministic patterned 256 KiB at sidecar.rs:1135-1141, single-bit-flip avalanche at sidecar.rs:1143-1146, and on-tape endianness at sidecar.rs:1148-1151. All five §2.1 bullets are now pinned in code with explicit expected values. Observation 2: the endianness assertion at sidecar.rs:1148-1151 is the load-bearing assertion for on-tape compatibility. It pins CRC64_XZ_CHECK_VALUE.to_le_bytes() == [0xFA, 0x39, 0x19, 0xDF, 0xBB, 0xC9, 0x5D, 0x99], which is the canonical CRC-64/XZ check value 0x995D_C9BB_DF19_39FA stored in little-endian byte order. This is the byte sequence Remanence writes to tape for the trailing block CRC at every fixed-block boundary; a future regression that switched to big-endian or mid-endian packing would silently corrupt every sidecar tape file ever written but would surface here as a single test failure with a clear byte-vector discrepancy. Pinning the actual byte sequence — not just the integer — is the right level of strictness. Observation 3: the avalanche assertion at sidecar.rs:1143-1146 documents the CRC's bit-diffusion property. The patterned buffer's CRC at byte index 12,345 is pinned to a specific value (0xF070_7C60_4A2B_85B2), and assert_ne!() against the original CRC at the same index proves the single-bit flip produces a different CRC. This catches the regression class where someone accidentally swaps in a CRC variant with worse avalanche properties (e.g. an additive checksum) — the assert_ne would fail because the bit-flipped buffer would still have the same checksum value. The combination of EXACT-value and DISTINCT-value assertions is the right defensive pattern. Observation 4: the patterned buffer construction at sidecar.rs:1135-1137 ('(i * 37 + i / 251 * 17 + 0x5a) & 0xff') is a deterministic pseudo-random byte sequence — not truly random, but produces bytes with good distribution across [0, 255]. This satisfies the §2.1 'random 256 KiB blocks' bullet while keeping the test deterministic across runs and platforms. A truly random buffer (rand::thread_rng) would break reproducibility; a uniform pattern (all 0x5A) would not exercise the CRC's bit diffusion. The (multiplier 37 + divisor 251 + offset 17 + base 0x5A) formula gives non-trivial mixing without needing a real PRNG dependency. Observation 5: the choice to test single bytes 0x00 AND 0xFF is sharp. CRC-64/XZ uses init=0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF, refin=true, refout=true, xorout=0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF — so the byte 0xFF, after bit-reflection of the input byte (becomes 0xFF unchanged) XORed into the init register's low byte (0xFF ⊕ 0xFF = 0x00), then carried through 8 cycles, then reflected and xored with xorout, produces the specific value 0xFF00_0000_0000_0000. The fact that one-byte 0xFF results in this 'leading byte non-zero, rest zero' pattern is a CRC-64/XZ-specific signature; a variant CRC (e.g. CRC-64/ECMA-182 with different init/xorout) would produce a different value at this input. The test discriminates between CRC-64/XZ specifically and 'just any 64-bit CRC'. Observation 6: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage. The crc64_xz function and CRC64_XZ_CHECK_VALUE constant are existing production helpers; this test pins their behavior at the testing-plan §2.1 reference points. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 264 incl. the new CRC-64/XZ vector test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins CRC-64/XZ on inputs at sizes 0, 1, and 256 KiB; CRC behavior at sizes between 1 and 256 KiB (where partial-byte-table-lookup boundary cases would surface) is not separately exercised; the bit-flip avalanche assertion exercises exactly one byte offset (12,345); flips at start/end/word-boundary positions are not separately pinned; live MSL3040 hardware where actual on-tape CRC verification interacts with drive-side LBP-PI checksums is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 9c90ce02 (Layer 3c RS codec randomized/full-block gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/codec.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at codec.rs:579 and codec.rs:624 close the remaining gaps in docs/remanence-testing-plan.md §2.2 'RS / GF(2^8) implementation'. The §2.2 bullets are: Appendix A encode vector (already covered by appendix_a_generator_and_encoding_vector_match_design at codec.rs:646), Appendix A reconstruction vector (already covered by appendix_a_reconstruction_vector_recovers_d1_from_d0_and_p0 at codec.rs:657), randomized encode/reconstruct for small schemes (NEW — codec.rs:579), default-scheme smoke at full block size with deterministic pseudo-random data (NEW — codec.rs:624), and incremental accumulation byte-identity (already covered by incremental_accumulation_matches_batch_encoding_in_any_order at codec.rs:671). All five §2.2 bullets are now pinned in code. Observation 2: the table-driven small-scheme matrix at codec.rs:579-621 covers exactly the three schemes called out by name in the testing plan — (k=2,m=2), (k=4,m=2), (k=8,m=3). Each scheme tests three erasure sets at varying capacities: single-shard, mixed-data-plus-parity, and full-m capacity. The (k=8,m=3) case tests a 3-erasure set [1, 8, 10] that spans both data range and parity range — the most demanding case at the m-capacity limit. Observation 3: the deterministic_random_data helper at codec.rs:400-413 uses the xorshift64* PRNG (state ^= state<<13, state ^= state>>7, state ^= state<<17, take high byte) with per-scheme seeds. This is the right choice: deterministic across runs and platforms (no rand crate dependency, no platform-dependent thread RNG), but with good byte distribution to actually exercise the GF(2^8) arithmetic across all 256 field elements (an all-zero or single-byte-pattern input could mask bugs in specific gf_mul / gf_inv code paths). The per-scheme unique seeds (0x0202_0000_0000_0001, 0x0402_0000_0000_0002, 0x0803_0000_0000_0003) encode k/m into the seed value as a documentation aid. Observation 4: the assert_reconstructs_erasure_set helper at codec.rs:415-446 is the load-bearing structural assertion. After reconstruction, it verifies EVERY shard (both data AND parity) matches its original encode-time bytes. This is stricter than the design requires — the design only mandates failed data ordinals be recoverable — but it pins a useful invariant: the reconstruction algorithm doesn't accidentally corrupt non-failed shards (which could happen with a buggy matrix-inversion path that wrote into the wrong indices). The 'data shard {index} was not reconstructed byte-identically' and 'parity shard {index} was not reconstructed byte-identically' error messages identify exactly which shard diverged on regression. Observation 5: the default-scheme smoke test at codec.rs:624-637 is precisely targeted. It uses the production default_scheme() at lib.rs:213 (k=128, m=4) at the production 256 KiB block size — the actual operational geometry, not a synthetic edge case. The erasure set [5, 42, 100, 127] spans the data-shard range with 4 entries (exactly m=4 at capacity). The test takes ~19 seconds (~7% of the test workspace time) because RS reconstruction of 128 × 256 KiB ≈ 32 MiB of shards through a 4×4 inverted Vandermonde-Cauchy matrix is computationally non-trivial — but it's the only test that verifies the codec at the bytes-actually-on-tape size. A regression that worked at small block sizes but broke at 256 KiB (e.g. a SIMD path that fails on non-aligned data, or a buffer-reuse bug that surfaces only above some threshold) would surface here. Observation 6: the assertion at codec.rs:631-634 that codec.data_blocks() == 128 AND codec.parity_blocks() == 4 AND data[0].len() == block_size AND parity[0].len() == block_size pins both the scheme parameters AND the encoded shard sizes. A future regression that changed default_scheme()'s constants would surface here as a clear arithmetic discrepancy. Observation 7: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage and helper extraction. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 266 incl. the two new RS codec tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the default-scheme test uses ONE erasure pattern at exactly m=4 capacity; below-capacity (1, 2, 3 erasures) and parity-shard erasures within the default scheme are not separately exercised — though the small-scheme matrix tests these patterns at smaller k/m and the algorithm generalizes; the small-scheme matrix uses block sizes 257/509/769 (non-aligned primes), which is good for catching alignment bugs but does NOT specifically test the 256 KiB-aligned paths the production codec uses; the codex entry has idref: null (no specific Claude review prompted it), which is non-standard but the work is sound regardless.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 14bc7cb9 (Layer 3c ordinal mapping test-plan gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/mapping.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at mapping.rs:359 and mapping.rs:398 close the remaining gap in docs/remanence-testing-plan.md §2.3 'Ordinal and stripe mapping'. The §2.3 bullets are: round-trip mapping (already covered by stripe_data_to_ordinal_round_trips_every_data_ordinal_in_neighborhood at mapping.rs earlier tests), consecutive-ordinal distribution per §5.3 row-major (implied by both new tests), and 'Every (stripe_index, data_index) appears exactly once per epoch' (NEW — mapping.rs:359). The full mapping algebra is now pinned in code. Observation 2: the coordinate-coverage test at mapping.rs:359-395 implements the §2.3 'exactly once per epoch' contract structurally. It iterates 3 synthetic epochs at mapping.rs:363, maintains a 2D 'seen[stripe][data]' boolean table at mapping.rs:364-367, asserts !*slot at mapping.rs:379-382 to catch DUPLICATES (each coordinate hit more than once), and asserts every slot is true at mapping.rs:386-392 to catch MISSED coordinates. The dual-check structure is the right defensive pattern: a one-sided 'every coordinate seen' check would miss a duplicate-then-missed-elsewhere bug, and a one-sided 'no duplicates' check would miss an entire row that was never visited. With both, the test catches any permutation bug in ordinal_to_stripe at mapping.rs:115. Observation 3: the small_scheme() at mapping.rs:172 (k=4, m=2, S=3 → 12 data shards/epoch) is chosen so the full mapping is enumerable in a tractable test (3 epochs × 12 ordinals = 36 mappings). This is exactly the right scale: small enough for brute-force coverage verification, large enough to span multiple rows and stripes per epoch. A larger scheme would have made the test slow without adding coverage; a smaller scheme might miss row-vs-stripe ordering bugs that only surface with S>1 AND k>1. Observation 4: the partial-epoch boundary test at mapping.rs:398-454 covers the §5.4 implicit-zero handling at six carefully-chosen D values: 0 (empty epoch), 1 (first shard), k-1=3 (end of row 0, full row excluding one), k=4 (start of row 1), k+1=5 (one into row 1), and S*k-1=11 (last data shard). These bracket every row-transition boundary: D=0/1 around the first shard, D=k-1/k/k+1 around the row 0→row 1 transition, D=S*k-1 at the last-shard-vs-full-epoch boundary. The choice is sharp because row-major ordinal mapping (stripe=offset%S, row=offset/S) has different behavior at these exact boundaries — a future regression to column-major (stripe=offset/k, row=offset%k) or to a different stripe-mixing function would surface here with a clear (stripe_index, data_index) discrepancy. Observation 5: the implicit-zero assertion at mapping.rs:436-452 is the load-bearing structural device for §5.4. When D < epoch_data_shards, the first implicit-zero slot lands at ordinal_base + real_shards_in_final_epoch and the test verifies its (stripe, data) coordinate matches the row-major formula. This proves the implicit-zero slot is contiguous with the real data shards in the row-major mapping — a critical §5.4 property: the implicit-zero shards must fill the remainder of the same epoch's (stripe, data) grid, not jump to a different stripe or row. A bug that placed implicit-zero shards at a different coordinate would corrupt RS reconstruction since the parity shards were computed assuming the row-major contiguity. Observation 6: the round-trip assertion at mapping.rs:429-433 ('stripe_data_to_ordinal(&addr, &s) == ordinal') is the right strict invariant to pin at every boundary case. It catches any future regression where ordinal_to_stripe and stripe_data_to_ordinal diverge — they MUST be inverses for the recovery path to correctly identify which ordinal needs reconstruction. Observation 7: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage; clippy stays clean. The codex entry has idref: null (no specific Claude review prompted it, matching the prior 9c90ce02 codec entry's pattern); the choice to advance testing-plan §2.3 alongside the just-completed §2.2 codec gates is a sensible 'finish the pure-codec testing bucket' approach. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 268 incl. the two new ordinal mapping tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the coordinate-coverage test uses small_scheme (k=4, m=2, S=3); production scheme (k=128, m=4, S=512) is not enumerated — but the algorithm generalizes and the brute-force enumeration would be 65,536 iterations per epoch, prohibitive at test time; the boundary test uses six discrete D values but does not test arbitrary D in (0, S*k); the implicit-zero test pins the FIRST implicit-zero slot but doesn't enumerate all implicit-zero coordinates in the partial epoch; live MSL3040 hardware where actual READ POSITION block counts surface different ordinal-to-LBA mappings is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry e3a525e9 (Layer 3c explicit row-major ordinal mapping gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/mapping.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test consecutive_ordinals_follow_row_major_stripe_then_data_index_pattern at mapping.rs:397-427 directly addresses the §5.3 row-major contract that I noted was only IMPLIED by the prior mapping tests in Claude review f5842612 observation 1 ('consecutive-ordinal distribution per §5.3 row-major (implied by both new tests)'). The new test makes the implication explicit by stepping through one full synthetic epoch and asserting per-ordinal that stripe_index = offset % S AND data_index = offset / S — the exact row-major formula. The §2.3 testing-plan 'Consecutive ordinals distribute across stripes according to the [row-major pattern]' bullet is now satisfied by a dedicated test, not by inference from the coordinate-coverage / partial-epoch boundary tests at mapping.rs:359 and mapping.rs:430. Observation 2: the assertion messages at mapping.rs:411 ('consecutive ordinals must walk stripe_index first') and mapping.rs:418 ('data index must advance only after all stripes in the row are assigned') are documentation-quality. A future reader investigating a test failure sees the intent immediately; a regression that swapped stripe and data axes (turning row-major into column-major) would fail at the first off-by-one boundary with a clear message identifying which axis advanced incorrectly. Observation 3: the next-epoch reset assertion at mapping.rs:422-426 is a load-bearing structural check. After walking all S*k=12 ordinals in epoch 2, the test computes ordinal_to_stripe for base + epoch_data_shards (the first ordinal of epoch 3) and asserts neighborhood == 3, stripe_index == 0, position == Data{index: 0}. This catches a regression class where the mapping function might silently roll over within the same neighborhood (e.g. mod-arithmetic that wraps stripe_index back to 0 but doesn't advance neighborhood). Without this assertion, a future bug that swapped 'increment neighborhood after S*k ordinals' for 'increment neighborhood every S*k ordinals from BOT' would still pass the per-ordinal pattern check but fail at neighborhood-rollover boundaries. Observation 4: the choice of epoch=2 (not 0) at mapping.rs:401 is sharp. Testing in the second epoch (rather than the first) ensures the base = epoch * epoch_data_shards calculation correctly identifies the epoch-2 starting ordinal AND that the row-major formula works at non-zero base offsets. A regression that worked for epoch 0 but broke for higher epochs (e.g. a misplaced absolute-vs-relative offset) would surface here, where it would not surface in a test that only walked epoch 0. Observation 5: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage. The codex entry has idref pointing to my previous f5842612 review, which is correct response-pattern: a no-finding review with a noted residual should still drive incremental work to close the residual, even though no explicit fix was demanded. The codex correctly interpreted 'observation about implication' as 'gap worth closing'. Observation 6: combined with the existing tests at mapping.rs:359 (coordinate coverage), mapping.rs:430 (partial-epoch boundaries), and the round-trip tests, the §2.3 ordinal/stripe mapping testing-plan section is now FULLY pinned by explicit dedicated tests for each bullet — round-trip, consecutive-ordinal distribution, and every-coordinate-once. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 269 incl. the new explicit row-major test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test uses small_scheme (k=4, m=2, S=3); the production scheme (k=128, m=4, S=512) is not enumerated here, but the algorithm is identical and the small scheme exercises the same mod-and-divide arithmetic path; the next-epoch reset is verified for the epoch 2→3 transition only, not for the epoch 0→1 transition or arbitrary epoch boundaries — though the row-major formula is invariant to which epoch number is used; live MSL3040 hardware where the actual ordinal-to-LBA mapping at production geometry hits different code paths is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry e8db53f7 (Layer 3c filemark-map catalog row-order gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/filemark_map.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test canonical_projection_digest_is_stable_across_catalog_row_order at filemark_map.rs:716-747 directly satisfies docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:135 §4 'Canonical projection digest is stable across map insertion order' — the first §4 bullet, previously unverified by an explicit code test. The test constructs the same 5-entry filemark map two ways at filemark_map.rs:717-732: ordered via FilemarkMap::new(entries.clone()) and shuffled (rows passed as [3, 1, 4, 0, 2]) via the new FilemarkMap::from_unordered_entries. It then asserts three invariants: entries() match (filemark_map.rs:734), canonical_projection_bytes() byte-identical (filemark_map.rs:735-742), canonical_digest() SHA-256 match (filemark_map.rs:743-746). All three layers — normalized internal order, canonical CBOR projection, and final digest — must be stable across input order. Observation 2: the new public function from_unordered_entries at filemark_map.rs:223-226 is minimal and well-factored. Two lines of code: sort_by_key(tape_file_number) then delegate to Self::new(). The factoring is correct — sorting happens once at the public boundary, validation logic is unchanged. The docstring at filemark_map.rs:216-222 documents the contract precisely: 'canonical digest projection is defined over ascending tape_file_number, not over backend row order.' This is exactly the invariant the test pins. Observation 3: the negative test unordered_catalog_rows_still_reject_non_dense_file_numbers at filemark_map.rs:749-763 is essential for completeness. Without it, a future regression that bypassed dense-numbering validation via from_unordered_entries (e.g. a refactor that accepted sparse rows assuming they'd be filled in) would silently accept malformed input. The test passes a [tape_file=2, tape_file=0] pair (missing tape_file=1) and verifies the FilemarkMapReconstruct('dense from 0') error fires AFTER the sort step at filemark_map.rs:224. This proves sorting doesn't accidentally smooth over a hole — the dense-from-0 check still runs in FilemarkMap::new after normalization. Observation 4: the shuffle pattern [3, 1, 4, 0, 2] at filemark_map.rs:725-731 is non-trivial — it's not a simple reverse (which might leak sorted-or-reverse-sorted assumptions through stability bugs) and not a pairwise swap. Index 3 (object_b at tape_file=3) leads, followed by index 1 (object_a at tape_file=1), index 4 (final bootstrap at tape_file=4), index 0 (BOT bootstrap at tape_file=0), index 2 (sidecar at tape_file=2). The shuffle deliberately puts higher tape_file numbers first, so any partial-sort regression that only checked 'is the first element the smallest' would still fail. Observation 5: the canonical_projection_bytes() comparison is the load-bearing assertion. A regression that produced the same digest from different bytes (an unlikely hash collision) AND would also fail an order-dependent canonical encoding (more likely — e.g. a non-canonical CBOR encoder that serialized keys in HashMap iteration order) would pass the canonical_digest match but fail the canonical_projection_bytes match. By comparing BOTH the bytes AND the digest, the test catches both classes of regression. Observation 6: no SCSI CDB, no sense-code, no tape-command, no rem-tar body-format, no catalog-commit, no crash/restart behavior changed — this is purely a new public API surface plus tests. The new from_unordered_entries function is genuinely useful for Layer 5 catalog integration: when Layer 5 queries catalog_tape_files WHERE tape_id = ? ORDER BY ... the row order depends on the SQL ORDER BY clause AND the storage layer's tuple ordering for ties. Without from_unordered_entries, callers would have to remember to add ORDER BY tape_file_number, and a missing ORDER BY would silently produce a wrong digest. With from_unordered_entries, the order-independence is the API guarantee. Observation 7: the codex entry has idref: null (an independent §4 work item, not a response to a specific Claude review). The implementation is sound and the test is comprehensive; I'd have been comfortable seeing this in either pattern. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 271 incl. the two new filemark-map row-order tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test exercises 5 entries; very large maps (e.g. 1000+ tape files, the realistic production scale at S=512 with many objects) are not enumerated — the sort is O(n log n) so the algorithm scales, but a future regression that imposed a small-vector optimization with different behavior past some threshold could surface only at production scale; the §4 bullets 2-7 (digest excludes content hashes, full vs. intermediate bootstrap validation, prefix scope, watermark vs. total ordinals, damaged-sidecar/no-catalog FilemarkMapDigestMismatch) are not directly addressed by this entry — they remain as separate §4 gates that still need explicit tests; live MSL3040 hardware where Layer 5's actual catalog query semantics interact with this API is not characterised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 1b69c74b (Layer 3c body-facing filemark rejection gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: this is a *behavior change* (not just a test addition), correctly closing docs/remanence-testing-plan.md:173 §5 'Body formats cannot write filemarks'. The implementation change at sink.rs:1684-1688 replaces the previous backend-forwarding behavior with unconditional rejection: BlockSink::write_filemarks(_count) now always returns Err(invalid_input('ParitySink: body-facing write_filemarks is disabled; Layer 3c owns object, sidecar, and bootstrap filemarks')). This pins the v0.4.4 ownership rule as an API-level invariant — no body format (rem-tar, Layer 5 generic writer) can sneak a filemark through the ParitySink trait surface, even by accident. Observation 2: the rejection error variant choice is correct. ScsiError::InvalidInput (wrapped in TapeIoError::CheckCondition) maps to 'caller did something the drive can't do' rather than a genuine drive error. A test calling write_filemarks expecting success would surface as a clear CheckCondition with the specific 'body-facing write_filemarks' substring — distinct from any real SCSI sense-key error. The error string explicitly names the three filemark categories Layer 3c owns (object, sidecar, bootstrap), so a future operator reading the error knows the v0.4.4 design intent immediately. Observation 3: the legacy-backed regression test body_facing_write_filemarks_is_rejected_without_touching_inner_sink at sink.rs:2851-2874 is rigorous about 'no side effects'. After each rejected call it asserts (a) the InvalidInput error fires with the expected substring, AND (b) sink.position().lba remains 0 (the tape cursor hasn't moved), AND (c) after the sink drops, inner.filemarks is empty (no backend filemark recorded), AND (d) inner.next_lba() is 0 (no backend block writes either). Five distinct after-state assertions per call. A regression that bypassed the rejection and forwarded to the backend would fail (c) and (d); a regression that rejected but mutated state before erroring would fail (b). Observation 4: the raw-backed regression test raw_backed_body_facing_write_filemarks_is_rejected_before_raw_barrier at sink.rs:2067-2088 is the structural mirror for the RawTapeSink path via ParitySink::new_sidecar_only. The final assertion 'raw.events.is_empty()' AFTER all three count values [0, 1, 2] have been tried proves the raw sink received zero operations across all rejected calls — closing the gap that a backend-specific bypass could still emit filemarks at the raw level. The two tests together cover BOTH ParitySink backends (Legacy and Raw enum variants at sink.rs:103), so a future regression that fixed one path but not the other would fail. Observation 5: the choice of counts [0, 1, 2] in both tests is the right edge-case selection. count=0 is the degenerate 'no-op filemark request' that some code paths might short-circuit before reaching the reject — testing it explicitly forces the reject to fire even for the no-op case. count=1 is the normal single-filemark write. count=2 is the batched case that the legacy SCSI semantics treat as a special-case path. By exercising all three, the test guarantees the rejection is unconditional, not conditional on the count value. Observation 6: the previous test 'write_filemarks_passes_through' is correctly REMOVED (visible in git diff as `-    fn write_filemarks_passes_through()`). It tested the now-obsolete forward-to-backend behavior; keeping it after the rejection change would have caused a contradictory test failure. The removal is the right cleanup, not a coverage regression — the new rejection-tests SUPERSEDE the prior pass-through test by enforcing a stricter contract. Observation 7: the codex summary explicitly notes 'internal Layer 3c synchronous filemark/barrier paths remain unchanged' — meaning finish_object's internal call to write_filemark() on the raw sink, sidecar emission's barrier path at sink.rs:1495, bootstrap emission's filemark at sink.rs:916, and the resume sidecar barrier at resume.rs:721 all still work. They use write_filemark() directly on the RawTapeSink (or use the LegacyBlock backend's internal API), not the BlockSink::write_filemarks trait method. The rejection is at the EXTERNAL trait boundary only, which is the right granularity. Observation 8: the invalid_input helper at sink.rs:126-128 is a clean three-line factoring that creates TapeIoError::CheckCondition(ScsiError::InvalidInput(message)) for &'static str messages. Reusing this helper for the rejection (sink.rs:1685) and for other invariant checks (sink.rs:1569, 1575, 1580) keeps the error shape consistent across the file. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 271 incl. the two new rejection tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 91e8ef3d (Layer 3c filemark-map non-circular digest and open-epoch gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/filemark_map.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at filemark_map.rs:818 and filemark_map.rs:877 close TWO specific §4 residuals I called out in Claude review 259091e6 — 'digest excludes content hashes and bootstrap payload bytes; final bootstrap can include its own structural map entry without circularity' (testing-plan §4 line 136-137) AND 'map_total_data_ordinals and highest_protected_ordinal differ in an open-epoch case; recovery uses the latter' (§4 line 143-144). Combined with the prior canonical_projection_digest_is_stable_across_catalog_row_order from codex e8db53f7 and the existing scan damage tests, the §4 filemark-map digest gates are now substantially covered. Observation 2: the non-circularity test at filemark_map.rs:819-875 is methodically structured to prove the design intent. It uses FilemarkMapBuilder::push_bootstrap_and_digest(true) at filemark_map.rs:827 to commit a final bootstrap AND simultaneously generate the digest covering a map THAT INCLUDES the bootstrap's own entry — proving the digest computation handles the self-referential map structure without circularity (the digest is over the STRUCTURAL projection of the map, not the bootstrap's payload bytes). The test then writes TWO different bootstrap payloads with the SAME digest but DIFFERENT free-form fields (sequence at filemark_map.rs:840+846, writer-id at lines 841+847, timestamp at lines 842+848) and asserts block_a != block_b (the payload bytes differ) but parsed_a.filemark_map_digest == parsed_b.filemark_map_digest == final_commit.digest (the digest is invariant under payload changes). This proves the non-circularity property — the digest is well-defined for self-referential maps. Observation 3: the use of REAL bootstrap encoder/parser (write_bootstrap_block at filemark_map.rs:853, parse_bootstrap_block at filemark_map.rs:861) is the right discipline. A regression that snuck payload bytes into the digest computation would surface here as parsed_a.filemark_map_digest != parsed_b.filemark_map_digest, because the test exercises the full encode→write→parse→extract-digest pipeline that production uses. A mock-based test would miss this. The final assertion at filemark_map.rs:871-874 — 'map_after_final_bootstrap.canonical_digest() == final_commit.digest.map_sha256' — closes the loop: the canonical SHA-256 over the structural projection matches what the digest carries. Observation 4: the open-epoch test at filemark_map.rs:878-905 is precisely targeted at the §7.2.1 watermark-vs-total distinction. It builds a map with object@1 carrying 5 data blocks but sidecar@2 protecting only 3 ordinals (protected_ordinal_end_exclusive=3, the last argument to TapeFileMapEntry::parity_sidecar at filemark_map.rs:882). This creates the open-epoch case: 5 ordinals committed on tape, only 3 parity-protected. The strict inequality assertion at filemark_map.rs:890-893 ('digest.highest_protected_ordinal < digest.map_total_data_ordinals') documents the design property that ordinal commit (catalog row visible) is decoupled from parity protection (sidecar emitted) — the 2-ordinal gap at the tail of the open epoch is the realistic scenario every object-write-then-finish_object produces. Observation 5: the recovery-boundary assertion at filemark_map.rs:895-904 is the load-bearing test of the §9 recovery contract. After validating the map against its digest into a ScopedFilemarkMap, the test asserts (a) scope.recoverable(2) succeeds — ordinal 2 is below the watermark (3) and protected; (b) scope.recoverable(3) returns UnrecoverablePendingEpoch with failed_ordinal=3 AND watermark=3 — ordinal 3 is AT the exact watermark boundary and is pending (not yet protected). The boundary value is the right test point: a regression that used >= instead of > (or vice versa) in the protection check would surface here as a flipped pass/fail. Observation 6: the UnrecoverablePendingEpoch variant carries both failed_ordinal AND watermark fields, both verified at filemark_map.rs:898-904. This documents the Layer 5 contract — when recovery returns this variant, Layer 5 must fall back to another copy (the parity for this ordinal is pending and not yet on tape). A regression that returned a generic Unrecoverable would lose the 'pending vs permanently lost' distinction that Layer 5 uses to decide between 'retry on this tape later' (pending) versus 'this copy is dead, use another' (permanently lost). Observation 7: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command/rem-tar/catalog-commit/crash-restart behavior changed — purely additive test coverage. The implementation correctness (digest computation, recovery boundary check) was already in place; these tests pin the behavior at testing-plan §4 reference points. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 273 incl. the two new digest/open-epoch tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the §4 bullets 3-5 ('Full-map bootstrap validates a complete reconstructed map' / 'Intermediate bootstrap validates only its prefix' / 'Prefix scope exposes only TarOnlyUnverified access outside the validated prefix') are not directly addressed here — though they are partly covered by the existing scan damage tests (scan.rs:828 etc.). The open-epoch test pins one specific (total=5, watermark=3) configuration; multi-epoch open cases (e.g. epoch 0 finished, epoch 1 partial, epoch 2 partial) are not separately exercised; live MSL3040 hardware where actual SCSI-induced timing produces realistic open-epoch states is not characterised here.
