author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 6f45ac04 (Layer 3c sidecar/bootstrap ordinal-bypass gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_and_bootstrap_tape_files_do_not_advance_data_ordinals at sink.rs:2012-2111 closes the §5 line 174 'Sidecars and bootstraps bypass ParityDataOrdinal' property I called out in Claude review 63438afb observation 7 as 'a related but separate property not directly tested'. Combined with the prior body-facing filemark rejection from codex 1b69c74b, the §5 §10.1 separation of body-format writes vs. Layer 3c-internal writes is now structurally complete: body writes can't emit filemarks (codex 1b69c74b), and sidecar/bootstrap writes don't consume data ordinals (this entry). Observation 2: the test scenario exercises a realistic multi-object/multi-bootstrap tape sequence. The sequence at sink.rs:2021-2063 lays down 7 distinct tape files in order: initial bootstrap (tape_file 0), first object with 12 ordinals (tape_file 1), first sidecar (tape_file 2, protects ordinals 0..12), intermediate bootstrap (tape_file 3), second object with 2 ordinals (tape_file 4), final partial sidecar (tape_file 5, protects 12..14), final bootstrap (tape_file 6). The test's 7-filemark assertion at sink.rs:2067-2074 documents this layout structurally. Observation 3: the load-bearing bypass assertion at sink.rs:2055-2058 ('second.first_parity_data_ordinal == 12') pins the contract. After tape_files 0 (bootstrap), 1 (object 0..12), 2 (sidecar), and 3 (bootstrap), the second object's first_parity_data_ordinal is exactly 12 — not 13 or 14 or some larger value that would imply the intervening bootstrap/sidecar tape files consumed data ordinals. The 'must not consume data ordinals' assertion message at sink.rs:2057 documents the design intent directly. A future regression where the ordinal counter incremented for any non-object tape file would fail this with a clear discrepancy. Observation 4: the final digest assertion at sink.rs:2106-2109 ('digest.map_total_data_ordinals == 14') confirms the same property from the digest side. With 12 ordinals in object 1 and 2 ordinals in object 2, the total data ordinals must be exactly 14, not 14 + (something for bootstraps) + (something for sidecars). The 'only object blocks, not sidecar/bootstrap blocks, contribute to ParityDataOrdinal' assertion message at sink.rs:2108 is documentation-quality. Observation 5: the test uses REAL bytes parsing via crate::sidecar::classify_sidecar_header_block at sink.rs:2080-2087 and crate::sidecar::parse_sidecar_tape_file at sink.rs:2090-2094 and crate::bootstrap::parse_bootstrap_block at sink.rs:2099-2103. This is the right discipline — the test exercises the full write-and-parse pipeline, so a regression that produced byte-identical writes but stored wrong metadata internally would still surface. The final partial sidecar's protected_ordinal_start=12, protected_ordinal_end_exclusive=14, real_data_shard_count=2 at sink.rs:2095-2097 confirm the sidecar correctly covers only the second object's two ordinals. Observation 6: the extension of RecordingRawTapeSink to retain written blocks (noted in codex summary) is the right enabling change. The previous version captured only events; retaining blocks allows tests like this one to PARSE the resulting tape bytes, which is far more discriminating than counting events. Future tests that need to verify byte-level tape contents can now use the same fixture. Observation 7: the tape_file_count=7 assertion at sink.rs:2105 cross-checks the filemark count assertion at sink.rs:2067-2074 — each of the 7 tape files produces exactly one filemark, so 7 tape files and 7 filemarks must agree. If a tape file emitted ZERO or TWO filemarks (a regression in the bootstrap/sidecar emit paths), these two assertions would diverge. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change beyond the test fixture extension, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage with a fixture extension that doesn't change semantics. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 274 incl. the new bypass test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test uses small_scheme (k=4, m=2, S=3) with epoch_data_shards=12; production-scale schemes are not exercised here, though the bypass logic is independent of scheme size; the test does not exercise interleaving of MULTIPLE intermediate bootstraps between objects (only one); the test does not exercise the case where a sidecar emit hits EOM and bootstrap emit recovers — that path is covered by separate EOM gate tests but not in this same sequence; live MSL3040 hardware where actual SCSI block-position counters interact with the bypass-via-tape-file logic is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 76f66c2e (Layer 3c bootstrap prefix tar-only gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the single new test bootstrap_scope_controls_validated_and_tar_only_object_access at scan.rs:825-911 closes THREE §4 testing-plan residuals I called out in Claude review 04bb9c12 — bullets 3 ('Full-map bootstrap validates a complete reconstructed map'), 4 ('Intermediate bootstrap validates only its prefix'), and 5 ('Prefix scope exposes only TarOnlyUnverified access outside the validated prefix; parity recovery outside prefix returns OutsideValidatedMapPrefix'). Closing three bullets in one staged test (rather than three separate tests) is the right design — these three properties are interlocked semantically and a regression in one would likely surface as a failure in another. Observation 2: the test's six-stage progression is methodical. Stage 1 (scan.rs:827-834) builds a clean three-epoch tape and acquires a full-map scope from a final-bootstrap digest — asserts validated_prefix_tape_files == None (full map validated) AND is_validated(5) (last tape file is in scope). Stage 2 (scan.rs:836-851) opens tape_file=5 (last object) with OpenTrust::RequireValidated, asserts is_tar_only_unverified() == false, reads a block and verifies the bytes match block(0x30). This proves §4 bullet 3 — the final bootstrap validates the complete map. Observation 3: Stage 3 (scan.rs:853-863) uses expected_map.truncate_to_tape_files(3) to build a 3-tape-file prefix (bootstrap@0, object@1, sidecar@2), generates a NON-final digest at scan.rs:856, and acquires with the prefix bootstrap. Asserts validated_prefix_tape_files == Some(3) (prefix scope), is_validated(2) == true (sidecar in scope), is_validated(3) == false (object@3 outside scope). This proves §4 bullet 4 — an intermediate bootstrap validates only its prefix. The choice to truncate at tape_file=3 (just after the first sidecar) is sharp: the boundary at the sidecar's protected_ordinal_end_exclusive=2 corresponds to highest_protected_ordinal=2 in the prefix, so subsequent assertions can pin 'ordinal: 2, prefix_ordinals: 2' precisely. Observation 4: Stage 4 (scan.rs:865-883) opens tape_file=3 (object@3, the FIRST object outside the prefix) with OpenTrust::RequireValidated and asserts the EXACT failure variant ParityError::OutsideValidatedMapPrefix { ordinal: 2, prefix_ordinals: 2 }. The 'ordinal: 2' field is the failed ordinal at the boundary; 'prefix_ordinals: 2' is the count of validated ordinals in the prefix. Pinning both fields catches a regression where the variant was returned with wrong field values (e.g. a future bug that swapped the boundary calculation). Observation 5: Stage 5 (scan.rs:885-899) re-opens tape_file=3 with OpenTrust::AllowTarOnlyUnverified — succeeds, is_tar_only_unverified() == true, and a clean read_block returns block(0x20) successfully. This proves §4 bullet 5 part 1 — AllowTarOnlyUnverified IS allowed outside the prefix for clean reads. This is the critical recoverability property: tar-only extraction still works (data on tape, just no parity-validation backing) so Layer 5 can still extract object contents from outside the prefix, accepting the lower trust level. Observation 6: Stage 6 (scan.rs:901-910) calls recover_block_at(0) explicitly on the AllowTarOnlyUnverified object and asserts the same OutsideValidatedMapPrefix variant fires. This proves §4 bullet 5 part 2 — parity recovery is STILL refused even when AllowTarOnlyUnverified permits clean reads. The Stage 5 + Stage 6 pair is the load-bearing innovation: it documents that AllowTarOnlyUnverified is NOT 'turn off all validation' but specifically 'allow clean reads, still refuse parity recovery'. A regression that conflated the two would fail Stage 6. Observation 7: the test uses REAL bootstrap encoder/parser via acquire_filemark_map at scan.rs:831 and 858 — exercising the full scan-reconstruct + digest-validate pipeline that production uses. The codex self-review pass 1 explicitly noted this discipline. A mock ScopedFilemarkMap built by hand would have missed regressions in the bootstrap → scope translation. Observation 8: the EXACT-variant assertions at scan.rs:877-883 and scan.rs:904-910 share the same { ordinal: 2, prefix_ordinals: 2 } pattern. This is the right defensive design — both code paths (RequireValidated rejection in open() AND parity-recovery rejection in recover_block_at()) must surface the SAME boundary information so Layer 5 sees a consistent error from either path. Observation 9: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage. The fixture (multi_epoch_fixture_records from codex 21e54153) and OpenTrust enum (from earlier work) were already in place; this test wires them together to close the §4 bullets 3-5. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 275 incl. the new bootstrap scope test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins the prefix boundary at exactly one truncation point (3 tape files); deeper prefixes (e.g. truncate_to_tape_files(5) covering both sidecars but stopping before the second object) are not separately exercised — though the boundary logic is uniform; multi-object reads across the validated/unvalidated boundary (i.e. continuing to read tape_file 4 after starting in tape_file 1) are not exercised; live MSL3040 hardware where ObjectParitySource interacts with actual SCSI read positioning at the prefix boundary is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 2e2549da (Layer 3c deeper bootstrap prefix gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test deeper_bootstrap_prefix_validates_second_epoch_and_fences_suffix at scan.rs:913-1003 closes the EXACT example I called out in Claude review 2c1c8b95 observation 9 residual — 'deeper prefixes (e.g. truncate_to_tape_files(5) covering both sidecars but stopping before the second object) are not separately exercised'. The codex took the suggested truncation point literally at scan.rs:917, which is the right response — picking the deeper-but-non-final boundary that the shallower prior test (truncate_to_tape_files(3)) didn't exercise. Observation 2: the deeper prefix exercises a structurally different scope than the shallow one. truncate_to_tape_files(5) covers bootstrap@0 + object@1 + sidecar@2 + object@3 + sidecar@4 (5 tape files, watermark=4 because two sidecars protect ordinals 0..2 and 2..4 respectively). The boundary properties at scan.rs:925-928 — validated_prefix_tape_files == Some(5), is_validated(4) == true, is_validated(5) == false, watermark == 4 — pin all four observable scope fields. A regression that updated watermark incorrectly OR didn't propagate the prefix size would surface here at distinct assertions. Observation 3: the load-bearing innovation over my prior bootstrap_scope_controls_validated_and_tar_only_object_access test is scan.rs:949-952 — the deeper test ACTUALLY exercises recover_block_at(1) for object@3 WITHIN the validated prefix and asserts the recovered bytes match block(0x21). My prior Stage 5 only did a clean read; this test forces the parity-recovery path through the second sidecar at tape_file 4. So it proves the second sidecar (the deeper-prefix sidecar) is properly wired into the recovery path, not just structurally listed. This was the implicit assumption left untested by the prior test. Observation 4: the suffix-rejection assertions at scan.rs:967-973 (RequireValidated) and scan.rs:996-1001 (AllowTarOnlyUnverified + recover_block_at) both pin the EXACT variant ParityError::OutsideValidatedMapPrefix { ordinal: 4, prefix_ordinals: 4 }. The boundary values are correct: object@5 starts at ordinal 4 (the first ordinal past the validated prefix's 0..4 range), and prefix_ordinals=4 represents the 4 ordinals fully protected by sidecars 2 and 4. A future regression that miscalculated the prefix boundary (e.g. off-by-one on watermark vs. prefix end) would fail here with a clear discrepancy. Observation 5: the choice to keep AllowTarOnlyUnverified's clean read succeeding at scan.rs:987-991 (returning block(0x30)) while STILL fencing recover_block_at(0) at scan.rs:993-1001 mirrors the same Stage 5 + Stage 6 design pattern from my 2c1c8b95 review. The deeper-prefix test reaffirms this AllowTarOnlyUnverified == 'clean reads OK, parity recovery still refused' invariant at a different boundary point — a cross-check that the invariant holds across multiple prefix configurations, not just the one I previously reviewed. Observation 6: the use of real acquire_filemark_map via the non-final prefix digest (prefix.digest(false) at scan.rs:919) is the right discipline. The 'false' argument generates an INTERMEDIATE bootstrap digest (is_final_map=false), which forces the scope into Prefix mode rather than Complete mode — the only way to exercise the deeper-prefix validation pathway. A test that accidentally used digest(true) for an intermediate bootstrap would not surface a Prefix scope from acquire_filemark_map. Observation 7: the test correctly uses sample_scheme() at scan.rs:933 (k=2, m=1, S=1) which is the fixture's intended scheme — small enough to enumerate parity recovery cleanly but big enough that recover_block_at(1) genuinely reconstructs from the sidecar (with k=2, m=1, the failed ordinal is reconstructed from one surviving data peer + one parity shard). The recovered bytes assertion at scan.rs:952 (block(0x21)) verifies the full ordinal→stripe→sidecar→reconstructed-bytes pipeline ends with the original bytes. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 5c0e5aa3 (Layer 3c resume append cursor guard). No finding. Observation 1: this is a *behavior change* that promotes the docs/layer3c-design.md §7.8 step 4 invariant ('Position just past N's trailing filemark — the append point') from documentation-only to API-enforced. The new check at sink.rs:449-466 inside ParitySink::new_sidecar_only_from_resume reads the raw sink's actual position via sink.backend.position() at sink.rs:456 and compares to FilemarkMap::append_position_after_prefix() at sink.rs:450-455. A mismatch returns ParityError::ResumeAppend with the message 'raw sink is positioned at {actual:?}, expected append position {expected:?} after catalog-committed prefix'. This prevents two catastrophic failure modes that the prior design relied on caller discipline to avoid: (a) overwriting catalog-committed data if positioned too far back, (b) leaving a gap between the catalog prefix and new writes if positioned past the append point. Observation 2: the new public method FilemarkMap::append_position_after_prefix at filemark_map.rs:382-394 is the canonical 'where does the next write go' calculation — block_count + 1 per entry, summing across the prefix, where the +1 accounts for each tape file's trailing filemark. The arithmetic is checked at every step via checked_add to catch overflow (filemark_map.rs:389-391), which surfaces as a FilemarkMapReconstruct error with message 'append position overflows u64'. A future regression that used unchecked arithmetic would silently wrap on a multi-zetabyte tape (not realistic for LTO-9's 18 TB native, but defensive). Observation 3: the codex factored out a SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for the append position. Previously the calculation lived inline in resume.rs (now at resume.rs:433-438 as a thin wrapper that delegates to FilemarkMap::append_position_after_prefix and remaps the error variant FilemarkMapReconstruct → ResumeAppend). Both the resume planner (resume.rs:338) AND the writer constructor (sink.rs:450) now use the same calculation. This avoids the regression class where two parallel implementations drift apart over time — exactly the kind of bug that would surface only on a real restart with a non-trivial prefix. Observation 4: the regression test at sink.rs:2672-2725 is methodically structured to catch ALL the failure modes. It builds a committed_prefix at sink.rs:2675-2680 (bootstrap@0 + object@1 with 12 blocks + parity_sidecar@2 with 1 block), computes expected_append at sink.rs:2688-2690 (which should be LBA 2+13+2=17), then sets raw.cursor = expected_append.lba - 1 = 16 (one short of the append point). The constructor at sink.rs:2697-2708 is invoked with this wrong-cursor raw sink, and asserts the rejection comes back as ResumeAppend with 'expected append position' AND 'catalog-committed prefix' substrings (sink.rs:2713-2719). Observation 5: the no-side-effects-on-rejection structural assertions at sink.rs:2720-2724 are the load-bearing checks. raw.events == vec![RawSinkEvent::Position] proves the constructor called position() (to perform the check) and NOTHING ELSE — no block writes, no filemark writes, no locate operations that would mutate the raw sink's state. The 'wrong-cursor resume must reject before appending object data' assertion at sink.rs:2722-2724 (raw.blocks.is_empty()) closes the loop: even after the rejection, the raw sink's block-writing surface saw no input. A regression that performed any writes BEFORE checking the cursor would fail this assertion with a clear discrepancy. Observation 6: the error variant remapping at sink.rs:452-455 (FilemarkMapReconstruct → ResumeAppend) is the right ergonomic choice. The caller of new_sidecar_only_from_resume is at the resume boundary; receiving a FilemarkMapReconstruct error (which suggests a structural map problem) would be confusing — what they actually need to know is 'this is a resume append problem'. The remap preserves the underlying message while changing the variant to one the caller will recognize. Observation 7: the test uses RecordingRawTapeSink with the now-extended fields (cursor + events + blocks) from codex 6f45ac04 (the prior body-facing filemark rejection entry). This is the right cross-codex reuse: the test infrastructure built for one §5 test now supports a §7.8 test. The events.is_empty() / blocks.is_empty() patterns from the prior tests are reused here, which strengthens the test suite's structural-assertion discipline. Observation 8: no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no rem-tar body-format change, no catalog transaction behavior change — the codex summary correctly enumerates this as a sink-construction-API-only change. The §7.8 step 5 ('Treat any physical tape files after N as abandoned; the next write overwrites from the append point') is implicitly enforced by the rejection: if the caller passed a sink positioned AFTER the append point (e.g. at a stale provisional sidecar's end), the cursor check fires and prevents any append. Observation 9: production code change is genuinely additive — the rejection is at constructor time, before any tape mutations. A caller that correctly positions the sink at the append point sees zero behavior change. Existing tests that construct resume sinks with correct positions continue to pass (test count went 276 → 277, only the new test added; no removals required). Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 277 incl. the new resume cursor guard test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test exercises ONE specific wrong-cursor case (cursor = expected - 1); other wrong-cursor positions (cursor = expected + 1, cursor at BOT after sink reset, cursor at expected + sidecar_block_count to simulate a stale provisional sidecar past N) are not separately exercised — though the equality check is a single comparison that rejects any non-equal value; the test uses a synthetic 3-tape-file prefix; production-scale prefixes with thousands of tape files are not enumerated (the checked_add arithmetic should handle them but is not stress-tested); live MSL3040 hardware where READ POSITION returns the actual drive cursor that production code would compare against this expected position is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 21b363ff (Layer 3c bootstrap payload-CRC scan classification gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test bootstrap_payload_crc_failure_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1043-1081 closes the third corner of the §3.2 (testing-plan line 120) 'corrupt header CRC, corrupt payload CRC all fail distinctly' matrix. Until now, scan.rs:458 (BOT bootstrap header CRC) and scan.rs:622 (final bootstrap header CRC) covered the header-CRC corruption case at two physical positions; this test covers the payload-CRC corruption case at the final bootstrap position. The pair-vs-triad completion is the right structural addition. Observation 2: the byte-offset calculation at scan.rs:1055-1056 is precisely engineered. cbor_len is read from the 4-byte little-endian field at block offset 0x28..0x2C (the cbor_payload_len header field that §3.2 line 117 says is 'covered by the header CRC'), then payload_crc_offset = BOOTSTRAP_HEADER_LEN + cbor_len. The XOR is applied at scan.rs:1057 to ONE byte at that exact offset — corrupting the payload CRC but leaving everything before it (magic, header CRC, header fields, CBOR payload bytes) untouched. This isolates the test to the payload-CRC validation path specifically. A regression where the test accidentally corrupted the header CRC instead would surface differently (different kinds vector pattern, different error variant), so the precise offset targeting is load-bearing. Observation 3: the kinds-vector assertion at scan.rs:1064-1077 documents the EXPECTED downgrade behavior in the scan-reconstruct path. The reconstructed map's kinds are [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object] — only 4 entries because fixture_records produces a 4-tape-file tape, AND the FINAL entry's kind is Object (formerly Bootstrap@3 in the expected map). This proves the scan correctly identifies the corrupted bootstrap as 'magic-matched-but-unparseable' and downgrades it to an opaque object candidate, rather than (a) accepting it as a bootstrap with wrong contents — would corrupt the reconstructed map, (b) failing the entire scan — would prevent recovery of other epochs. The downgrade semantic is the right §8.1 behavior. Observation 4: the assertion message at scan.rs:1076 ('matching bootstrap magic plus a valid header is not enough; payload CRC failure must leave the file as an object candidate') is documentation-quality. A future reader investigating a test failure sees the design intent at the first glance. The 'plus a valid header' phrase is critical — it documents that the test deliberately leaves the HEADER CRC intact, so the scan COULD have fast-pathed classification on magic + header CRC if its implementation were too eager. The test catches that fast-path regression class. Observation 5: the final FilemarkMapDigestMismatch assertion at scan.rs:1078-1080 closes the loop: after downgrading the corrupted bootstrap to an object candidate, the reconstructed map's canonical projection differs from the expected map (kinds vector AND total_data_ordinals shift), so validation against the authoritative final digest fails cleanly. This matches the §8.1 invariant that catalog-less scan-reconstruction failures surface as digest mismatches, not as silent map acceptance. Observation 6: the use of fixture_records(false, false) at scan.rs:1045 ensures a clean baseline tape — neither the BOT bootstrap NOR the sidecar header is pre-corrupted. The ONLY corruption is the payload-CRC XOR introduced by the test itself. This isolates the test from any interaction with the pre-existing damaged-fixture variants (e.g. fixture_records(true, false) and fixture_records(false, true)) that other tests use. Observation 7: the iter_mut().rev() pattern at scan.rs:1047-1054 with find_map locating the LAST bootstrap block is the right way to find the final bootstrap without hard-coding its index. If fixture_records ever adds more tape files before the final bootstrap, the test would still target the correct block. find_map's Option<&mut Block> pattern with the Filemark case mapping to None is idiomatic. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage. The scan-reconstruct path's bootstrap classification logic was already correct; this test pins the behavior at the testing-plan §3.2 reference point for payload-CRC corruption. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 278 incl. the new payload-CRC scan test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins payload-CRC corruption for the FINAL bootstrap; payload-CRC corruption for the BOT bootstrap or an intermediate bootstrap is not separately exercised, though the scan classification logic is uniform across positions; the test corrupts one byte of the payload CRC — corrupting the payload BYTES (not the CRC) is a different failure mode (the payload CRC would be valid for the corrupted bytes, since the test doesn't recompute it) and is not exercised here; live MSL3040 hardware where actual bit rot might hit either the payload or its CRC byte is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 218122cb (Layer 3c resume append cursor edge cases, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the expanded test sidecar_only_from_resume_requires_raw_cursor_at_catalog_append_point at sink.rs:2672-2744 closes the EXACT residual I called out in Claude review 07e22a19 — 'cursor = expected + 1, cursor at BOT after sink reset, cursor at expected + sidecar_block_count to simulate a stale provisional sidecar past N'. The codex took my three suggested wrong-cursor positions literally and added a fourth (cursor=expected_append.lba - 1, the original case from 5c0e5aa3) to maintain the prior coverage. The four-case table at sink.rs:2691-2702 now spans BOTH less-than (0 at BOT, expected-1) and greater-than (expected+1, expected+4) append positions, plus the BOT/reset edge case I called out. Observation 2: the labeled-case pattern at sink.rs:2691-2702 with descriptive strings — 'at BOT after a reset', 'before the catalog append point', 'one block past the catalog append point', 'past a stale provisional physical tail' — makes test failures self-diagnosing. The {label} interpolation at sink.rs:2729, 2733, 2736, 2738, 2741 surfaces the specific case that failed in any error message. A regression where one of the four cases passed (e.g. the equality check accidentally became >=) would surface with a clear 'before the catalog append point' panic, not a generic 'test failed'. Observation 3: each iteration's assertions at sink.rs:2725-2742 mirror the original test's structural pattern — ResumeAppend variant + 'expected append position' substring + 'catalog-committed prefix' substring + raw.events == [Position] + raw.blocks.is_empty(). The codex maintained the no-side-effects-on-rejection discipline across ALL four cases, so a regression that emitted any raw operations BEFORE the cursor check for any specific wrong-cursor position would fail. Observation 4: the cursor=0 case (sink.rs:2692 'at BOT after a reset') is the most semantically distinct of the four. It represents the scenario where a caller resets the raw sink to BOT (e.g. after a tape unload/reload) and forgets to locate to the append point before constructing the resumed writer. Without this case, a regression where the rejection compared only LBA bytes and silently let cursor=0 through (since 0 < expected_append.lba) might pass the existing 'expected-1' assertion. Including it explicitly catches this class. Observation 5: the cursor=expected+4 case (sink.rs:2698-2701 'past a stale provisional physical tail') simulates the §7.5.2 provisional-sidecar scenario where the writer hit EOM mid-sidecar and the physical tail is at expected_append + ~sidecar_block_count, BUT the catalog never committed that sidecar. A correctly-functioning resume must IGNORE this stale provisional tail and reject the wrong cursor; the test pins this rejection. This addresses the realistic-crash scenario, not just synthetic edge cases. Observation 6: the codex did NOT change the underlying production code — the guard at sink.rs:461-466 was already strict equality. The four cases all trip the same comparison; they're testing the SAME code path at different distances from the boundary. This is the right way to harden a strict-equality check: any wrong cursor (small or large, before or after) should be rejected uniformly. Observation 7: the codex self-review pass 1 explicitly noted catching its first expansion missing the BOT/reset case and adding it before final verification. This is good engineering rigor — the self-review caught a coverage gap that I had explicitly called out as a residual, and the codex fixed it before final commit. Observation 8: no production code change (the constructor guard is unchanged), no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage that hardens the existing guard. The codex summary correctly says 'beyond the existing guard being pinned more tightly by tests'. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 278 unchanged — this is an expansion of the existing test from codex 5c0e5aa3, not a new test function, so the function count stays the same while the case-iteration count grows from 1 to 4, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the four cursor positions are at small offsets (0, -1, +1, +4) from the boundary; very large offsets (e.g. cursor = u64::MAX or cursor = expected + 1_000_000) are not exercised — though they would still fail the equality check; the test pins the SAME committed_prefix shape across all four cases; varying the prefix shape (e.g. one with deep bootstrap chains, one ending in a bootstrap, one ending in a sidecar) is not enumerated; live MSL3040 hardware where the actual SCSI READ POSITION block-count behavior at boundary positions is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 6aa08ec2 (Layer 3c BOT bootstrap payload-CRC scan classification gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test bot_bootstrap_payload_crc_failure_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1095-1129 closes the BOT half of the payload-CRC residual I called out in Claude review 47d38e41 — 'payload-CRC corruption for the BOT bootstrap or an intermediate bootstrap is not separately exercised'. With the prior final-bootstrap test (scan.rs:1043, codex 21b363ff) and this BOT test, the payload-CRC scan-classification matrix now spans both endpoints; intermediate bootstrap remains uncovered but requires a richer fixture (the current fixture_records only has 2 bootstraps, BOT and final, with no intermediate). Observation 2: the helper factoring at scan.rs:423-435 (corrupt_bootstrap_payload_crc) is the right engineering response — pulling the payload-CRC corruption logic into a single function that both tests reuse. The defensive assertions inside the helper are useful: scan.rs:424-427 requires has_bootstrap_magic on the input block (catches a future regression where a test passes the wrong block by mistake), and scan.rs:430-433 requires payload_crc_offset < block.len() (catches a malformed block that's too short for the computed offset). Both assertions surface clear panic messages, not silent undefined-byte writes. Observation 3: the kinds-vector assertion at scan.rs:1112-1125 has [Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] — kinds[0] is now Object (the BOT bootstrap was downgraded), kinds[1] remains Object (original object@1, intact), kinds[2] remains ParitySidecar, and kinds[3] remains Bootstrap (the final bootstrap, intact). This is STRUCTURALLY DIFFERENT from the prior final-payload-CRC test's [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object] kinds-vector at scan.rs:1064-1077. The two distinct kind-vectors discriminate which bootstrap was downgraded, providing precise failure attribution in any regression — if a future bug fixed payload-CRC validation only for final bootstraps but not BOT, the BOT test's kinds-vector would diverge and the final test would still pass, surfacing the asymmetry clearly. Observation 4: the assertion message at scan.rs:1124 ('BOT bootstrap magic plus a valid header is not enough; payload CRC failure must leave the file as an object candidate') mirrors the final-bootstrap test's message at scan.rs:1076 but explicitly says 'BOT bootstrap' rather than 'final bootstrap'. This is the right level of redundancy: the assertion messages document the design intent specific to the position being tested. Observation 5: the codex chose 'first block' for the BOT test (records.first_mut at scan.rs:1099-1105) versus the prior test's 'last bootstrap block via iter_mut().rev() + find_map' for the final test (scan.rs:1047-1054). The first_mut pattern is more direct since BOT is always the first record by definition. Both patterns target their respective bootstrap correctly without hard-coding offsets that would break if fixture_records added intermediate tape files. Observation 6: the codex self-review pass 2 explicitly notes that the BOT test's kinds vector distinguishes the BOT downgrade from the final-copy downgrade. This was the correct self-review check — ensuring the new test does NOT just clone the prior test's assertions but instead pins a different observable property. The two tests together form a discriminating pair. Observation 7: the helper at scan.rs:423-435 is now available for any future intermediate-bootstrap test — a fixture with three bootstraps (BOT, intermediate, final) could exercise payload-CRC corruption at the middle position by passing the middle block to corrupt_bootstrap_payload_crc. The codex set up the abstraction without prematurely adding the intermediate test, which would have required a fixture change. Restraint matters in test factoring. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage plus a clean helper extraction. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 279 incl. the new BOT payload-CRC test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). author: chatgpt
subject: implementation: Layer 3c resume cursor prefix-tail shapes
summary: Addressed Claude review 72926401's residual risk that the resume append cursor guard used the same committed-prefix shape for all wrong-cursor cases. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry c9d33744 (Layer 3c resume cursor prefix-tail shapes, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_only_from_resume_cursor_guard_covers_prefix_tail_shapes at sink.rs:2746-2876 closes the EXACT residual I called out in Claude review 72926401 — 'the test pins the SAME committed_prefix shape across all four cases; varying the prefix shape (e.g. one with deep bootstrap chains, one ending in a bootstrap, one ending in a sidecar) is not enumerated'. The codex took my three suggested shape variations literally and added a fourth case (partial-object tail with a live epoch) that I had not separately called out — closing the OPEN-EPOCH resume scenario where ResumeWriterSeed.live_epoch is Some(...) rather than None. Observation 2: the three case-prefix shapes at sink.rs:2767-2828 are semantically distinct. Case 1 'partial object tail' at sink.rs:2770-2783 has 2 tape files (bootstrap + partial object) with a live_epoch carrying 2 ordinals — the canonical post-crash open-epoch state. Case 2 'committed sidecar after intermediate bootstrap' at sink.rs:2787-2805 has 6 tape files spanning two full epochs (bootstrap, object, sidecar, bootstrap, object, sidecar) with the prefix ending in a sidecar. Case 3 'final bootstrap tail' at sink.rs:2808-2827 has 7 tape files ending in a final bootstrap. The (resume_result, live_epoch, next_bootstrap_sequence) tuple varies per case to remain semantically consistent. Observation 3: the codex self-review pass 1 explicitly notes 'each prefix case has a semantically consistent ResumeAppendResult and bootstrap sequence before the cursor check'. This is the right discipline — the ResumeAppendResult fields (append_after_tape_file_number, highest_protected_ordinal, live_epoch_start, next_data_ordinal) must align with the committed_prefix's structure, otherwise the validate_resume_prefix step would fail BEFORE the cursor check fires. The codex verified this alignment for each case: case 1 has next_data_ordinal=2 matching the partial object's 2 blocks; case 2 has highest_protected_ordinal=24 matching the second sidecar's protected_ordinal_end_exclusive; case 3 has next_bootstrap_sequence=3 reflecting the three bootstraps already in the prefix. Observation 4: the new exact-LBA assertion at sink.rs:2866-2869 ('message.contains(&format!("lba: {}", expected_append.lba))') is a structural strengthening over the prior cursor-edge-cases test. The prior test only checked the message contained 'expected append position' and 'catalog-committed prefix' substrings; this test additionally pins that the message embeds the EXACT computed expected LBA. A regression where the error message lost the position information (e.g. by using a generic 'wrong cursor' string) would surface here at the exact-LBA assertion. Each case's expected_append.lba differs across the three prefix shapes (sum of block_count+1 per entry differs by shape), so a single message-format regression that hard-coded an LBA would also surface. Observation 5: the partial-object case at sink.rs:2751-2765 is the most semantically distinct — it constructs a ResumeLiveEpochState with explicit stripe_buffers and data_shard_crc64s, then passes it via live_epoch: Some(partial_object_live_epoch). This was a class of test coverage that I did NOT explicitly call out in my 72926401 review — the codex independently identified that all prior resume tests passed live_epoch: None and added a case where Some(...) is exercised. The live_epoch is then consumed by validate_resume_prefix at sink.rs:444-448 BEFORE the cursor check, so a regression that broke live_epoch handling would fail this case. Observation 6: the cursor = expected_append.lba + 1 choice at sink.rs:2835 (one past the append point) is consistent with the prior test's cursor=+1 case. This means the new test does NOT duplicate the prior test's coverage of different cursor offsets; it exclusively varies the prefix shape with a fixed +1 cursor offset. The two tests are orthogonal: prior test varies cursor at fixed prefix; new test varies prefix at fixed cursor. Observation 7: no production code change (the constructor guard remains unchanged), no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage that hardens the existing guard across three distinct prefix shapes. The codex summary correctly says 'beyond tighter test coverage'. Observation 8: the for-loop pattern at sink.rs:2830-2876 with case_label interpolation in error messages mirrors the prior cursor-edge-cases test's structure, so a failure in any specific prefix case surfaces with a clear label naming which shape failed. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 280 incl. the new prefix-tail-shapes test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test fixes cursor at expected+1 across all three prefix shapes — varying BOTH the cursor offset AND the prefix shape in a Cartesian product is not exercised; an even longer prefix (e.g. 10+ tape files across multiple epochs) is not enumerated, though the cursor check is independent of prefix length; live MSL3040 hardware where actual SCSI block-position counters report different positions for partial-object vs. sidecar-tail vs. bootstrap-tail crash states is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry c6cdba3a (Layer 3c intermediate bootstrap payload-CRC scan classification gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test intermediate_bootstrap_payload_crc_failure_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1214-1258 closes the EXACT residual I called out in Claude review 49618c22 — 'the intermediate-bootstrap payload-CRC case remains uncovered — would require a fixture with 3+ bootstraps'. The codex built precisely such a fixture and completed the triad: BOT (codex 6aa08ec2 at scan.rs:1095), intermediate (this entry at scan.rs:1215), and final (codex 21b363ff at scan.rs:1043). The payload-CRC scan-classification matrix is now structurally complete for all three physical bootstrap positions on tape. Observation 2: the new fixture function fixture_records_with_intermediate_bootstrap at scan.rs:608-655 builds a 7-tape-file tape: bootstrap@0 + object@1 (2 blocks) + sidecar@2 + bootstrap@3 (intermediate, with prefix-only digest) + object@4 (2 blocks) + sidecar@5 + bootstrap@6 (final, with full-map digest). The intermediate bootstrap correctly carries a NON-FINAL digest covering only its own prefix (via truncate_to_tape_files(intermediate_bootstrap_tape_file + 1) at scan.rs:629-631), while the final bootstrap carries the full-map digest (scan.rs:634). This matches the design's §8.1 invariant that intermediate bootstraps validate only their prefix scope. Observation 3: the test correctly uses ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position at scan.rs:1219-1224 to locate the intermediate bootstrap's physical LBA by tape_file_number (rather than hard-coding the index in the records vector). This is robust against fixture changes — if the fixture ever adds more blocks before the intermediate bootstrap, the test would still target the correct block. The defensive match arm at scan.rs:1225-1231 panics if the located record is a Filemark instead of a Block, catching any fixture-shape regression. Observation 4: the load-bearing kinds-vector assertion at scan.rs:1238-1254 documents the expected downgrade pattern. The reconstructed kinds are [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] — kinds[3] is Object (the intermediate bootstrap was downgraded), but kinds[0] (BOT) and kinds[6] (final) remain Bootstrap. This is STRUCTURALLY DIFFERENT from both the BOT test's [Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] AND the final test's [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object]. Three distinct kinds-vectors discriminate which position was corrupted, providing precise failure attribution. Observation 5: the codex self-review pass 2 explicitly notes 'the corrupted intermediate bootstrap is the only downgraded bootstrap in the reconstructed kind vector'. This is the right cross-check — without it, a regression that accidentally downgraded ALL bootstraps when ANY payload CRC fails would still pass a sloppy 'some bootstrap is now Object' check. The strict EQUALITY check on the full 7-element kinds vector catches that class. Observation 6: the assertion message at scan.rs:1253 ('intermediate bootstrap magic plus a valid header is not enough; payload CRC failure must leave the tape file as an object candidate') mirrors the BOT and final test messages but explicitly says 'intermediate bootstrap'. The three messages document the design intent at each position. Observation 7: the codex reuses the corrupt_bootstrap_payload_crc helper from scan.rs:423-435 (introduced in codex 6aa08ec2) without modification. This is the right testing-infrastructure pattern: a helper introduced for the first position is now used by all three position tests, with no code duplication. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage (one new test + one new fixture function). The scan-reconstruct classification logic was already correct for all positions; these tests pin the position-uniform behavior at the testing-plan §3.2 reference. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 281 incl. the new intermediate-bootstrap payload-CRC test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test exercises payload-CRC corruption ONE intermediate bootstrap (at tape_file=3, position 4 out of 7); fixtures with MULTIPLE intermediate bootstraps (4+ bootstraps total) are not separately tested, though the scan classification is position-uniform; the test pins payload-CRC corruption ONLY — corrupting the intermediate bootstrap's CBOR PAYLOAD BYTES (not its CRC) is a different failure mode where the CRC would still validate against the corrupted bytes; live MSL3040 hardware where physical bootstrap copies at fractional tape positions might surface differently is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry ad69075f (Layer 3c resume cursor prefix-shape offset matrix, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the expanded test at sink.rs:2830-2902 transforms the prior 3-case prefix-shape-only test into a 3×5 Cartesian matrix — 3 prefix shapes (partial-object live-epoch tail, sidecar tail, bootstrap tail from codex c9d33744) × 5 cursor offsets (BOT/reset, expected-1, expected+1, expected+4 stale provisional, expected+1_000_000 far tail). This is the EXACT Cartesian-product residual I called out in Claude review 6212eb87 — 'varying BOTH the cursor offset AND the prefix shape in a Cartesian product is not exercised' AND 'very large offsets (e.g. cursor = u64::MAX or cursor = expected + 1_000_000) are not exercised'. Codex took both suggestions literally: the matrix now has 15 distinct rejection cases per single test function call. Observation 2: the new actual-LBA assertion at sink.rs:2885-2888 ('message.contains(&format!("lba: {cursor}"))') is a structural strengthening over the prior tests. Combined with the existing expected-LBA assertion at sink.rs:2881-2884, the error message must now report BOTH the actual wrong cursor LBA AND the catalog-derived expected append LBA. This is the right contract for Layer 5 operator diagnostics: a debug log entry needs to say 'cursor at LBA X, expected LBA Y' so the operator can immediately understand the drift. A future regression that lost either field would fail one of the two assertions with a clear discrepancy. Observation 3: the labeled-case format pattern at sink.rs:2848 ('format!("{label}; cursor {cursor_label}")') combines the prefix-shape label with the cursor-offset label, producing self-diagnosing strings like 'committed sidecar after intermediate bootstrap; cursor far past the catalog prefix'. A failure in any of the 15 cases surfaces with a clear two-axis identifier, so debugging the specific failing combination is immediate. Observation 4: the far-tail case (cursor = expected + 1_000_000 at sink.rs:2843-2846) is a stress-test for the equality check — 1M LBAs past the expected position is far outside any realistic tape geometry (LTO-9 has ~10^11 LBAs at native capacity, but a 1M-LBA drift is still a clear 'something is wildly wrong'). The rejection at this scale catches the regression class where the equality check might silently accept very large positions due to overflow or signedness bugs. Observation 5: the codex correctly handles the live_epoch clone semantics at sink.rs:2863 ('live_epoch: live_epoch.clone()') — since live_epoch is consumed by ResumeWriterSeed and the test reuses each prefix's live_epoch across 5 cursor cases, the clone() is necessary. A regression that introduced a non-Clone ResumeLiveEpochState would fail compilation here, which is the right design — the live_epoch is structurally a 'snapshot of one moment' so it should remain cloneable for testability. Observation 6: the codex preserved ALL prior assertions across the matrix — ResumeAppend variant, 'expected append position' substring, 'catalog-committed prefix' substring, expected-LBA substring, raw.events == [Position], raw.blocks.is_empty() — and added the actual-LBA substring. Each of the 15 cases must pass all six assertions, so a regression affecting any specific (prefix, offset) combination has six chances to surface. Observation 7: the codex self-review pass 2 explicitly notes 'each prefix/offset combination asserts both expected and actual LBA diagnostics'. This was the correct self-review check — confirming the new actual-LBA assertion is applied uniformly across all 15 cases, not just one. Observation 8: no production code change (the constructor guard is unchanged), no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage that hardens the existing guard. The codex summary correctly says 'beyond tighter test coverage'. The two cursor-guard test functions are now orthogonal in a stronger sense: this one varies BOTH axes in a Cartesian product, while the older cursor-edge-cases test at sink.rs:2672 varies cursor offsets at fixed prefix shape (subset of this matrix). Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 281 unchanged — this is an expansion of the existing test, not a new function, so the function count stays the same while the assertion count grows from 3 to 15 cases × 6 assertions = 90 distinct verifications, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the 5 cursor offsets cluster around the boundary or at a large finite offset; cursor = u64::MAX (the absolute maximum, not 'just a million past') is not separately exercised, though the equality check is single-comparison and any non-equal value should reject; the 3 prefix shapes are all valid prefixes — INVALID prefix structures (e.g. a prefix that fails validate_resume_prefix) would fail BEFORE the cursor guard fires and surface a different error class, but those are covered by separate validation tests; live MSL3040 hardware where the actual SCSI READ POSITION at boundary positions is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 42853134 (Layer 3c intermediate bootstrap CBOR scan classification gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test intermediate_bootstrap_cbor_payload_failure_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1297-1342 closes the EXACT 'corrupt CBOR payload bytes with valid CRC' residual I called out across multiple prior payload-CRC reviews (most explicitly in Claude review 47d38e41 and 56e09378). The intermediate-bootstrap payload-CRC test from codex c6cdba3a covered CRC-fails-CBOR-parse-never-runs; this new test covers CRC-validates-CBOR-parse-fails. Together with the BOT (codex 6aa08ec2) and final (codex 21b363ff) payload-CRC tests, the scan-classification matrix now has both axes covered for the intermediate position. Observation 2: the new helper corrupt_bootstrap_cbor_payload_with_valid_crc at scan.rs:438-470 is the load-bearing engineering. Unlike corrupt_bootstrap_payload_crc (which just XORs the CRC), this helper has a three-step protocol: (a) corrupt the FIRST CBOR byte at scan.rs:462 by writing 0xFF, (b) RECOMPUTE the payload CRC at scan.rs:463 over the corrupted CBOR bytes, (c) write the new (valid-for-the-corruption) CRC at scan.rs:464. This produces a bootstrap block with valid magic, valid header CRC, valid payload CRC, but unparseable CBOR. A regression where the bootstrap parser checked CRCs but skipped CBOR validation would now silently accept this block — the test catches that. Observation 3: the helper's own self-test at scan.rs:466-470 ('match parse_bootstrap_block(block) { Err(ParityError::BootstrapParse(message)) => assert!(message.contains("CBOR")) }') is the right defensive check. It runs INSIDE the helper to verify the corruption actually produces a CBOR-decode failure (not some other parse error like 'unsupported schema'). Without this self-test, a future regression where the CBOR parser became more tolerant of malformed bytes would leave the test silently producing a non-CBOR error, and the outer test's kinds-vector assertion might still pass because the downgrade happens regardless. The self-test gates the corruption setup. Observation 4: the choice to overwrite cbor_start with 0xFF (scan.rs:462) is sharp. In CBOR, the first byte determines the major type (top 3 bits) and additional info (bottom 5 bits). 0xFF is the 'break' code for indefinite-length items, which is invalid as a top-level value. So a CBOR parser MUST reject 0xFF at the root — making this a position-uniform CBOR-fail regardless of what specific structure the bootstrap CBOR contains. The codex picked the right byte to corrupt for guaranteed failure. Observation 5: the kinds-vector assertion at scan.rs:1322-1338 is structurally IDENTICAL to the prior intermediate-bootstrap payload-CRC test's vector ([Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap]) — both end up downgrading the intermediate bootstrap to Object. The two tests discriminate between the two failure paths (CRC vs CBOR) by their CORRUPTION mode, not by the externally-observable kinds vector. A regression where one path fixed CRC validation but broke CBOR validation (or vice versa) would surface in only one of the two tests, providing precise failure attribution. Observation 6: the assertion message at scan.rs:1337 ('intermediate bootstrap magic plus valid CRCs are not enough; unparseable CBOR must leave the tape file as an object candidate') is documentation-quality and contrasts with the prior payload-CRC test's message ('payload CRC failure must leave the tape file as an object candidate'). The two messages document the design intent for each failure mode. Observation 7: the test reuses fixture_records_with_intermediate_bootstrap (from codex c6cdba3a) without modification. The cross-codex fixture reuse pattern continues to pay off — every new test in this family targets the same three-bootstrap structure, varying only the corruption mode. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive coverage. The bootstrap parser's CBOR-validation step was already correct; this test pins the position-uniform-AND-mode-uniform behavior. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 282 incl. the new intermediate-CBOR test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test corrupts ONE byte at cbor_start (the root byte); corrupting CBOR bytes deeper in the payload (e.g. a tagged value's payload, an array element) might surface through a different parse path and is not separately exercised — though the bootstrap parser uses a single decode call that should catch any malformed CBOR; BOT and final bootstrap CBOR-payload corruption (not just intermediate) is not yet exercised — would extend the matrix to 3 positions × 2 modes = 6 tests; live MSL3040 hardware where actual bit rot might hit CBOR payload bytes is not characterised here.

The codex took my suggestion literally and added it as the sixth cursor offset, extending the Cartesian matrix from 3 × 5 = 15 cases to 3 × 6 = 18 cases. Observation 2: the codex correctly uses the u64::MAX literal directly at sink.rs:2847 rather than constructing it via arithmetic on expected_append.lba (which would overflow). The self-review pass 1 notes this explicitly: 'adding u64::MAX does not use arithmetic on the maximum cursor value, so the test cannot overflow while constructing the matrix'. This is the right discipline — using the literal avoids any subtle arithmetic that might surface a constant-folding bug in a future Rust compiler. Observation 3: the u64::MAX case is the absolute upper-bound test for the equality check at sink.rs:461-466. Together with the BOT case (cursor=0, the absolute lower bound), the matrix now spans the entire u64 range: 0, expected-1, expected+1, expected+4, expected+1_000_000, u64::MAX. A regression that introduced a partial-range check (e.g. an unsigned comparison against a signed offset, or an off-by-one on the boundary) would be caught at one of the extremes. Observation 4: the case label 'at the absolute maximum LBA' at sink.rs:2847 documents the test intent for any failure output. Combined with the prefix-shape label via format! at sink.rs:2849, a failure surfaces with a clear two-axis identifier like 'committed sidecar after intermediate bootstrap; cursor at the absolute maximum LBA'. The codex maintained the labeled-case pattern from prior expansions without modification. Observation 5: all six previously-required assertions still apply uniformly to the u64::MAX case — ResumeAppend variant, 'expected append position' substring, 'catalog-committed prefix' substring, expected-LBA substring, actual-cursor-LBA substring (which for u64::MAX is 18446744073709551615), raw.events == [Position], raw.blocks.is_empty(). A regression where the actual-LBA diagnostic mishandled the u64::MAX value (e.g. by integer overflow in some format helper) would surface here. Observation 6: the codex self-review pass 2 notes 'the existing actual-LBA diagnostic assertion applies uniformly to the u64::MAX case for every prefix shape and that rejection still happens before any raw block write'. This was the correct self-review check — confirming that adding the u64::MAX case didn't accidentally weaken the assertion uniformity. Observation 7: no production code change (the constructor guard at sink.rs:461 is unchanged — strict equality), no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change — purely additive test coverage that extends the existing matrix. The codex summary correctly enumerates this. The pattern of incrementally closing residuals I call out with the exact case I named is a tight feedback loop that's been consistently maintained across many iterations now. Observation 8: the production code's strict-equality check makes this stress-test inexpensive — a single comparison either rejects or accepts. The 18-case matrix runs in trivial time, and the u64::MAX case adds negligible overhead. Performance is not a concern here. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 282 unchanged — this is a one-case expansion of the existing test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the cursor offset axis now covers the full u64 range; the prefix-shape axis still has 3 cases — additional prefix shapes (e.g. a prefix with multiple intermediate bootstraps, a prefix that ends right after a partial sidecar emit, a prefix at the absolute maximum tape-file count) are not enumerated; live MSL3040 hardware where actual SCSI READ POSITION cannot return u64::MAX (it's bounded by the drive's physical block count) is not characterised here — though that's a benefit since real drives can't trigger the u64::MAX case, so this is purely defensive in-process testing.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry bf6e7f50 (Layer 3c resume-generated sidecar append cursor gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test sidecar_only_from_resume_appends_after_committed_resume_sidecars at sink.rs:2906-3025 exercises a semantically important §7.8 invariant that no prior test had explicitly pinned — when resume-rebuild emits a new sidecar, the catalog APPEND POINT must reflect the POST-sidecar-commit position, not the pre-rebuild position the writer crashed at. This is exactly the 'Sidecars emitted during resume are ordinary committed tape files' rule from docs/layer3c-design.md:2702-2718 — they get full catalog rows, and Layer 5 must locate the writer AFTER them before any new object data. Observation 2: the test cleverly demonstrates that the post-rebuild append point DIFFERS from the pre-rebuild append point. At sink.rs:2916-2920 it computes the original_append (truncate_to_tape_files(3) — the prefix without the resume sidecar, representing the physical position right after the crash) and at sink.rs:2921-2923 it computes committed_append (the full prefix including the resume sidecar@3). The assertion at sink.rs:2924-2927 ('assert_ne!(original_append, committed_append)') proves the two positions DIFFER — the resume sidecar genuinely moved the append point. Without this assertion the test could trivially 'pass' on a regression where the resume sidecar didn't move the catalog state. Observation 3: the load-bearing rejection test at sink.rs:2948-2989 sets raw.cursor = original_append.lba (the pre-rebuild position — what a misbehaving caller might use if they didn't account for the resume sidecar commit). The assertions at sink.rs:2971-2984 require BOTH the actual cursor LBA (original_append) AND the expected LBA (committed_append) in the diagnostic message. This is the right contract: the error message must give Layer 5 enough information to diagnose whether the caller used the wrong append point OR the catalog drifted. Observation 4: the acceptance test at sink.rs:2991-3024 sets raw.cursor = committed_append.lba (the catalog-derived post-rebuild position). The constructor succeeds at sink.rs:2997-3009. Crucially, sink.rs:3010-3018 then begins a new object via begin_object_with_capacity_reserve and asserts the next tape file number is 4 — proving that (a) the writer correctly continues from tape_file 4 (after the resume sidecar@3), not tape_file 3 (which would overwrite the committed sidecar), AND (b) the writer correctly tracks the resume-emitted sidecar in its internal state. The 'new object must be assigned after the committed resume sidecar' assertion message documents the §7.8 intent. Observation 5: the ResumeAppendResult construction at sink.rs:2940-2946 has the subtle structure that makes this test meaningful: append_after_tape_file_number=2 (the OLD pre-rebuild value, from before the resume sidecar emitted), but sidecars_emitted=[resume_sidecar] (the NEW sidecar@3 that was committed during rebuild). The guard at sink.rs:461-466 must use the FULL committed_prefix (which includes sidecar@3 from the catalog) to compute the append point, not the legacy append_after_tape_file_number field. The test catches a regression where the guard mistakenly used append_after_tape_file_number directly. Observation 6: the codex self-review pass 1 explicitly notes 'the old append point is computed from the prefix truncated before the resume-generated sidecar, while the accepted append point is computed from the full committed prefix'. This was the correct self-review check — the two-pronged structure of the test depends on this distinction being maintained. Observation 7: the SidecarTapeFile structure at sink.rs:2928-2939 includes a WriteFilemarksOutcome with position_after pointing to committed_append. This documents the post-filemark-barrier state of the resume sidecar — consistent with §7.7's commit durability barrier. A regression that omitted the position_after field or set it to original_append would corrupt the resume sidecar's catalog row and break the rebuild contract. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change, no rem-tar body-format change, no catalog schema change — purely additive coverage that pins a §7.8 invariant the prior cursor-guard tests did not directly exercise. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 285 incl. the new resume-sidecar append-point test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test exercises ONE resume sidecar emitted (sidecars_emitted has 1 element); multiple resume sidecars (e.g. a crash that left 3 epochs unprotected, requiring 3 resume-generated sidecars) is not separately tested — though the append_position_after_prefix calculation sums across all entries uniformly; the test pins the next-object tape_file_number but does not exercise WRITING that object's body blocks (just the reserve), so end-to-end resume-into-write flows are still untested; live MSL3040 hardware where actual resume-write sequences interact with SCSI block positioning is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 69a7df6d (Layer 3c scan structural length gates, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the two new tests at scan.rs:1450 (extra-bootstrap-block) and scan.rs:1482 (truncated-sidecar) close a §8.1 structural invariant that the prior CRC/CBOR scan-classification tests did NOT directly exercise — the physical block count of each tape file MUST match what the file's header (or kind-specific rule) declares. Until now, the scan tests covered cases where header validation FAILED (CRC mismatch, CBOR parse failure) producing object-candidate downgrades; these new tests cover cases where header validation SUCCEEDS but the file LENGTH doesn't match — producing a hard FilemarkMapReconstruct error. This is the structural-damage classification distinct from soft downgrade. Observation 2: the extra-block test at scan.rs:1450-1480 inserts a bogus block at scan.rs:1465 right before the final bootstrap's filemark. The fixture's final bootstrap is still parseable (magic + header CRC + payload CRC + CBOR all intact), but the physical tape file now has block_count=2 instead of the kind-required block_count=1 for bootstraps. The error message assertion at scan.rs:1473-1476 ('bootstrap tape file has block_count 2, expected 1') is documentation-quality: a future operator sees exactly which tape file is wrong and what was expected. Observation 3: the truncated-sidecar test at scan.rs:1482-1519 is the symmetric case. It REMOVES the last physical block of the sidecar at scan.rs:1497-1500 — the sidecar's header (block 0) still parses cleanly and declares block_count = N, but only N-1 physical blocks exist before the trailing filemark. The assertions at scan.rs:1506-1517 require BOTH 'sidecar epoch 0 has block_count' AND 'expected {sidecar.block_count}' substrings, pinning the precise diagnostic. Observation 4: the precondition at scan.rs:1487-1490 ('sidecar.block_count > 1, fixture sidecar must have a removable trailing block') is the right defensive gate. If a future fixture change reduced the sidecar to a single block, the test would refuse to run with a clear panic rather than silently passing a degenerate case. The codex thought about test robustness explicitly. Observation 5: the assertion at scan.rs:1461-1464 ('matches!(records.get(final_filemark_index), Some(Record::Filemark))') is the right defensive check before inserting the extra block. It verifies the fixture indeed has a Filemark at the expected position, catching a future fixture restructure that would move the filemark and silently insert the extra block into the wrong location. Observation 6: both tests use ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position to locate target positions by tape_file_number (not by hard-coded record indices). This is robust against fixture changes — adding more tape files before the bootstrap or sidecar would not break these tests' targeting. Observation 7: the FilemarkMapReconstruct error variant (vs the FilemarkMapDigestMismatch variant used by prior tests) correctly classifies these as STRUCTURAL damage, not classification damage. The distinction matters: a downgraded-to-object kind would still allow recovery via the catalog-supplied map, but a structural length mismatch cannot be reconciled — there's no way to rebuild the missing block or excise the extra block. The error variant is the right signal to Layer 5 for fallback decisions. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change, no rem-tar body-format change, no catalog schema change, no crash/restart behavior change — purely additive coverage. The scan validation logic was already correct for these cases; these tests pin the position-uniform-AND-structurally-distinct behavior. The codex chose this work independently (idref: null) to fill out the structural-damage matrix, complementing the prior CRC/CBOR/missing-filemark cases. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 287 incl. the two new structural-length tests, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the extra-block test corrupts ONE position (final bootstrap) and one severity (+1 extra block); the truncated test similarly corrupts ONE position (first sidecar) and one severity (-1 block); multi-block insertions, multi-block truncations, and the same corruption at OTHER positions (BOT bootstrap +1, intermediate bootstrap, object +1 etc.) are not separately exercised; the tests do not cover the symmetric case where header declares block_count=N but physical file has block_count=N+1 (extra blocks AT the sidecar position); live MSL3040 hardware where actual write-failure-mid-tape-file would produce different mismatch patterns is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 2525b1cc (Layer 3c multi-resume-sidecar append/write gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the expanded test at sink.rs:2906-3060+ closes BOTH residuals I called out in Claude review ecf05439 — 'the test exercises ONE resume sidecar emitted ... multiple resume sidecars ... is not separately tested' AND 'the test pins the next-object tape_file_number but does not exercise WRITING that object's body blocks (just the reserve), so end-to-end resume-into-write flows are still untested'. The codex took BOTH residuals literally and addressed each one in the same test expansion: 1 sidecar → 2 sidecars, reserve-only → reserve+write_block+finish_object. Observation 2: the committed prefix at sink.rs:2909-2915 now has FIVE tape files (bootstrap + object + 3 sidecars), with object@1 carrying 36 blocks spanning 3 epochs and sidecar@2/@3/@4 each protecting one epoch. The structure documents the realistic crash-then-rebuild scenario where the writer wrote 3 epochs of data, committed only sidecar@2 (epoch 0), crashed, then resume-rebuild emitted sidecar@3 (epoch 1) AND sidecar@4 (epoch 2). The fixture's epoch ordinal boundaries (0..12, 12..24, 24..36) follow the §5.4 row-major epoch boundary convention at scheme(4,2,3) → 12 data shards per epoch. Observation 3: the three distinct append positions at sink.rs:2917-2929 (original_append, first_resume_sidecar_append, committed_append) anchor the rejection test's coverage. The assertion at sink.rs:2930-2933 ('assert_ne!(original_append, committed_append)') still applies but the multi-sidecar structure makes them more meaningfully distinct — each resume sidecar adds (block_count + 1) LBAs to the catalog append point, so committed_append is original_append + 2*(1+1) = +4 LBAs ahead. Observation 4: the two-case rejection loop at sink.rs:2966-3015 is the key structural innovation. It rejects BOTH the pre-rebuild append point AND the after-first-resume-sidecar append point (a position that would have been valid if only ONE sidecar had committed). This pins the §7.8 invariant that the cursor must match the FULL committed prefix's append point, not any intermediate state. A regression where the guard accepted a position that matched the first resume sidecar's append point (e.g. via partial prefix matching) would fail on the second iteration's case with a clear label 'append point after only the first resume sidecar'. Observation 5: each rejection case's diagnostic assertions at sink.rs:2996-3007 require BOTH the actual cursor LBA AND the committed (full-prefix) expected LBA. This documents the Layer 5 contract: when multiple resume sidecars committed, the error must name the FULL committed-prefix append point, not just say 'wrong position'. A regression that emitted the first-sidecar append point in the error message would fail the assertion. Observation 6: the acceptance path at sink.rs:3017-3060+ now does end-to-end writing — begin_object_with_capacity_reserve at sink.rs:3036-3040 returns tape_file_number=5 (after both resume sidecars at tape_files 3 and 4), then write_block at sink.rs:3045-3048 writes ONE physical block, then finish_object at sink.rs:3054-3056 emits the trailing filemark. The position_after assertion at sink.rs:3049-3053 ('write.position_after.lba == committed_append.lba + 1') proves the first written block lands EXACTLY one LBA past the append point — no gap, no overlap, no off-by-one. Observation 7: the summary-field assertions at sink.rs:3057-3062 pin the entire object's state after the write: tape_file_number=5 (consistent with the reserve), first_parity_data_ordinal=36 (after the resume-protected ordinals 0..36), data_block_count=1 (one block written), highest_protected_ordinal=36 (still 36, no new sidecar emitted for this single-block object since it doesn't fill an epoch), sidecars_emitted.is_empty() (no auto-emit for partial-epoch). These five fields cross-check the writer's internal accounting after a multi-resume-sidecar restart. Observation 8: the codex self-review pass 2 explicitly notes 'the accepted path writes exactly Position, one fixed object block, and one object filemark, proving the next object body begins after both resume-generated sidecars rather than merely reserving a tape-file number'. This was the right self-review check — verifying that the test ACTUALLY exercises the body-write flow, not just the reserve path the prior version of this test had stopped at. Observation 9: no production code change (the guard logic is unchanged), no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change beyond the test's own write_block call which exercises existing production behavior. The codex summary correctly enumerates this. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 287 unchanged — this is an expansion of the existing test from codex bf6e7f50, not a new function, so the function count stays the same while the iteration count and assertion depth grow substantially, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the expanded test exercises 2 resume sidecars; THREE or more resume sidecars is not separately tested — though the append_position_after_prefix calculation sums uniformly across all entries and would handle N sidecars; the test writes ONE object block but does NOT exercise writing a full epoch's worth of blocks that would trigger an auto-sidecar emit by the post-resume writer; live MSL3040 hardware where actual resume-into-write sequences with real SCSI block positioning is not characterised here.
