author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 4ac5b54b (Layer 3c sidecar extra-block structural scan gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test valid_sidecar_header_with_extra_block_is_structural_damage at scan.rs:1521-1565 closes the EXACT 'sidecar N+1' residual I called out in Claude review 5cfc4453 — 'the tests do not cover the symmetric case where header declares block_count=N but physical file has block_count=N+1 (extra blocks AT the sidecar position)'. The codex took the residual literally and added the symmetric case to the existing N-1 truncated-sidecar test (codex 69a7df6d). The two tests now form a structurally complete pair: header declares N, physical N-1 → FilemarkMapReconstruct (truncated); header declares N, physical N+1 → FilemarkMapReconstruct (extra block, this entry). Observation 2: the test's targeting at scan.rs:1530-1542 is methodical. It computes last_sidecar_block via ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position (not hard-coded), then derives sidecar_filemark_index = last_sidecar_block.lba + 1 (the position where the trailing filemark currently lives). The defensive assertion at scan.rs:1538-1541 ('matches!(records.get(sidecar_filemark_index), Some(Record::Filemark))') verifies the fixture state before mutation, catching any future fixture restructure that would put the insertion in the wrong place. Then records.insert at scan.rs:1542 pushes the filemark one position later, creating an N+1-block sidecar structurally. Observation 3: the assertion at scan.rs:1554-1561 pins BOTH the actual measured block_count (N+1) AND the expected block_count (N) in the diagnostic message via `format!("block_count {}, expected {}", sidecar.block_count + 1, sidecar.block_count)`. This is the right precision — a regression where the diagnostic lost either field, or printed the actual as N (the same as expected) due to a bug in the measurement code, would surface here. The 'block_count N+1' format string also documents the test's intent: it's exercising the +1 over-count case, not generic 'wrong count'. Observation 4: the precondition assertion at scan.rs:1526-1529 ('sidecar.block_count > 0, fixture sidecar must have a trailing filemark target') is the right defensive gate. If a future fixture change degenerated the sidecar to 0 blocks (an invalid structure that wouldn't get this far in practice), the test would refuse to run rather than silently passing a degenerate case. Observation 5: the codex correctly chose to insert the extra block AT the filemark index, not at some other position. This places the bogus block AS PART OF the sidecar's physical tape file (between block_count-1 and the filemark), not between two tape files. A regression where the test inserted at a different index would either (a) shift the filemark to a non-adjacent position (breaking the fixture structure), or (b) extend a non-sidecar tape file (testing the wrong file). The codex picked the right insertion point. Observation 6: the error variant FilemarkMapReconstruct (not FilemarkMapDigestMismatch) correctly classifies this as STRUCTURAL damage. As I observed in Claude review 5cfc4453, structural damage means the scan path is unusable at this tape file — the catalog-supplied map's authority cannot reconcile the physical mismatch. The error variant choice is consistent with the truncated-sidecar test from codex 69a7df6d and the extra-bootstrap-block test from the same entry. Observation 7: the codex self-review pass 1 explicitly notes 'the inserted block is placed at the sidecar filemark index derived from ScopedFilemarkMap::physical_position, so the valid sidecar header remains block 0 and the failure is measured length N+1'. This was the correct self-review check — confirming the test's setup actually produces the intended structural mismatch rather than corrupting some other part of the sidecar. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change, no rem-tar body-format change, no catalog schema change, no crash/restart behavior change — purely additive coverage. The scan validation logic was already correct for this case; the test pins the symmetric N+1 behavior at the §8.1 reference. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 288 incl. the new sidecar N+1 test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins ONE extra block (N+1 exactly); multi-block insertions (N+2, N+10) at the sidecar position are not separately exercised, though the scan's measurement is N-vs-expected-equality so any non-zero excess would trip the same path; the symmetric N+1 case is now covered at the sidecar position but not yet at the OTHER tape file positions (e.g. object N+1 or bootstrap N+2 vs the existing N+1 bootstrap test); live MSL3040 hardware where actual write-failure-mid-tape-file would produce different mismatch patterns is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 034a7163 (Layer 3c bootstrap scan block-size mismatch gate, in crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs). No finding. Observation 1: the new test bootstrap_wrong_declared_block_size_becomes_object_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1177-1226+ pins a §8.1 invariant that the prior CRC/CBOR/structural-length tests did NOT exercise — bootstrap classification requires not only valid magic + valid header CRC + valid payload CRC + parseable CBOR, but ALSO that the bootstrap's declared block_size_bytes matches the actual physical block size being scanned. Until now, all bootstrap-corruption tests either failed CRC/CBOR validation or failed block-count validation; this test demonstrates that a fully-internally-valid bootstrap can STILL fail classification if its declared block_size disagrees with the scan's block_size. This is the right closing test for the bootstrap-classification matrix. Observation 2: the test's setup at scan.rs:1180-1197 is rigorous. It builds a wrong_size_payload via bootstrap_payload(prefix_map.digest(false), 0) at scan.rs:1182, then mutates payload.block_size_bytes to BLOCK_SIZE - 1 at scan.rs:1183. The bootstrap is then encoded via write_bootstrap_block at scan.rs:1185 — producing a structurally complete, internally-valid bootstrap block. The codex correctly uses the PROD encoder, so this isn't a hand-crafted invalid block; it's a real bootstrap whose only fault is the wrong declared block_size. Observation 3: the load-bearing assertion at scan.rs:1199-1202 ('parse_bootstrap_block(bot_block).is_ok(), "test bootstrap should fail only scan block-size classification"') is the right defensive discipline. It verifies the test setup actually produces the intended failure mode — a bootstrap that PARSES correctly but FAILS scan classification. Without this assertion, a future regression in the bootstrap parser that rejected wrong-size bootstraps would silently turn this test into a parser-bug test (passing for the wrong reason). Observation 4: the kinds-vector assertion at scan.rs:1209-1222 is [Object, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] — kinds[0] is now Object (the BOT bootstrap was downgraded due to block_size mismatch), kinds[3] remains Bootstrap (the final bootstrap is intact). This is structurally IDENTICAL to the prior BOT-payload-CRC test (codex 6aa08ec2) and the BOT-CBOR test (codex 9f851e0d) kinds vectors — all three BOT-position tests produce the same downgrade pattern. The three tests are discriminated by the FAILURE MODE in their setup, not by the externally-observable kinds vector. Observation 5: the assertion message at scan.rs:1221 ('valid bootstrap magic and CRCs are not enough when block_size_bytes disagrees with the scan block size') correctly contrasts with the prior tests' messages ('valid header is not enough', 'valid CRCs are not enough'). The three-axis matrix is now: payload-CRC fails / CBOR-parse fails / block-size mismatches. Each axis has a distinct setup but produces the same downgrade outcome. Observation 6: the FilemarkMapDigestMismatch assertion at scan.rs:1223-1224 closes the loop: after the BOT is downgraded, the reconstructed map's canonical projection differs from the expected map (kinds vector shift), so validation against the authoritative final digest fails. This matches the §8.1 invariant that catalog-less scan-reconstruction failures surface as digest mismatches. Observation 7: the codex self-review pass 1 explicitly notes 'the injected BOT block still parses successfully with bootstrap magic and CRCs, so the failure is specifically scan block-size classification rather than a damaged bootstrap parse'. This was the right self-review check — confirming the test exercises the intended failure path. Observation 8: no production code change, no public API change, no SCSI CDB or sense-code change, no tape-command behavior change, no rem-tar body-format change, no catalog schema change, no crash/restart behavior change — purely additive coverage. The scan-reconstruct block-size validation was already correct; this test pins the position-uniform block-size-mismatch behavior at the §8.1 reference. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-library 214, remanence-parity 289 incl. the new bootstrap block-size mismatch test, parity integration 7, scan_round_trip 14, other workspace suites 95/16, hardware-gated suites ignored). Residual risk: the test pins block_size = BLOCK_SIZE - 1; other size mismatches (BLOCK_SIZE * 2, very small sizes like 64 bytes, zero) are not separately exercised, though the scan's equality check is uniform; the symmetric case at intermediate and final bootstrap positions is not exercised — would extend the test matrix; live MSL3040 hardware where a real bootstrap from a different-block-size tape might surface differently (e.g. via SCSI fixed-block-size negotiation) is not characterised here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 6beffc15 (object-position length-drift scan + digest gate: object_with_truncated_file_is_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1534 and object_with_extra_block_is_candidate_and_digest_mismatches at scan.rs:1586). Finding [no finding]: the pair pins the §8.1 distinction that residual codex review 969fe552 named — bootstrap and sidecar length drift fail structurally inside scan_reconstruct_filemark_map (sidecar at scan.rs:1668 'sidecar epoch 0 has block_count', bootstrap at scan.rs:1525 'bootstrap tape file has block_count 2, expected 1') while object length drift is preserved by classification-by-elimination (§8.1, design L3071-3086) and is only caught by ScopedFilemarkMap::validate_against_digest against the authoritative final bootstrap digest. Each test asserts (a) reconstruction succeeds, (b) the full kind vector remains [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] (scan.rs:1565-1572 and 1621-1628) — defending against silent re-classification of neighbors, (c) entries[1].block_count reflects the exact drift (object.block_count-1 at scan.rs:1574 and object.block_count+1 at scan.rs:1630), and (d) the rejection is specifically ParityError::FilemarkMapDigestMismatch (scan.rs:1580 and 1636), not a different error masquerading as success. The N+1 test inserts at object_filemark_index (scan.rs:1601) so the inserted Block(0xEF) lands strictly inside the object tape file and shifts the trailing filemark by one — the object's first block (0xA0) is unchanged, so elimination-classification still routes it to object_candidate per design L3060-3067. The N-1 test removes the last object block, leaving block 0xA0 as the only object block; first-block classification is again unchanged. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 291 passed; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry e4eb2ab1 (object scan magic and multi-slot drift gates: multi_epoch_object_extra_blocks_are_digest_checked_at_non_first_slots at scan.rs:1642 and object_first_block_with_structural_magic_but_invalid_crc_stays_object_candidate at scan.rs:1713). Finding [no finding]: the pair cleanly closes the two residuals named in Claude review f23cf014 — multi-slot/multi-block object drift and adversarial first-block magic. The multi-epoch test iterates over the two non-first object slots tape_file_number 3 and 5 in the 8-entry multi_epoch_fixture_records (scan.rs:577-587 layout B/O/S/O/S/O/S/B), inserts two extra blocks at the object's trailing-filemark boundary (object_filemark_index at scan.rs:1657), reconstructs successfully, asserts the full kind vector is preserved (scan.rs:1685-1694), asserts the affected entry's block_count == object.block_count + 2 (scan.rs:1702), and asserts ParityError::FilemarkMapDigestMismatch (scan.rs:1706). This proves the §8.1 distinction (design L3071-3086) is robust beyond the single-block first-slot case I pinned in 6beffc15. The structural-magic test exercises both bootstrap and sidecar magic placed in an object's first block: the bootstrap arm constructs a valid bootstrap_block then flips BOOTSTRAP_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET (scan.rs:1738), self-asserts parse_bootstrap_block fails (scan.rs:1740), and after replacement asserts reconstructed == expected_map plus digest validates (scan.rs:1776-1781) — matching the scan classifier at scan.rs:176-188 which falls through to push_object when parse_bootstrap_block errors. The sidecar arm clones the genuine sidecar header (carrying valid HMAC-derived per-tape magic, which is essential since sidecar magic at scan.rs:190 derives from tape_uuid), flips SIDECAR_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET, self-asserts classify_sidecar_header_block returns Err(SidecarParse) (scan.rs:1761-1764), and after replacement asserts the same expected_map equality + digest validation — matching the classifier fall-through at scan.rs:190-207 which only promotes on Ok(Some(header)), not on Err. The post-replace digest passes because filemark-map digest is computed over structural metadata (kind, block_count, epoch range) and excludes object payload bytes — implicitly confirming that scan does not treat magic-looking-but-broken object payload as a structural hijack. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 293 passed, +2 from 291; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the structural-magic test uses *invalid* CRCs; the symmetric adversarial case where an object payload carries a *valid* bootstrap header CRC would hit the block_count != 1 structural error at scan.rs:179-183 rather than the digest gate — that variant is not exercised and the routing is undocumented in this test. Multi-slot truncation (symmetric -N drift, vs. the tested +N) and simultaneous drift across both non-first object slots are not tested. A perfect (CRC-valid) sidecar clone inside an object first block would create an epoch-id collision against the genuine sidecar; that collision-detection path is not exercised either.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 4bbf823a (contiguous-damage length matrix: contiguous_damage_required_length_matrix_matches_recovery_threshold at recovery.rs:1325). Finding [no finding]: the test pins the §9.2 + model.rs:93 contiguous_damage_threshold = S × m semantics across the full matrix {1, S-1, S, 2S, mS, mS+1} on the k=4, m=3, S=5 geometry (recovery.rs:1326), and crucially loops failed_ordinal over the entire damaged span (recovery.rs:1343 `for failed_ordinal in 0..damage_len`) so every lost ordinal in the damaged window is independently reconstructed — not just one representative. The per-case expected_lost values (1, 1, 1, 2, 3) at recovery.rs:1333-1339 correctly track the row-major interleave (design §5.2.1, scheme.rs L731-739): under row-major mapping ordinal % S → stripe, contiguous damage of length L hits each stripe ceil(L/S) times for L ≤ mS, so the loss-per-failed-stripe is 1 for L ∈ {1, S-1, S}, 2 for L=2S, and 3 for L=mS — the parity bound m=3 — keeping each stripe recoverable. The mS+1 case at recovery.rs:1371-1388 asserts both lost_count = m+1 and limit = m on the Unrecoverable variant, which is the canonical signal that exactly one stripe (stripe 0, since ordinal 15 % 5 = 0 hits stripe 0 a fourth time) is overloaded. Each iteration also takes a fresh raw_tape (recovery.rs:1344) so recovery-cache state from one ordinal does not leak into another. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 294 passed, +1 from 293; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the damage is anchored at body_lba 0; off-zero damage starts (e.g. mid-stripe alignment such as body_lba S/2 or near the epoch boundary at body_lba k*S-1) are not exercised — under row-major mapping the loss-per-stripe count is start-invariant, but a misaligned start that crosses the epoch's k*S ordinal boundary into the next epoch is not tested by this single-epoch fixture. Geometries beyond k=4/m=3/S=5 (notably the default k=128/m=4/S=512 and the conservative k=64/m=6/S=256) are not separately exercised here, though the threshold formula is purely geometric. Sidecar-damage interaction (damaged sidecar payload concurrent with object damage, which §9.2 still requires to surface as Unrecoverable) is not part of this matrix and is covered separately.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 84919a39 (same-stripe object erasure recovery gate: multiple_object_data_erasures_in_one_stripe_recover_up_to_m at recovery.rs:1136). Finding [no finding]: the test complements the contiguous-damage matrix from 4bbf823a by exercising *non-contiguous* same-stripe erasure patterns — ordinals {0,5} (n=2) and {0,5,10} (n=3 = m) on the k=4, m=3, S=5 geometry. The row-major mapping at mapping.rs:115-131 places ordinals 0/5/10 into stripe_index 0 at data positions 0/1/2 respectively (since position = ordinal_in_epoch / S and stripe_index = ordinal_in_epoch % S), and the test independently asserts this geometry via ordinal_to_stripe (recovery.rs:1163-1177) before injecting erasures — so a regression in either the production mapping or the test's expectation would be loudly caught. The test pins recovery's lost_shards vector exactly (recovery.rs:1201 `assert_eq!(recovered.lost_shards, expected_lost)`), not just its length, ensuring the StripePosition::Data{index} reported by production matches the row-major data-shard identity. Recovered bytes are checked against the original object block at ordinal 0 (recovery.rs:1200). The n=m=3 case is the canonical at-the-bound case for §9.2 / Appendix A.5 erasure recovery: exactly m lost data shards in a single stripe with all m parity shards available is the boundary that distinguishes recoverable from unrecoverable under RS(k,m). Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 295 passed, +1 from 294; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: failed_ordinal is always 0; the symmetric case where the *requested* failed_ordinal is 5 or 10 (a different lost ordinal in the same set) is not exercised — recovery should be ordinal-agnostic for a given lost set, but the assertion would catch a routing bug only on failed_ordinal=0. Multi-stripe non-contiguous erasures (e.g. {0,1,5,6} hitting stripes 0 and 1 each with 2 lost) and same-stripe erasures combined with parity-shard erasures (e.g. n=2 data + 1 parity shard, still recoverable as long as total ≤ m) are not in this matrix. The n=1 case is covered by adjacent tests in the suite; an n=m+1=4 same-stripe overflow gate (which should surface ParityError::Unrecoverable with lost_count=4, limit=3) is also not present in this specific test but is exercised by the contiguous-damage threshold test from 4bbf823a.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry e1b5c9fe (off-zero + cross-epoch contiguous-damage gate: contiguous_damage_threshold_is_start_invariant_within_and_across_epochs at recovery.rs:1535, plus helper extractions sidecar_for_epoch_at_with_data_crcs at recovery.rs:639 and the new two-epoch fixture pair scoped_two_epoch_sidecar_map / raw_tape_two_epoch_sidecars at recovery.rs:699 and recovery.rs:773). Finding [no finding]: this closes the residual I named in e7b07af7 — the prior contiguous-damage matrix was anchored at body_lba 0, so the start-invariance property of §9.2 (the row-major mapping makes loss-per-stripe count independent of starting ordinal under length-only damage, design L731-739) was implicit. The new test makes start-invariance explicit at start_body_lba = 2 (mid-stripe inside epoch 0) and start_body_lba = epoch_data_shards - 1 = 19 (deliberately straddling the epoch-0/epoch-1 boundary at ordinal 20), iterates failed_ordinal over the entire damaged span (recovery.rs:1564), and crucially derives expected_lost per-(epoch, stripe) at recovery.rs:1587-1594 — so a recovery routing bug that mis-attributed damage from one epoch's stripe to a same-stripe-index in a different epoch would be caught. The cross-epoch case at start=19 exercises an essential design property: damage from ordinal 19 (epoch 0, stripe 4, row 3) through ordinal 33 (epoch 1) must use the correct per-epoch sidecar for parity recovery; the test verifies recovery succeeds for every affected ordinal regardless of which side of the epoch boundary it sits on. The off-zero mS+1 overload at recovery.rs:1610-1638 targets start=2 with damage_len=16; under row-major mapping ordinals 2..18 spread across 5 stripes as (3, 3, 4, 4, 3) lost data shards (stripes 2 and 3 each take 4 hits from rows 0,1,2,3), so failed_ordinal=2 (stripe 2) sees lost_count=4 > limit=3 → Unrecoverable, asserted exactly. The helper refactor at recovery.rs:605-637 unifies sidecar_for_epoch and sidecar_for_epoch_with_data_crcs as thin wrappers over sidecar_for_epoch_at_with_data_crcs with epoch_id=0, protected_ordinal_start=0 — preserves all prior call-site semantics. Each iteration also constructs raw_tape_two_epoch_sidecars freshly (recovery.rs:1565) so cache/state from a previous attempt cannot mask a fault. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 296 passed, +1 from 295; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the cross-epoch case only tests forward damage (epoch 0 → epoch 1); a damage start inside epoch 1 wrapping back is impossible in this single-object fixture but extending into a hypothetical epoch 2 is not tested. The two-epoch fixture uses a single object tape file for both epochs; the alternate topology where each epoch's object data lives in its own tape file (multi-object multi-epoch) is not exercised by this test. The off-zero overload is tested only at start=2 in epoch 0 stripe 2; a symmetric overload that straddles the epoch boundary (e.g. start=18 with len=mS+1, which would hit stripe 3 in epoch 0 with 1 lost from ord 18 plus parts of epoch 1) is not exercised — though under the per-epoch-per-stripe accounting it should not overload any single epoch+stripe.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 459a51cb (§9.2 non-contiguous recovery residual closure: modified multiple_object_data_erasures_in_one_stripe_recover_up_to_m at recovery.rs:1223 to iterate every requested failed_ordinal in the lost set; new non_contiguous_object_erasures_across_stripes_recover_per_failed_stripe at recovery.rs:1298; new same_stripe_data_and_parity_erasures_recover_at_rs_limit at recovery.rs:1355; helper extraction object_lbas_for_ordinals at recovery.rs:816). Finding [no finding]: codex's three follow-ups close all three residuals named in Claude review 0944f57b in one batch. (1) The modified test now loops `for failed_ordinal in &lost_ordinals` (recovery.rs:1267) so ordinals 5 and 10 are exercised as the requested failed ordinal, not just ordinal 0 — a routing bug specific to non-zero failed ordinals would now surface. The lost_shards assertion stays at the deterministic [Data{0}, Data{1}] (n=2) / [Data{0}, Data{1}, Data{2}] (n=3) because the set is constructed symmetrically and recovery's reported shards are stripe-relative, not ordinal-relative. (2) The new multi-stripe non-contiguous test (recovery.rs:1298) uses {0,1,5,6} — under row-major mapping (mapping.rs:115-131) ordinals 0/5 hit stripe 0 positions Data{0}/Data{1} and ordinals 1/6 hit stripe 1 positions Data{0}/Data{1}; expected_lost is derived per-failed-stripe (recovery.rs:1310-1316) and asserted to [Data{0}, Data{1}] for every failed ordinal — defending against a bug where recovery aggregated losses across stripes. (3) The new data+parity test (recovery.rs:1355) damages object ordinals {0,5} (stripe 0 Data{0,1}) plus the first parity shard's physical block; it pins the fixture by asserting sidecar.index.parity_entries[0] is (stripe 0, parity 0) at recovery.rs:1364-1365 — so the test fails loudly if codec or layout changes shift parity ordering — and asserts lost_shards = [Data{0}, Data{1}, Parity{0}] at exactly the m=3 RS limit (recovery.rs:1391-1395), pinning the §9.2 Appendix A.5 erasure boundary for mixed data+parity losses. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 298 passed, +2 from 296; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the data+parity test damages only parity_entries[0] (stripe 0 parity 0); damaging non-first parity shards (e.g. parity_entries[1] = stripe 0 parity 1, or any stripe-N parity) is not separately exercised — the recovery path should be parity-index-symmetric but is not pinned here. All three patterns use the same k=4/m=3/S=5 single-epoch geometry; multi-epoch variants (e.g. data+parity erasures spanning two epochs' sidecars) are not exercised. An n=4 overload case mixing data+parity (e.g. 3 data + 1 parity in one stripe → lost_count=4 > m=3) that should surface ParityError::Unrecoverable is also not present in this matrix, though the symmetric data-only overload is covered by 4bbf823a's contiguous-damage threshold test.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 0dfcd4b9 (epoch-boundary mS+1 recovery gate: m_s_plus_one_damage_straddling_epoch_boundary_recovers_when_each_epoch_stripe_is_bounded at recovery.rs:1765). Finding [no finding]: this closes the third and final residual I named in ea0e4f9d — the symmetric overload that straddles the epoch boundary. Damage spans ordinals 18..34 (damage_start=18, damage_len=16=mS+1). Under the row-major / per-epoch accounting (mapping.rs:115-131, design L731-739): epoch 0 contributes only ordinals 18 (stripe 3) and 19 (stripe 4) — 1 loss each; epoch 1 contributes ordinals 20..33 = 14 ordinals which distribute across 5 stripes as (3, 3, 3, 3, 2) lost data shards (the four stripes 0-3 take 3 hits = at the m=3 bound, stripe 4 takes 2 hits). No single epoch+stripe exceeds m=3, so all 16 affected ordinals recover. This pins the §9.2 invariant that the contiguous_damage_threshold formula at model.rs:93 is a *per-epoch* property — physical damage can exceed it globally and still recover provided the per-(epoch,stripe) accounting stays at-or-below m. The test iterates failed_ordinal over the full damaged span (recovery.rs:1793), uses a fresh raw tape per attempt (recovery.rs:1794), derives expected_lost per-(neighborhood, stripe_index) at recovery.rs:1814-1821 — so a recovery routing bug that conflated stripes across epochs or summed losses globally would be loudly caught. The recovered.lost_shards <= m assertion at recovery.rs:1830 makes the invariant explicit. Together with e1b5c9fe (off-zero in-epoch overload at start=2 → Unrecoverable) and 4bbf823a (single-epoch mS+1 overload at start=0 → Unrecoverable), this triplet covers the three orthogonal mS+1 scenarios: (a) overflow inside one epoch's stripe; (b) overflow inside one epoch's stripe with off-zero start; (c) globally over-threshold but per-epoch within bound. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 299 passed, +1 from 298; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the fixture uses a single object tape file holding both epochs and a per-epoch sidecar; the multi-object multi-epoch topology (each epoch's data in its own object tape file separated by filemarks) is still not exercised here. Three-or-more-epoch fixtures with damage spanning more than one epoch boundary are also untested — though under the per-epoch accounting the principle should hold uniformly. The epoch-boundary residual I named for the missing alternate topology (multi-object multi-epoch) remains open.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry bd854e40 (multi-epoch mixed data/parity erasure gate: data_and_parity_erasures_across_two_object_epochs_recover_per_epoch_sidecar at recovery.rs:1634). Finding [no finding]: this closes the multi-epoch mixed-erasure residual I named in ed6f47cb. The fixture reuses the multi-object two-epoch topology (B/O/S/O/S = files 0..4) introduced in abdd9ae5 and damages *both* epoch sidecars in one raw source: epoch 0 ordinals {0,5} + epoch 0 sidecar stripe 0 parity 1 (via parity_lba_for_shard at recovery.rs:1658), and epoch 1 ordinals {23,28} + epoch 1 sidecar stripe 3 parity 2 (recovery.rs:1659). Each epoch+stripe sees Data{0}, Data{1}, Parity{N} = 3 lost shards at the m=3 RS limit. The fixture-strength block at recovery.rs:1661-1691 verifies (a) the two ordinal groups have different neighborhood (epoch_data_shards × 1 vs ×0) values, (b) different stripe_index (0 vs 3) values, and (c) the canonical [Data{0}, Data{1}] position vector — these assertions prevent silent fixture drift where damage groups accidentally collapsed into the same epoch+stripe. Recovery is driven through recover_object_block_from_sidecar at recovery.rs:1722-1730 for failed ordinals 5 (epoch 0) and 28 (epoch 1); the assertions at recovery.rs:1742-1753 pin (a) per-epoch sidecar routing (sidecar_tape_file_number 2 vs 4), (b) exact lost_shards including the correct parity-index per epoch (Parity{1} vs Parity{2}), and (c) lost_shards.len() == m=3 — the RS bound. Crucially, this test would fail if recovery aggregated lost shards across both sidecars (it would report 6 losses instead of 3) or routed to the wrong epoch's sidecar — defending against cross-epoch interference, a real concern for the §9.2 + §10.1 epoch-local recovery invariant. Combined with 459a51cb (parity 0), b57d825a (parity 1/2, single epoch), and now this entry, the mixed data+parity coverage now spans {parity 0, 1, 2} × {single-epoch, multi-epoch} except for parity 0 in multi-epoch. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 303 passed, +1 from 302; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: of the three residuals I named in ed6f47cb, only one is closed here — (1) multi-epoch mixed erasures. The other two remain: (2) multiple parity shards in the same stripe (e.g. 1 data + 2 parity = m total; or 0 data + m parity = m total, a degenerate corner where data peers are unaffected); (3) mixed *parity-only* damage across multiple stripes in one recovery attempt — though the current test technically damages parity in two stripes across two epochs, no single recovery attempt traverses both parity-damaged stripes since each recovery only ever needs one stripe's shards. The 'multiple parity in one stripe' residual is genuinely unexercised.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry ee3782ae (final partial-epoch recovery gate using §5.4 implicit-zero data shards: final_partial_epoch_recovery_supplies_implicit_zero_peers at recovery.rs:1345, with sidecar_for_epoch_at_with_data_crcs at recovery.rs:639 extended to handle real_data_shards < k*S). Finding [no finding]: this closes the asymmetric-epoch / implicit-zero residual I named in d20fd2e6 — the only previously-uncovered case from the §9.2 recovery matrix where a sidecar declares logical_shard_count > real_data_shard_count. The fixture helper at recovery.rs:660-675 now constructs each stripe's k data shards by ordinal lookup against object_blocks, falling back to a zero_block for positions where ordinal >= object_blocks.len(). This mirrors the production §5.4 path where missing final-epoch positions are encoded as implicit zeros for RS parity math while the sidecar header carries real_data_shard_count and data_crc_count tied only to real shards. The test's fixture invariants at recovery.rs:1355-1376 explicitly pin: logical_shard_count==20, real_data_shard_count==7, data_crc_count==7, and protected_ordinal_end_exclusive==7; failed_ordinal=4 is real (in stripe 4, position Data{0}), and the three same-stripe peers 9, 14, 19 are all >= real_data_shards=7 (so they must be implicit zeros — recovery.rs:1372-1375). Recovery succeeds with lost_shards = [Data{0}] only (recovery.rs:1395-1398) — implicit-zero peers are *not* counted as erasures because they're known a priori. This is the canonical signal that production recovery distinguishes 'missing because not real' from 'missing because damaged'. The test would fail loudly if recovery treated implicit zeros as additional erasures (lost_shards would balloon from 1 to 4, but m=3 — recovery would still 'succeed' in this fixture, but the lost_shards equality assertion would fail). Recovered bytes are verified against the original real shard at recovery.rs:1391-1394, and sidecar_tape_file_number==2 pins the routing. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 307 passed, +1 from 306; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: only one real_data_shards value (7 of 20) is tested; symmetric edge cases — e.g. real_data_shards=1 (a single real shard with k*S-1 implicit zeros), real_data_shards=k*S-1 (one implicit zero), or real_data_shards exactly equal to k (so the first row is full and remaining rows are implicit zeros) — are not separately exercised. The failed ordinal is in stripe 4 (the last stripe by index) where all peers are implicit; failing an ordinal in stripe 0 or stripe 1 (where some peers are real and some are implicit, depending on real_data_shards=7) is the mixed-real-and-implicit-peer case and is not separately exercised. A partial-epoch fixture combined with damaged-data-or-parity in the partial epoch is also untested — though the recoverability bound is still m regardless.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 8f1a4937 (parity-overload recovery gate: all_same_stripe_parity_shards_lost_with_failed_data_is_unrecoverable at recovery.rs:1960). Finding [no finding]: this closes the parity-overload residual I named in 691e1439. Under k=4, m=3, S=5 the test damages failed ordinal 5 (stripe 0, Data{1}) plus *all m=3* parity shards of stripe 0 (loop at recovery.rs:1973-1981 iterates parity_index 0..m). Total losses in stripe 0: 4 = m+1, which exceeds the RS bound. Recovery surfaces ParityError::Unrecoverable with stripe == failed_address (the full StripeAddress is pinned, not just stripe_index), lost_count == m+1, limit == m (recovery.rs:2025-2027). The fixture-strength block at recovery.rs:1982-2004 explicitly verifies the *non-failed* same-stripe data peers (ordinals 0, 10, 15 → Data{0}, Data{2}, Data{3}) remain readable — this proves the unrecoverability comes from parity-side losses, not from accidental data-peer loss. Note: this is *m parity + 1 data* = m+1 total, not the literal '0 data + m+1 parity' I named (which is geometrically impossible since there are only m=3 parity shards per stripe); codex correctly chose the equivalent reachable boundary case where every parity shard is lost. Together with the existing same_stripe_data_and_parity_erasures_over_rs_limit_is_unrecoverable from b57d825a (3 data + 1 parity = m+1), this entry covers the symmetric overload axis: both 'all data peers lost + parity-recoverable' and 'all parity lost + data-peer-recoverable' surface as Unrecoverable with the same lost_count/limit assertion shape. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 308 passed, +1 from 307; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the test uses stripe 0; an analogous overload in stripe 4 (the last stripe by index) or a non-zero stripe is not separately exercised. Multi-stripe parity overload where *two* stripes each lose all their parity simultaneously — irrelevant to a single recovery attempt's success/failure but a stress-test on the lost_shards aggregation path — is also not exercised. A parity-overload that includes the per-stripe-CRC failure path (where the parity bytes are CRC-failed rather than physically unreadable) is not part of this test, though I/O-level erasure semantics treat both as Unreadable from §9.2's perspective.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 25807c40 (mixed real/implicit final-partial-epoch recovery gate: final_partial_epoch_recovery_mixes_real_and_implicit_zero_peers at recovery.rs:1403). Finding [no finding]: this closes the mixed-real-and-implicit-peer residual I named in 31cf2498. With real_data_shards=7 and S=5, stripe 0 contains ordinals {0, 5, 10, 15} — two of which (0 and 5) are real on-tape shards and two of which (10 and 15) are implicit zeros (since 10 >= 7 and 15 >= 7). The fixture invariants at recovery.rs:1418-1439 explicitly assert this geometry: failed_ordinal=0 is Data{0}, real_peer_ordinal=5 is Data{1} in the same stripe, and the assertion at recovery.rs:1428-1430 ('real_peer_ordinal < real_data_shards') pins the peer's reality. The loop at recovery.rs:1431-1439 asserts ordinals 10 and 15 are same-stripe but >= real_data_shards (implicit zeros). The test damages both real shards (failed_ordinal=0 and real_peer_ordinal=5) so stripe 0 sees 2 real-shard erasures; the implicit zeros at Data{2} and Data{3} are not erasures because they're known a priori. Recovery succeeds with lost_shards = [Data{0}, Data{1}] at recovery.rs:1459-1465 — pinning that the implicit peers are not added to the erasure list. This is the critical invariant for §5.4 mixed-peer recovery: the production path must distinguish 'missing-because-not-real' (known zero, free) from 'missing-because-damaged' (counts against the RS bound). The test would loudly fail if recovery treated implicit zeros as erasures (lost_shards would have 4 elements instead of 2). Recovered bytes verified at recovery.rs:1455-1458, sidecar routing at recovery.rs:1466. Together with ee3782ae's all-implicit case (failed ordinal 4 in stripe 4 with 3 implicit peers), this entry covers the mixed-peer case at stripe 0 where the implicit/real split is geometry-dependent. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 309 passed, +1 from 308; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the real_data_shards=1 boundary case (single real shard with k*S-1=19 implicit zeros), real_data_shards=k=4 case (first row full, remaining rows implicit), and real_data_shards=k*S-1=19 case (single implicit zero) are still not separately exercised. A partial-epoch fixture combined with damaged parity in the partial epoch is also untested — though the recoverability bound is still m regardless, and the implicit-zero accounting is independent of parity-side state.

Finding [no finding]: this closes all three boundary residuals I named in 1a4046c5 with a single table-driven test covering D=1, D=k, and D=k*S-1. (1) Case 'single real shard with damaged parity' (recovery.rs:1485-1497): real_data_shards=1, only one real shard exists at ordinal 0; the test damages ordinal 0 + same-stripe Parity{1}. Recovery succeeds with lost_shards = [Data{0}, Parity{1}] — proving the implicit-zero peers at ordinals 5, 10, 15 (all of Data{1, 2, 3}) are correctly treated as known-zero and not aggregated into the erasure count. (2) Case 'D equals k' (recovery.rs:1498-1507): real_data_shards=k=4, so the first row is exactly full and rows 1, 2, 3 are all implicit zeros. failed_ordinal=3 is in stripe 3, position Data{0}; recovery proves the row=0 case works at the row-boundary geometry. (3) Case 'single implicit zero at epoch tail' (recovery.rs:1508-1521): real_data_shards=k*S-1=19, with the single implicit zero at ordinal 19 (the last position of stripe 4). The test damages 3 real same-stripe shards (ordinals 4, 9, 14 → Data{0,1,2} of stripe 4), pushing the failed stripe to exactly m=3 lost real shards. Recovery succeeds by combining the one implicit-zero peer (ordinal 19 → Data{3}) with parity to reconstruct ordinal 14 — a sharp test that exercises the implicit-zero peer being the *only* surviving data peer in the stripe. The table-iteration block at recovery.rs:1524-1646 has uniformly strong fixture invariants per case: sidecar header field assertions (logical_shard_count, real_data_shard_count, data_crc_count), case.failed_ordinal < case.real_data_shards (real shard), case.real_data_shards < epoch_data_shards (partial epoch), same-stripe geometry for both damaged and implicit ordinals, distinct-LBA dedup, and recovered-bytes + lost_shards + sidecar_tape_file equality. Together with 31cf2498 (D=7 all-implicit failed stripe) and 1a4046c5 (D=7 mixed real/implicit failed stripe), the §5.4 partial-epoch matrix now covers D ∈ {1, k, 7, k*S-1} which spans the extreme low, row-boundary, mid-range, and extreme-high real-shard counts. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 311 passed, +1 from 310; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). summary: Review of codex entry df4ed19a (cross-epoch final-partial recovery gate: final_partial_epoch_after_full_epoch_recovers_with_epoch_local_damage at recovery.rs:1650). Finding [no finding]: this closes the cross-epoch final-partial residual I named in e3fe2b6d — a fixture combining a *full* preceding epoch (epoch 0) with a *partial* final epoch (D=k+1=5 at epoch 1). The partial sidecar header is pinned at recovery.rs:1679-1699: epoch_id=1, protected_ordinal_start=20, protected_ordinal_end_exclusive=25, logical_shard_count=20, real_data_shard_count=5, data_crc_count=5. The damage matrix at recovery.rs:1735-1762 is unusually rich for one test — it combines: (a) epoch-0 same-stripe data damage (ordinals 0 and 5 → Data{0,1} stripe 0) + epoch-0 stripe-0 parity 1; (b) partial epoch 1 stripe-0 Data{0} (the failed ordinal 20) + stripe-0 parity 2; (c) partial epoch 1 *noise* in stripe 4 (ordinal 24 + parity 0); (d) implicit-zero peers at partial epoch 1 ordinals 25/30/35 (asserted same-stripe at recovery.rs:1721-1733). The two-case recovery iteration at recovery.rs:1772-1827 pins the canonical invariants: (1) epoch-0 ordinal 5 recovers from sidecar tape file 2 with lost_shards [Data{0}, Data{1}, Parity{1}] (3 = m losses, the canonical at-the-bound recoverable case); (2) partial epoch 1 ordinal 20 recovers from sidecar tape file 3 with lost_shards [Data{0}, Parity{2}] (only 2 losses — implicit-zero peers and partial epoch stripe-4 noise are correctly excluded). This single test compounds five orthogonal invariants in one fixture: cross-epoch sidecar routing (per epoch), implicit-zero peer accounting (per §5.4), cross-stripe noise isolation (within an epoch), asymmetric epoch sizing (full + partial), and mixed data+parity damage. A regression in any one would surface here, and the lost_shards equality assertions (not just lengths) catch mis-attribution that would not change counts. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 313 passed, +1 from 312; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Finding [no finding]: this closes the cross-object-boundary damage residual I named in be322f64. Damage spans ordinals 18..24 = 7 ordinals: epoch 0 tail (18, 19 in tape file 1) + the *entire* partial-epoch real-shard set (20..24 in tape file 3, exactly D=k+1=5 ordinals). The fixture-strength block at recovery.rs:2108-2123 is the key invariant: boundary_gaps.len() == 1 (computed via damaged_lbas.windows(2).filter() at recovery.rs:2109-2112) — proving the damaged-LBA sequence contains *exactly one* LBA gap, and that gap straddles the full-object/partial-object boundary (boundary_gaps[0][0] == full_tail_lba, [0][1] == partial_head_lba at recovery.rs:2118-2119). The recovery loop at recovery.rs:2127-2186 iterates every damaged ordinal, derives expected_lost per-(epoch, stripe), and pins sidecar tape file 2 for ordinals < epoch_data_shards (epoch 0) and sidecar 4 otherwise (epoch 1). Per-(epoch, stripe) damage breakdown is uniformly 1 loss per affected stripe (epoch 0: stripes 3 and 4 have 1 loss each; epoch 1: all 5 stripes have 1 loss each), well under m=3, so every recovery succeeds. This is the strongest direct test of cross-object-boundary damage in the workspace; together with df4ed19a's single-object cross-epoch damage and 3c11d823's two-recovery cross-object proof, the multi-object full-then-partial topology is now fully covered for {separate-recovery, single-damage-set-crossing-boundary}. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry a96118b2 (boundary sidecar-noise recovery gate: full_then_partial_boundary_with_sidecar_noise_recovers_per_epoch_sidecar at recovery.rs:2203). Finding [no finding]: this closes the sidecar-damage residual I named in 2e67329d. The fixture is the same B/O/S/O/S topology as 4693ad9a (full epoch in tape file 1, D=k+1 partial in file 3) with the same damaged_ordinals span 18..24, plus parity damage on *both* sidecars: first_sidecar parity stripe(full_tail.stripe_index)=4 Parity{1} (recovery.rs:2274-2280), and partial_sidecar parity stripe(partial_head.stripe_index)=0 Parity{2} (recovery.rs:2281-2287). Two unusual fixture-strength assertions verify the sidecar damage is correctly placed: (a) recovery.rs:2288-2292 asserts first_sidecar_noise_lba sits *inside the boundary sidecar gap* (full_tail_lba < lba < partial_head_lba) — defending against a regression where the sidecar damage accidentally fell into the object region or beyond; (b) recovery.rs:2293-2296 asserts partial_sidecar_noise_lba sits after partial_tail_lba — the second sidecar is after the partial object data. Per-(epoch, stripe) damage breakdown: (e0, s3): 1 loss; (e0, s4): 2 losses (data ord 19 + sidecar parity 1); (e1, s0): 2 losses (data ord 20 + sidecar parity 2); (e1, s1..4): 1 loss each — all ≤ m=3, so every recovery succeeds. The expected_lost computation at recovery.rs:2351-2365 combines per-(epoch,stripe) data positions plus sidecar parity positions if the sidecar damage's (epoch, stripe) matches the failed ordinal's, and the lost_shards equality assertion at recovery.rs:2388 catches mis-attribution. Most importantly, the tracer flags saw_full_sidecar_noise / saw_partial_sidecar_noise at recovery.rs:2323-2324, 2366-2371, 2396-2403 *require* that at least one recovery's expected_lost contains each sidecar-parity position — preventing a silent regression where the sidecar noise was placed in stripes none of the damaged ordinals' recoveries actually visited. This compounds three orthogonal invariants in one test: object-data cross-object damage (from 4693ad9a), sidecar-parity damage isolated per epoch (from b57d825a / bd854e40), and the physical placement of damaged sidecar bytes inside the boundary gap. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 316 passed, +1 from 315; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: only single-parity-shard damage per sidecar is tested; damaging *multiple* parity shards in one sidecar at the boundary (e.g. m=3 parity shards in first_sidecar all damaged) is not exercised here, though 8f1a4937 covers the same-stripe full-parity-overload case in a non-boundary fixture. Damaging the sidecar *header* or *index* blocks (rather than parity body blocks) is not part of this test; the sidecar-header damage path is the §8.1 'damaged sidecar header → FilemarkMapDigestMismatch' invariant covered separately by the scan-reconstruct tests. A multi-epoch fixture where the *middle* sidecar (not adjacent to a final-partial) takes damage during cross-object recovery is also not separately tested.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 94b1287d (boundary sidecar parity-overload gate: full_then_partial_boundary_all_sidecar_parity_shards_stay_epoch_local_overload at recovery.rs:2411). Finding [no finding]: this closes the boundary multi-parity-shard residual I named in 930fbeb9. The fixture uses the same B/O/S/O/S topology and boundary-pinned ordinals (full_tail=19, partial_head=20) as 4693ad9a and a96118b2, but escalates a96118b2's single-parity-per-sidecar noise to *all m=3 parity shards* in the failed stripe of each epoch's sidecar (via the loop at recovery.rs:2489-2516). The failed-stripe selection is symbolic and rigorous: full_tail_address asserts stripe_index == S-1 = 4 and position == Data{k-1} (recovery.rs:2470-2479), partial_head_address asserts stripe_index == 0 and position == Data{0} (recovery.rs:2480-2485). The placement invariants pin: first-sidecar parity LBAs inside (full_tail_lba, partial_head_lba) at recovery.rs:2497-2501 — sharper than a single sidecar-LBA check because it iterates *all m=3 parity shards* and each one must be in the gap; partial-sidecar parity LBAs after partial_tail_lba at recovery.rs:2511-2514. LBA count formula at recovery.rs:2517-2520: 2 (object) + 2m (parity in both sidecars) = 8 expected; uniqueness dedup follows. The two-case recovery iteration at recovery.rs:2530-2575 verifies both boundary ordinals trigger Unrecoverable with stripe == failed-stripe (the *full StripeAddress*, not just stripe_index, pinned by ordinal_to_stripe at recovery.rs:2531-2532). The lost_count==m+1 assertion at recovery.rs:2564-2568 carries the explicit invariant message: 'adjacent epoch sidecar damage must not inflate the failed stripe loss count' — proving the m+1=4 count for the failed stripe is not inflated by the 1+3=4 erasures present in the *other* epoch's sidecar. Three orthogonal invariants compound in this single test: cross-object-boundary placement (from 4693ad9a), full m-parity-shard overload (from 8f1a4937), and cross-epoch isolation (from bd854e40). Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 317 passed, +1 from 316; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: noise is symmetric (both sidecars damaged identically at the failed stripe); a variant where only *one* sidecar takes full parity overload and the other side has no damage is structurally identical to 8f1a4937 + cross-object plumbing and unlikely to surface new bugs. A boundary parity-overload where the partial epoch's failed stripe is *not* stripe 0 (e.g. partial epoch has D=k+2 real shards, putting damage in stripe 1) is also untested; the partial-head ordinal's stripe is dictated by D-related geometry.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 15af22fb (nonzero partial-stripe boundary parity-overload gate: refactored helper assert_full_then_partial_boundary_sidecar_parity_overload at recovery.rs:2432, new test full_then_partial_boundary_sidecar_parity_overload_handles_nonzero_partial_stripe at recovery.rs:2422 with D=k+2=6 and partial_failed_offset=1). The helper at recovery.rs:2432-2623 parameterizes the entire fixture+assertion flow by (final_partial_real_shards, partial_failed_offset, case_name), and the existing 94b1287d test is now a thin wrapper at recovery.rs:2411-2419 with the same D=k+1, offset=0 parameters as before — preserving its semantics while enabling the new geometry. The new test asserts the partial epoch's failed shard is at stripe 1 Data{0} (recovery.rs:2524-2532) rather than the partial-head stripe 0 — a deliberately different stripe to verify the §10.1 epoch-local accounting is stripe-symmetric within an epoch, not stripe-0-specific. Helper invariants are case-name-prefixed throughout (recovery.rs:2443, 2508, 2527, 2609, 2614) so a regression in either case reports which case failed. The new test's partial_failed_lba sits inside the partial object range (recovery.rs:2506-2509: partial_head_lba ≤ failed_lba ≤ partial_tail_lba) — a new invariant pinning that the failed shard is not in the partial object's header byte but in its body. Per-(epoch, stripe) damage breakdown for the new case: (e0, s4): 1 data + 3 parity = 4 = m+1 (overload); (e1, s1): 1 data + 3 parity = 4 = m+1 (overload). Both failed ordinals trigger Unrecoverable with stripe == expected_stripe and lost_count = m+1. Combined with the original 94b1287d test (stripe 0), this parameterization now spans {partial-head stripe 0, nonzero stripe 1} for the partial epoch. The helper structure makes adding further stripe variants (e.g. offset 2, 3, S-1=4) a one-line task. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 318 passed, +1 from 317; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the helper's partial_failed_offset < S constraint (recovery.rs:2442-2444) limits exercised offsets to {0..S-1} = {0..4} — the data row 0 only; failed shards at non-zero data rows (e.g. partial_failed_offset=5 → ord_in_epoch=5 → row 1 of stripe 0) would require D ≥ k+5 and are not exercised. The full_tail failed stripe is always S-1=4 by fixture construction; varying it would require a different boundary-data target (not just changing offsets). Both these are increasingly synthetic — the §10.1 invariant is independent of these by construction.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 8af69e1b (§7.5.1 early-warning write gate: sidecar_only_early_warning_on_object_and_sidecar_writes_does_not_abort at sink.rs:2274 and bootstrap_early_warning_finishes_bootstrap_tape_files at sink.rs:2316). Finding [no finding]: this is the first sink/writer-side test entry in the recent stream (recovery-matrix coverage was largely saturated in the previous entries). The two tests directly pin the *EW asserted* column of the §7.5.1 normative table (design L2442-2483): EW must be propagated to the public filemark_outcome but must NOT abort the session — only EOM aborts. Test 1 (sink.rs:2274) constructs an EarlyWarningRawTapeSink that asserts EW on blocks {1, 13} and filemarks {1, 2}, drives a 12-block object plus its trailing filemark plus the sidecar (whose first body block is block 13) plus the sidecar's trailing filemark, and then sink.finish() to write the final bootstrap. The assertions pin: object.filemark_outcome.early_warning == true (EW propagated through finish_object — design L2465-2467), object.filemark_outcome.end_of_medium == false (no abort signal), sidecars_emitted[0].filemark_outcome.early_warning == true (EW propagated through the sidecar tape file's trailing filemark — design L2469-2474), highest_protected_ordinal == 12 (watermark *still advances* despite EW, proving 'data-committed' semantics from §7.2.1), and crucially raw.block_count > 13 (sink.rs:2310-2311 — proving the final bootstrap was actually written after all the EW signals, not aborted). Test 2 (sink.rs:2316) is the bootstrap-EW path (design L2476-2483): EW on both bootstrap blocks and filemarks, write_bootstrap() + finish(), and the final bootstrap parses to a valid is_final_map digest with tape_file_count=2 — proving even the final bootstrap successfully writes when EW is asserted on every output. These are *normative* tests for the §7.5.1 EW column, pinning the design's specified policy and complementing the existing EOM tests (finish_object_rejects_eom_on_object_filemark_before_map_commit at sink.rs:2353). The shift from recovery-matrix coverage to writer-side phase tests is appropriate at this saturation point. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 320 passed, +2 from 318; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the §7.5.1 table also specifies behavior for the *reserve check pre-write* phase (design L2452-2454: EW or EOM both reject the object with CapacityReserveExceeded before any block is written) — that pre-write reserve path is not exercised by these tests. The 'EW asserted but reserve no longer covers pending sidecars + final bootstrap' branch (design L2456-2463) — where EW *should* abort because the residual reserve is too tight — is also unexercised; the tests use small_scheme with 12 blocks, well under any reasonable reserve threshold. EOM-side coverage of the same phases is exercised separately by sink.rs:2353. The 'rewrite whole object on next tape' path requires multi-tape fixtures and is not part of this gate.

subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 4c7967c4 (§7.5.1 object-data EOM hard-abort + pre-write reserve-check gates: sidecar_only_rejects_eom_on_object_data_before_filemark_or_map_commit at sink.rs:2375, begin_object_with_capacity_reserve_rejects_tape_shortfall_before_raw_write at sink.rs:3395, and production-side EOM gate at sink.rs:1650-1655). Finding [no finding]: this is the first entry in the recent stream that changes *production* writer behavior — and it does so to match the §7.5.1 normative table's previously-uncovered EOM-on-object-data column (design L2456-2463). The production check at sink.rs:1650-1655 fires immediately after the raw block-write returns EOM (and skips the check only during finishing_padding so finalize-side EOM remains observable via the unprotected-range path). On EOM, the sink sets self.poisoned = true and returns TapeIoError::CheckCondition(ScsiError::InvalidInput) with the message 'object data block write reached end of medium before trailing filemark'. The check fires *before* last_data_lba updates (sink.rs:1661), object.written_blocks++ (sink.rs:1664), record_data_block (sink.rs:1669), and the s*k neighborhood-fill / parity-emission logic (sink.rs:1690+). Subsequent finish_object/finish calls observe self.poisoned and return ParityError::Invariant('ParitySink poisoned after prior error') from the early guards at sink.rs:744, 845. Test 1 at sink.rs:2375 pins this exact contract: write_block returns InvalidInput, finish_object/finish return poisoned-invariant errors, and raw.events == [WriteBlock] (no filemark, no sidecar, no bootstrap follows the EOM — sink.rs:2451-2455). This closes the §7.5.1 EOM column's central row and is the matching abort path for the EW informational case from 8af69e1b. Test 2 at sink.rs:3395 pins the §7.5.1 pre-write reserve-check phase I named in 9c7478f0 residuals: begin_object_with_capacity_reserve(2, 16) on small_scheme returns CapacityReserveExceeded with cause=TapeCapacity, projected_object_blocks=2, remaining_blocks=Some(16), reserve_blocks=Some(15), and raw.events.is_empty() — proving the reserve check happens before any raw operation (design L2452-2454). The existing spool-side variant at sink.rs:3432 covers the ParitySpoolCapacity cause. The production change is appropriately bounded: it adds one EOM check before mutation, sets the poisoned flag, returns a body-facing error — no public API rename, no SCSI CDB change, no rem-tar/catalog/crash-restart change. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 322 passed, +2 from 320; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the EW-with-shrunk-reserve branch (design L2456-2463: 'EW asserted ... continue ONLY if the remaining reserve still covers... else abort session, mark tape dirty') — where EW *should* abort because the reserve no longer covers pending sidecars+bootstrap — is not directly exercised; it requires a fixture where post-EW projected-usage exceeds the reserve. The §7.5.1 'rewrite whole object on next tape' multi-tape path is not exercised. The poisoned-session interaction with mid-object EW (where EW preceded the EOM) is also not tested as a combined sequence.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 3f3e14fa (capacity gate for oversized object no-spanning: begin_object_rejects_object_larger_than_empty_tape_without_spanning at sink.rs:3432). Finding [no finding]: this test pins the §7.5 no-spanning invariant — Layer 3c never splits a single object across multiple tapes, so an object that's larger than a fresh empty tape's usable block count must be rejected outright before any object starts. The fixture uses empty_tape_usable_blocks=20 and projected_object_blocks=21 (one over), and asserts: (a) cause == TapeCapacity (sink.rs:3458), (b) reported projected_object_blocks == 21 (sink.rs:3459), (c) remaining_blocks == Some(20) (sink.rs:3460), (d) projected > remaining at sink.rs:3461 — the *defining* invariant of this case, (e) reserve_blocks > 0 (sink.rs:3462-3465) — the reserve still includes sidecar/filemark/bootstrap/margin even when the object itself is oversized, proving the reserve accounting is preserved, (f) spool fields == None (only tape-side shortfall), (g) sink.active_object_tape_file_number() == None (no partial state left), (h) raw.events.is_empty() (no raw operation occurred — the rejection is purely pre-write). This is a deliberately narrow sub-case of the existing 4c7967c4 begin_object_with_capacity_reserve_rejects_tape_shortfall_before_raw_write at sink.rs:3395, but with a distinct semantic message: not 'reserve too tight for this object' but 'object too big for any tape, period'. The assertion `projected_object_blocks > empty_tape_usable_blocks` at sink.rs:3461 is the canonical signal that no future spanning logic could rescue this object. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 323 passed, +1 from 322; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the test only exercises projected = empty_tape_usable + 1; the symmetric boundary projected == empty_tape_usable (which should also fail since reserve > 0 by construction) and projected >> empty_tape_usable (very large object) are not separately exercised, but the asserted invariant (projected > remaining_blocks) is monotone. The case where projected ≤ empty_tape_usable but reserve_blocks + projected > empty_tape_usable (genuinely-too-tight reserve, not oversized object) is covered by 4c7967c4's test. A mid-write spanning attempt is impossible by construction (the gate fires before begin_object even completes), but no test directly asserts that property — though the §7.5 no-spanning invariant is structural.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 1a173435 (§7.5.1 EW-then-EOM object-data abort gate: sidecar_only_prior_early_warning_does_not_mask_later_object_data_eom_abort at sink.rs:2459). Finding [no finding]: this closes the EW-before-EOM poisoned-session residual I named in e516b4f5. The fixture uses an EwThenEomObjectDataRawTapeSink that emits EW on block 1 (count==1) and EOM on block 2 (count==2). The test asserts: (a) first write_block returns ew_outcome with early_warning=true, end_of_medium=false (sink.rs:2506-2507) — EW does NOT poison; (b) active_object_blocks_written() == Some(1) (sink.rs:2508) — the EW-only block was committed and counted; (c) second write_block returns TapeIoError::CheckCondition(ScsiError::InvalidInput) with 'object data block write reached end of medium' (sink.rs:2510-2521) — the EOM path at sink.rs:1650-1655 fires regardless of prior EW; (d) finish_object and finish both return ParityError::Invariant('poisoned') (sink.rs:2523-2541) — session abort is sticky; (e) raw.events == [WriteBlock, WriteBlock] (sink.rs:2544-2551) — no filemark, sidecar, or bootstrap follows the EOM even when an EW preceded it. This pins three orthogonal invariants in one fixture: EW and EOM are handled with *different policies* at the same code point (EW propagates upstream via data_outcome.early_warning, EOM poisons), a prior EW does NOT mask the EOM gate, and the poisoned state correctly persists across finish_object and finish (the same poisoning machinery as in 4c7967c4's pure-EOM test). The EW asserts a positive contract (block committed, count incremented) that the existing sidecar_only_rejects_eom_on_object_data_before_filemark_or_map_commit at sink.rs:2375 did not test — only the EOM-on-first-block negative path. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 324 passed, +1 from 323; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the EW-then-EOM sequence is two consecutive blocks (count==1 and count==2); a *longer* EW-tail-then-EOM sequence (e.g. 10 EW blocks then EOM on block 11) where the sink has accumulated multiple committed-but-EW-flagged blocks before the EOM is not separately exercised, but the count == Some(1) → +1 increment invariant generalizes. The EW-then-EOM-on-the-trailing-*filemark* (rather than on a data block) is exercised by finish_object_rejects_eom_on_object_filemark_before_map_commit at sink.rs:2555, but no test combines mid-object EW with EOM-on-filemark in one sequence. EW-then-EOM during finishing_padding (where the EOM check is intentionally suppressed at sink.rs:1650) is also not exercised, though that path is covered by the finalize-side unprotected-range reporting tests.

summary: Review of codex entry dd852fdf (no-spanning empty-tape boundary variants: begin_object_rejects_empty_tape_boundary_variants_without_spanning at sink.rs:3577). Finding [no finding]: this closes the exact-empty and very-large capacity-boundary residuals I named in 34b17f3f. The table-driven test exercises two cases: (1) 'exact-body-fill' with projected_object_blocks == empty_tape_usable_blocks == 20 — the body alone exactly fills the tape, but reserve_blocks > 0 forces rejection at the §7.5 pre-write gate; (2) 'far-oversized' with projected_object_blocks == 1,000,000 — extreme overshoot still hits the same TapeCapacity rejection path. The shared invariant block at sink.rs:3613-3651 asserts for each case: CapacityReserveExceeded with cause == TapeCapacity, the reported projected matches the input, remaining_blocks == Some(empty), reserve_blocks > 0, and the canonical assertion `projected_object_blocks.checked_add(reserve_blocks) > empty_tape_usable_blocks` at sink.rs:3642-3648 — the *defining* invariant that the body-plus-reserve overflow of empty-tape capacity is what triggers rejection, not the absolute size of either component alone. The checked_add defends against u64-overflow in the 1,000,000 case. Per-case relationship assertions at sink.rs:3627-3637 (exactly_fills_empty_tape flag) pin which boundary semantically applies. Combined with 3f3e14fa's projected = empty+1 case and 4c7967c4's reserve-tightness case, the no-spanning capacity-reject matrix now spans {below-empty (reserve-tight), at-empty (body-fills), above-empty-by-1 (oversized-by-1), far-above-empty (extreme)} — saturating the §7.5 'object never spans tapes' invariant for the pre-write gate. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 08afd5b3 (§7.5.1 EW-tail-before-object-filemark-EOM abort gate: sidecar_only_prior_early_warning_does_not_mask_object_filemark_eom_abort at sink.rs:2555). Finding [no finding]: this closes the two residuals I named in bd7fd057 in a single test — both the longer EW-tail sequence (10 EW blocks instead of 1) and the EW-then-EOM-on-trailing-*filemark* (rather than on a data block). The fixture EwTailThenFilemarkEomRawTapeSink uses ew_through_block=10 and eom_on_filemark=1, driving a 12-block object where blocks 1..10 each emit EW (with end_of_medium=false), blocks 11-12 are clean, and the object's trailing filemark emits EOM. Assertions pin: (a) per-block outcome.early_warning == (seed <= 10) at sink.rs:2620 — verifies EW signal propagation through write_block for the long tail; (b) active_object_blocks_written increments to Some(seed) for every block at sink.rs:2622 — all 12 blocks committed despite the EW tail, proving EW does not poison or block accounting; (c) finish_object returns ParityError::Invariant('object trailing filemark reached end of medium') at sink.rs:2625-2636 — the existing filemark-EOM check at sink.rs:787-789 fires, distinct from the data-block EOM path at sink.rs:1650-1655; (d) finish returns poisoned-invariant at sink.rs:2638-2646 — abort state persists; (e) raw.ew_blocks_seen == [1..=10] at sink.rs:2649 — fixture self-verifies that EW was actually emitted on the right blocks; (f) raw.events == [12 × WriteBlock, 1 × WriteFilemark] at sink.rs:2650-2655 — no sidecar emission, no further filemarks, no bootstrap, no map commit follows the EOM. This compounds three orthogonal invariants: long EW tail correctness (10 blocks of EW signal propagation), EW does not mask the *filemark* EOM path (not just the data EOM path covered by 1a173435), and the poisoning + raw-event-cutoff machinery applies uniformly across both EOM-on-data and EOM-on-filemark abort paths. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 326 passed, +1 from 325; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). EW on the trailing filemark itself (with no EOM) — i.e. successful finish_object with filemark.early_warning=true — is exercised by 8af69e1b's sidecar_only_early_warning_on_object_and_sidecar_writes_does_not_abort. The combined EW-on-blocks-PLUS-EW-on-filemark (no EOM) sequence is implicit in 8af69e1b but not separately combined with the EW-on-data sequence used here.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry f262b6c9 (spool reserve boundary variants: begin_object_spool_reserve_boundary_variants_do_not_touch_raw_tape at sink.rs:3809). Finding [no finding]: this closes the spool-side capacity-boundary residual I named in e55b9776, bringing spool coverage to parity with the tape-side coverage from 3f3e14fa + dd852fdf. The table-driven test has three cases pinning the spool boundary mechanics: (1) 'exact-spool-fit' with remaining_spool_bytes == required_spool_bytes — *admitted* and returns tape_file_number=0; (2) 'one-byte-short' with remaining = required-1 — rejects with cause=ParitySpoolCapacity; (3) 'far-oversized-spool-need' with projected = 1000 * data_shards_per_epoch but only one epoch's spool available — same ParitySpoolCapacity rejection. The fixture deliberately holds remaining_tape_blocks at 1,000,000 (sink.rs:3854) so tape capacity never bites — every failure must come from the spool side. The shared failure-case assertion block at sink.rs:3877-3912 pins: cause == ParitySpoolCapacity (not TapeCapacity), tape fields remaining_blocks and reserve_blocks are None (only spool side has fields), remaining_spool_bytes == Some(input) and required_spool_bytes == Some(expected) — both are exposed, and `remaining_spool_bytes < expected_required_spool_bytes` at sink.rs:3904 is the canonical shortfall predicate. Notably, the spool side admits equality (case 1) while the tape side rejects equality (dd852fdf's exact-body-fill) — a deliberate asymmetry pinned at sink.rs:3868-3871 with the explicit message 'equality at the spool boundary must be accepted', which reflects the design: required_spool_bytes is the *full* requirement including overhead, whereas tape's reserve_blocks is added *on top of* projected body blocks. raw.events.is_empty() at sink.rs:3917 in all cases (including success) — proves reserve evaluation precedes raw tape I/O regardless of outcome. The expected_required_spool_bytes computation at sink.rs:3841-3842 derives sidecar tape file bytes from scheme.stripes_per_neighborhood × parity_blocks_per_stripe plus header/index/filemark — so a regression in production's spool accounting (e.g. forgetting filemark overhead) would loudly fail at this fixture computation. Combined with the tape-side coverage, the §7.5 no-spanning + reserve gate matrix is now fully exercised across both capacity axes. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 327 passed, +1 from 326; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: combined tape-and-spool shortfall (both axes failing simultaneously) is not exercised — production must report a deterministic primary cause but the test does not pin which. A very-small-block-size (e.g. block_size=1) spool computation is also not separately tested, but the test uses block_size=8 which doesn't divide cleanly into 4096-typical reserve sizes, providing some defense against integer-arithmetic bugs that assume large block sizes.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 2d7e4ed6 (combined tape-and-spool shortfall gate: begin_object_combined_tape_and_spool_shortfall_reports_tape_capacity_first at sink.rs:3990). Finding [no finding]: this closes the deterministic-primary-cause residual I named in d7560af9. The test pins the design's policy that when *both* tape and spool axes fail simultaneously, the reported cause is TapeCapacity (not ParitySpoolCapacity). The cleverness is in the *probe* construction at sink.rs:3998-4016: the test first computes baseline.required_tape_blocks and baseline.required_spool_bytes via evaluate() with u64::MAX tape capacity (sink.rs:3992-3994 — gives the *true* spool requirement free of tape contention), then constructs an input that's short on both axes (remaining = required - 1 for each, sink.rs:3998-4002), then runs a spool_probe with tape capacity raised to baseline.required (sink.rs:4004-4007) and asserts that the same input fails with cause=ParitySpoolCapacity in *that* scenario. This is the key fixture-strength move: it proves the spool axis is *genuinely* insufficient in the combined input, not coincidentally adequate. Without this probe, a test could pass trivially if the spool side happened not to fail at all. Then the real begin_object call at sink.rs:4018-4044 verifies: cause=TapeCapacity, only tape fields populated (remaining_blocks=Some(required-1), reserve_blocks=Some(baseline.reserve_after_object_blocks)), spool fields are None (sink.rs:4039-4040) — proving the production code does NOT report both causes simultaneously or fall through to spool when tape fails. active_object stays None, raw.events stays empty. This is a stronger test than a simple 'both axes short → reports something' check because it deterministically pins which axis wins, and validates the policy choice (tape-evaluated-first) with the spool_probe. Combined with 3f3e14fa/dd852fdf (tape-only) and f262b6c9 (spool-only), the §7.5 capacity gate matrix now has full coverage including the simultaneous-failure deterministic-cause case. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 329 passed, +1 from 328; library 214 passed; scsi 95 passed; integration suites 16/14/7 passed). Residual risk: the test pins TapeCapacity as the primary-cause winner; if a future design change reversed this (e.g. evaluate spool first), this test would loudly fail and require an explicit policy decision — desirable. The case where only one axis is short by a different amount (e.g. tape short by 100 blocks, spool short by 1 byte) is not separately exercised, but the primary-cause precedence rule is independent of the magnitude of shortfall.
